subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When does spermatogenesis begin and where does it occur?
back: Begins at puberty; occurs in the seminiferous tubules.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the size of a mature sperm and its fertilizable span?
back: Size 50-60 microns; fertilizable span 48-72 hours.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How long does spermatogenesis take, and where do sperm attain maturity vs motility?
back: Takes 70-75 days (~72 days). Sperm attain maturity in the proximal epididymis and motility in the distal epididymis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How long does sperm maturation take in the epididymis, and what is the total time to form a mature motile sperm?
back: Maturation takes 12-14 days; total time is about 90 days (~74-76 days spermatogenesis + ~14 days maturation).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What do germ cells develop from, and what determines the male vs female germ cell line?
back: Germ cells develop from the epiblast; the SRY gene determines sex - males form spermatogonia, females form oogonia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outline the cell sequence of spermatogenesis from spermatogonia to mature sperm.
back: Spermatogonia (46XY) -> primary spermatocyte (46XY, meiosis I) -> secondary spermatocyte (23X or 23Y, meiosis II) -> spermatids (23X/23Y) -> spermiogenesis (no mitosis/meiosis) -> mature sperm (23X or 23Y).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When does oogenesis begin, and what is the fertilizable span of the ovum?
back: Begins in intrauterine life; ovum is fertilizable for 12-24 hours.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the size of a mature (Graafian) follicle?

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does the number of ovarian follicles change across life stages?
back: Maximum 6-7 million at the 5th month of intrauterine life (20 weeks); falls to 1-2 million at birth; falls further to 4-5 lakh (400,000-500,000) at puberty. Non-ovulating follicles undergo apoptosis (atresia).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Approximately how many oocytes are ovulated in a lifetime, and how many undergo atresia each month?
back: About 500 are ovulated over a lifetime; about 1000 undergo atresia every month.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: At what stage is meiosis I arrested in the oocyte, and when does the arrest end?
back: Arrested in the diplotene stage of prophase; arrest ends at puberty with the LH surge.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: At what stage is meiosis II arrested, and when does it end?
back: Arrested in metaphase; arrest ends at fertilization.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is a follicle?
back: A primary oocyte surrounded by follicular (granulosa) cells of the ovary.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outline the oogenesis sequence from primordial germ cell to fertilization.
back: Primordial germ cell -> oogonia (46XX) -> primary oocyte (46XX, arrested in meiosis I) -> secondary oocyte/egg (23X, arrested in meiosis II) + first polar body -> ovulation -> fertilization -> female pronucleus + second polar body.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the time interval between the LH surge and ovulation, and between the LH peak and ovulation?
back: LH surge to ovulation: 32-36 hours (best estimate), general range 24-36 hours. LH peak to ovulation: 10-12 hours.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the time interval between the estrogen peak and the LH peak?
back: 12-24 hours.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the two names for the first half of the cycle, and which hormone dominates it?
back: Proliferative or follicular phase; estrogen is the main hormone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What initiates the ovarian cycle, and which three hormones do granulosa cells produce?
back: FSH initiates the ovarian cycle. Granulosa cells produce estrogen (E2), inhibin B, and AMH (from small antral/preantral follicles).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is estrogen's feedback effect on FSH and LH, and when does it reverse for LH?
back: Estrogen has negative feedback on both FSH and LH, except when estrogen is >=200 pg/mL for 48 hours, which triggers a positive LH surge instead.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What initiates and what maintains the LH surge, and what causes ovulation?
back: Initiated by estrogen (>=200 pg for 48 hrs); maintained by estrogen plus progesterone. Both LH and FSH surge just before ovulation, but ovulation itself is caused by the LH surge.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the two-cell pathway for estrogen synthesis (LH/theca vs FSH/granulosa, plus adipose tissue).
back: LH acts on theca cells to produce androgens; FSH-induced aromatase in granulosa cells converts these androgens to estradiol (E2). Peripherally, adipose tissue aromatase converts androgens to estrone (E1).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which tumor markers are raised in granulosa cell tumors?
back: Inhibin B and AMH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the second half of the cycle called, which hormone dominates, and for how long?
back: Secretory/luteal phase, dominated by progesterone, with a fixed duration of 14 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is progesterone's feedback effect on LH/FSH at low vs high concentration?
back: Low concentration: positive feedback. High concentration: negative feedback on LH and FSH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which cells secrete progesterone in the luteal phase?
back: Luteinized granulosa cells.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where are FSH and LH receptors located in females vs males?
back: FSH receptors: granulosa cells (female), Sertoli cells (male). LH receptors: granulosa cells and theca cells (female).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What triggers resumption of meiosis I, and how long before ovulation does this occur?
back: The LH surge, about 3 hours before ovulation; a small amount of progesterone is also secreted just before ovulation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which three hormones does the corpus luteum secrete?
back: Progesterone, estrogen, and inhibin A.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the stages of follicular development from primordial to Graafian follicle.
back: Primordial follicle (primary oocyte + single flat granulosa layer) -> primary follicle (single cuboidal granulosa layer) -> early pre-antral follicle (multiple granulosa layers) -> late pre-antral follicle (granulosa + theca cells) -> antral follicle (antral cavity present) -> Graafian follicle (18-20mm, secondary oocyte, corona radiata, cumulus oophorus).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the corona radiata and cumulus oophorus?
back: Corona radiata surrounds the oocyte; cumulus oophorus connects the oocyte to the surrounding granulosa cells - both are features of the mature Graafian follicle.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What maintains the corpus luteum in non-pregnant vs pregnant women, and what prevents luteolysis?
back: LH maintains it in non-pregnant women; hCG maintains it in pregnant women and also prevents luteolysis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the lifespan of the corpus luteum in a non-pregnant woman?
back: About 12-16 days (10-12 days per an alternate source).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is corpus luteum size/activity and progesterone at their maximum, and when are LH/FSH at their minimum?
back: All occur 8 days after ovulation, i.e., day 22 of a 28-day cycle.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When should ovulation tests be performed, and how is the day of ovulation calculated?
back: Tests done on day 22 (one week before expected menstruation). Ovulation occurs 14 days before the next expected menstruation (cycle length minus 14).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is Mittelschmerz syndrome?
back: Mid-cycle abdominal pain occurring at the time of ovulation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the pathology of primary (spasmodic) vs secondary (congestive) dysmenorrhea.
back: Primary: progesterone withdrawal causes vasoconstriction and PGF2-alpha release, causing pain with no pelvic pathology. Secondary: caused by pelvic pathology - endometriosis (most common), adenomyosis, fibroid, or pelvic inflammatory disease.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare typical patient presentation in primary vs secondary dysmenorrhea.
back: Primary: young female, pain since menarche except initial anovulatory cycles. Secondary: reproductive age >=30, pain only starting at some later point with no prior history.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare pain location and timing in primary vs secondary dysmenorrhea.
back: Primary: suprapubic/generalized, just before or at menstruation, relieved within 72 hours. Secondary: localized, begins well before menstruation and persists after it ends.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare disease progression and pelvic exam findings in primary vs secondary dysmenorrhea.
back: Primary: pain decreases over time with physical activity, marriage, or childbirth; pelvic exam normal. Secondary: pain progressively worsens; pelvic exam abnormal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the treatment approach for primary vs secondary dysmenorrhea?
back: Primary: NSAIDs or OCPs (make cycles anovulatory). Secondary: treat the underlying pelvic pathology.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does androgenic side-effect risk change with progestin generation, and which generations have the least/most anti-androgenic effect?
back: Androgenic side effects decrease as generation increases. Third-generation progestins (desogestrel, norgestimate, gestodene) have the least androgenic effects; the fourth-generation progestin (cyproterone acetate) is anti-androgenic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the step-up drug therapy for minimal-mild vs moderate-severe endometriosis/dysmenorrhea?
back: Minimal-mild: 1st line NSAIDs/OCPs, 2nd line progesterone (downregulates estrogen receptors), 3rd line continuous GnRH, then laparoscopic management if no relief. Moderate-severe: 1st line continuous GnRH, then laparoscopic management if no relief.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define hirsutism and its scoring system.
back: Male-pattern hair growth plus alopecia and acne; scored using the Ferriman-Gallwey score, significant at >=8 (>6 in Asian females).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the treatment ladder for hirsutism due to raised androgens (PCOS, CAH, androgen-secreting tumors)?
back: 1st line: OCP, often combined with spironolactone (antiandrogenic). Alternatives: flutamide, finasteride, ketoconazole, cyproterone acetate. Topical: eflornithine. Last resort: continuous GnRH (lowers LH and FSH).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Why is an OCP added when using spironolactone for hirsutism in a woman who might conceive?
back: Spironolactone can cause ambiguous genitalia in a male fetus if conception occurs, so an OCP is added for reliable contraceptive cover.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the features of virilization and the androgen level at which it occurs.
back: Clitoromegaly, breast atrophy, deepening of voice, increased muscle mass, and hirsutism; occurs with androgen levels >=200 (ng/dL).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which drug should not be used to treat hirsutism, and why?
back: Danazol - it actually causes hirsutism as a side effect.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common cause of hirsutism in young women, and of rapid-onset hirsutism?
back: PCOS is the most common overall cause; an androgen-secreting ovarian tumor is the most common cause of rapid-onset hirsutism.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How do testosterone levels guide the hirsutism workup?
back: Normal levels: idiopathic hirsutism. 70-200 ng/dL: PCOS or late-onset CAH. >=200 ng/dL: CAH or an androgen-secreting tumor of the ovary/adrenal, causing virilization.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is clomiphene used for, and what are its main side effects?
back: Used for ovulation induction if the HPO axis is intact (FSH normal). Most common side effect is hot flushes; second most common is ovarian cyst formation; multiple pregnancy occurs in 7-10%; visual disturbance requires immediate discontinuation; vaginal dryness is also common.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is raloxifene used for, and its side effects?
back: Osteoporosis treatment; side effects are hot flushes and vaginal dryness.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is tamoxifen used for, and its key risks?
back: Breast cancer treatment; causes hot flushes and vaginal dryness, and can lead to endometrial cancer. It is teratogenic - minimum gap before pregnancy is 2 months (ideal 3 months).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are ospemifene and ormeloxifene used for?
back: Ospemifene manages vaginal dryness; ormeloxifene (ormiloxifene) is the active component of the contraceptive pill Centchroman (Chhaya).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Name the synthetic GnRH analogues and their routes of administration.
back: Leuprolide and goserelin, given as subcutaneous injections or intranasal spray (orally inactive).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the effects and uses of pulsatile GnRH?
back: Increases estrogen, LH, and FSH; used for delayed puberty, Kallmann syndrome, and anovulation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the effects and uses of continuous GnRH?
back: Decreases estrogen, androgen, LH, and FSH; used for fibroid, endometriosis, precocious puberty, hirsutism, ER-positive breast cancer, and prostate cancer.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the first-line drug group for fibroids that reduces bleeding but not fibroid size?
back: Tranexamic acid, OCP, and progesterone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the second-line drug group for fibroids that reduces both bleeding and fibroid size, by lowering estrogen?
back: Letrozole (blocks aromatase), danazol (side effect: hirsutism), continuous GnRH analogues, and GnRH antagonists.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which progesterone-pathway drugs are used for fibroids?
back: SPRM ulipristal (also used as an emergency contraceptive) and the progesterone antagonist mifepristone (RU-486, also used for medical abortion).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Name the GnRH antagonists and their use.
back: Elagolix and cetrorelix, used similarly to continuous GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where is natural GnRH secreted from, and how?
back: The arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus, released in a pulsatile manner.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the three natural estrogens, and where does each predominate?
back: E1 (estrone): most common in menopausal women. E2 (estradiol): most common in reproductive age and pregnancy. E3 (estriol): most specific to pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common synthetic estrogen used in OCPs vs in HRT?
back: OCP: ethinyl estradiol (EE). HRT: historically conjugated equine estrogen (CEE), now 17-beta-estradiol.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank the common estrogens by potency, highest to lowest.
back: Ethinyl estradiol (EE) > conjugated equine estrogen (CEE) > estradiol (E2) > estrone (E1) > estriol (E3).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which progestin generation is ideal for PCOS patients, and what combination is most used in OCPs?
back: 3rd/4th generation progesterone is ideal for PCOS; ethinyl estradiol plus a 3rd-generation progestin is the most commonly used OCP combination.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is hMG, its source, composition, uses, and disadvantages?
back: Human menopausal gonadotropin - synthetic FSH and LH sourced from the urine of postmenopausal women, containing 75 IU of LH and FSH. Used for IVF and anovulation. Disadvantages: highest risk of OHSS and multifetal pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the drug of choice for anovulation in PCOS, anovulation in general, anovulation from pituitary ablation, and anovulation from Kallmann syndrome?
back: PCOS: letrozole > clomiphene citrate. General anovulation: clomiphene citrate. Pituitary ablation: hMG. Kallmann syndrome: pulsatile GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the normal parameters of a menstrual cycle (length, duration, volume, and cycle-to-cycle variation)?
back: Length 24-38 days (old range 21-35 days); duration of bleeding 4.5-8 days (old range 2-8 days); volume of blood loss 20-80 mL; cycle-to-cycle variation 2-20 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define AUB and give the mnemonic for its causes.
back: AUB is any deviation from normal menstrual cycle characteristics. Causes (PALM-COEIN): Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia, Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial cause, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation order for AUB?
back: 1st: urine pregnancy test (except postmenopausal/virgin females). 2nd: TVS (except in puberty menorrhagia, where anovulation is a more likely cause than coagulopathy). 3rd: endometrial biopsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is endometrial biopsy indicated in reproductive-age women with AUB?
back: If endometrial thickness is >=11mm, if the patient is on tamoxifen (endometrial cancer risk), or if bleeding persists despite medical treatment.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is endometrial biopsy indicated in perimenopausal and postmenopausal AUB?
back: Perimenopausal (India >40y, international >45y): always indicated regardless of USG findings. Postmenopausal: if endometrial thickness is >=4mm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What instruments are used for endometrial biopsy, and when in the cycle is it best performed?
back: Pipelle or Vabra aspirator (worldwide) or Karman cannula (India); best performed 2-3 days before menstruation (an alternate source specifies day 26-27 of the cycle).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the clinical uses/indications of endometrial biopsy?
back: Investigation of choice for endometrial hyperplasia/cancer, Lynch syndrome screening, genital TB diagnosis, anovulation workup in infertile couples, and evaluation in late menopause (prolonged estrogen exposure).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is Metropathia hemorrhagica (Schroeder's disease)?
back: Anovulatory bleeding in a perimenopausal woman, presenting as 2-3 months of amenorrhea followed by bleeding, caused by decreased progesterone in anovulatory cycles.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does the endometrial gland pattern differ between ovulatory and anovulatory cycles?
back: Ovulatory cycles show coiled glands (corkscrew/sawtooth appearance); anovulatory cycles show simple tubular glands.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the order of preference for endometrial sampling procedures when repeat/definitive sampling is needed?
back: Fractional curettage + hysteroscopy > dilatation and curettage + hysteroscopy > fractional curettage alone > hysteroscopy alone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the indications for fractional curettage plus hysteroscopy?
back: Endometrial biopsy showing hyperplasia with atypia, an insufficient sample, hyperplasia without atypia but continued bleeding despite treatment, or cervical stenosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the medical management sequence for AUB, and the next step if bleeding persists?
back: Tranexamic acid, then OCP, then progesterone; if bleeding persists, proceed to fractional curettage plus hysteroscopy under general anesthesia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the malignancy risk in simple endometrial hyperplasia, and its treatment?
back: About 1.1% risk of malignancy; treated with progesterone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the most common causes of postmenopausal bleeding overall, and in India specifically?
back: Overall: endometrial/cervical polyp (37%, most common), then endometrial atrophy/senile endometritis (30%, second most common). In India, cervical cancer is the most common cause.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is postmenopausal bleeding with endometrial hyperplasia with atypia managed?
back: Malignancy risk 8-30%. Next step is fractional curettage plus hysteroscopy; best definitive management is total abdominal/simple hysterectomy (Type 1).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is postmenopausal bleeding with endometrial hyperplasia without atypia managed?
back: Malignancy risk 1-3%. Best management is the Mirena IUCD; alternative is oral progesterone (continuous, or cyclical for 12-14 days).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In postmenopausal bleeding, what is the management pathway based on TVS endometrial thickness?
back: If >4mm: endometrial biopsy; if bleeding persists, fractional curettage plus hysteroscopy. If <4mm: tranexamic acid; if bleeding persists, fractional curettage plus hysteroscopy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define hypomenorrhea, menorrhagia, polymenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and metrorrhagia.
back: Hypomenorrhea: reduced flow, <2 days or <20 mL (seen in Asherman syndrome). Menorrhagia: heavy bleeding, >80 mL or >8 days (fibroid/adenomyosis). Polymenorrhea: short cycles. Oligomenorrhea: long cycles/<9 cycles per year. Metrorrhagia: irregular/intermenstrual bleeding (seen with polyps).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the histological features of the proliferative phase endometrium?
back: Simple tubular glands, telescoping of glands, and pseudo-stratification (nuclei at different levels).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the earliest histological sign of ovulation on endometrial biopsy, and when does it appear?
back: Subnuclear vacuoles, appearing on day 17-18 (early secretory phase).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the histological features of the late secretory phase?
back: Corkscrew/sawtooth glands with secretion driven by progesterone from the corpus luteum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In AUB in adolescent girls, what is the most likely cause, the appropriate investigations, and what is contraindicated?
back: Anovulation is more common than coagulopathy. Investigate with UPT, serum iron, and coagulation profile. Per-vaginal exam and endometrial biopsy are contraindicated. Drug of choice is OCPs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the average age of menopause worldwide vs in India?
back: 51 years worldwide; 47 years in India.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define premature menopause/POI and late menopause.
back: POI/premature menopause: menopause before age 40. Late menopause: not achieved by age >=55.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is menopause diagnosed clinically, and how is POI diagnosed by FSH?
back: Menopause: clinical diagnosis after >=12 months of amenorrhea. POI: FSH normal 1-10 IU, suggestive >=25 IU, diagnostic >=40 IU, tested on two occasions one month apart.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are hot flashes, their mechanism, and their timing pattern?
back: A vasomotor symptom due to decreased estrogen, from an imbalance of serotonin and norepinephrine; worse at night (night sweats); presents as intense heat followed by sweating; seen in peri- and postmenopausal women.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How are hot flashes managed, and which drugs are not used?
back: HRT for severe flashes; SSRI (fluoxetine) as an alternative; also phytoestrogens, clonidine, gabapentin, and tibolone. Clomifene, raloxifene, and tamoxifen are not used; the approved SERM is estrogen plus bazedoxifene.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the pathology of menopausal osteoporosis, and the order of most common fracture sites?
back: Decreased estrogen causes a quantitative bone defect; fractures occur, in order of frequency, at vertebrae > neck of femur > Colles' fracture (wrist).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation of choice for osteoporosis, and the main drug therapies?
back: DEXA scan (bone mineral density) is mandatory/investigation of choice. First-line bisphosphonate: alendronate (pamidronate also used). SERM option: raloxifene. Others: tibolone, teriparatide (PTH analogue), strontium, denosumab (RANK ligand inhibitor). Teriparatide and strontium act directly on osteoblasts.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does menopause affect coronary artery disease risk, and is HRT used for prevention?
back: Estrogen is cardioprotective (raises HDL, lowers LDL); CAD risk rises after menopause, but HRT is not given for CAD prevention - in women >=60, HRT slightly increases CAD risk, and combined E+P HRT increases it more than estrogen-only HRT.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What causes senile vaginitis/genitourinary syndrome of menopause, its symptoms, and its management?
back: Caused by decreased estrogen; symptoms are vaginal dryness, spotting, and dyspareunia. First-line: vaginal lubricants. Second-line: HRT (estrogen cream). The SERM used is ospemifene (not clomiphene, raloxifene, or tamoxifen).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What hormonal changes characterize menopause?
back: Decreased AMH, inhibin, and estradiol (E2); increased vaginal pH and FSH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the contraindications to HRT.
back: History of breast cancer, thrombosis, MI or stroke, active liver disease, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, severe uncontrolled hypertension, severe hypertriglyceridemia, diabetes with vasculopathy, migraine with aura, and age >=35 with smoking.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the only indication for HRT, and how does uterine status determine the regimen?
back: Severe hot flashes is the only indication. If the uterus is present, use combined E+P; if the uterus is absent, use estrogen only.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which estrogen is most commonly used in HRT, and is HRT a contraceptive?
back: 17-beta-estradiol; HRT is not a contraceptive agent.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the advantages and disadvantages of estrogen-only HRT (EHRT)?
back: Advantages: decreased hot flashes, decreased osteoporosis, decreased colorectal carcinoma. Disadvantages: increased endometrial cancer risk (in women with a uterus), increased thrombosis, possibly increased ovarian cancer.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the advantages and disadvantages of combined E+P HRT?
back: Advantages: decreased hot flashes, decreased osteoporosis, decreased colorectal carcinoma, decreased endometrial cancer. Disadvantages: increased thrombosis, possibly increased ovarian cancer, increased breast cancer.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How long is HRT given in postmenopausal women vs perimenopausal/Turner syndrome/POI patients?
back: Postmenopausal women: maximum 5 years. Perimenopausal/Turner syndrome/POI: continued until the normal age of menopause (~51 years).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Does HRT affect cervical cancer risk or CAD risk?
back: HRT does not increase cervical cancer risk, and it does not decrease CAD risk.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is done for HRT in a woman with a history of venous thromboembolism?
back: Estrogen is not given; the drug of choice instead is fluoxetine (an SSRI) for symptom control.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the normal angles of anteflexion and anteversion of the uterus?
back: Anteflexion (between uterus and cervix): about 120 degrees. Anteversion (between vagina and cervix): about 90 degrees.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which ligament maintains the anteverted uterine position, and which prevents retroversion?
back: Round ligament maintains anteversion. The uterosacral ligament (more so than the round ligament) prevents retroversion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which ligaments support the uterus, and which one does not?
back: Supporting ligaments: cardinal ligament (transverse cervical/Mackenrodt's), uterosacral ligament, and pubocervical ligament. The broad ligament does NOT support the uterus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which muscle mainly supports the uterus?
back: Levator ani muscle (pubococcygeus and iliococcygeus).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is hysteroscopy used for, and how do the two distension media types differ?
back: Used to visualize the uterine cavity from inside. Gas distension medium is diagnostic only; liquid distension medium allows both diagnosis and treatment.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which distension media are used with unipolar cautery, and what are the fluid-deficit stopping points?
back: Electrolyte-deficient media (glycine - most common/best, mannitol, sorbitol): procedure stopped at 1000mL fluid deficit. Electrolyte-rich media (normal saline, Ringer lactate): stopped at 2500mL fluid deficit.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does the cervix-to-corpus ratio change across life stages?
back: At birth 1:1; before puberty 2:1; at puberty 1:2; at reproductive age 1:3; after menopause 1:1 (both atrophy).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the uterus develop from embryologically, and what do the round and ovarian ligaments develop from?
back: The uterus develops from the Mullerian duct. The round ligament develops from the distal end of the gubernaculum; the ovarian ligament develops from the proximal end of the gubernaculum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which three structures are present at the fundus of the uterus?
back: Round ligament, fallopian Tube, and Ovarian ligament (mnemonic RTO).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What connects the ovary to the uterus, and the ovary to the pelvic side wall?
back: Ovarian ligament connects ovary to uterus. The infundibulopelvic (suspensory) ligament, which contains the ovarian vessels, connects the ovary to the pelvic side wall.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the blood and nerve supply of the uterus?
back: Blood supply: uterine artery (branch of the anterior division of the internal iliac artery) plus utero-ovarian anastomosis. Nerve supply: T10-L1.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the branches of the uterine artery in sequence.
back: Uterine artery -> arcuate artery -> radial artery -> basal artery and spiral artery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the stepwise devascularization sequence for PPH management (Tsirulnikov's triple ligation).
back: 1) Uterine artery, unilateral then bilateral. 2) Utero-ovarian anastomosis. 3) Anterior division of the internal iliac artery - the third ligation is also called Sampson's artery/artery of the round ligament.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the 'living ligature'?
back: The middle layer of the myometrium, which physiologically clamps blood vessels after delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the Canal of Nuck?
back: A fold of peritoneum accompanying the round ligament through the inguinal canal, which can predispose to inguinal hernia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the artery of the round ligament, and where does it arise from?
back: Sampson's artery, arising from the inferior epigastric artery and anastomosing with the uterine artery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the contents of the broad ligament.
back: Fallopian tube, round ligament, ovarian ligament, uterine artery, ovarian artery, epoophoron, paroophoron, and Gartner's duct.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the relationship between the uterine artery and the ureter (the 'water under the bridge' sign).
back: The uterine artery lies anterior to the ureter and crosses over it about 2cm lateral to the internal os - the most common site of ureteric injury, described as 'water under the bridge.'

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank hysterectomy approaches by risk of ureteric injury, from highest to lowest.
back: Modified radical/Wertheim's > radical > laparoscopic > total abdominal > vaginal hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What percentage of women have a retroverted/retroflexed uterus, and how is it usually managed?
back: About 20%; usually correctable by manual repositioning.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What causes a fixed retroverted uterus that cannot be manually corrected?
back: Endometriosis (most common cause), or pelvic inflammatory disease/genital TB.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the order of pedicle clamping in abdominal vs vaginal hysterectomy?
back: Abdominal hysterectomy: 1st round/tube/ovarian (RTO) pedicle, 2nd cardinal ligament, 3rd uterine artery, 4th uterosacral ligament. Vaginal hysterectomy reverses this order.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In vaginal hysterectomy, where is the clamp placed with vs without bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO)?
back: Without BSO: clamp at the ovarian ligament. With BSO: clamp at the infundibulopelvic ligament.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What structures does total abdominal hysterectomy (TAH) remove?
back: The uterus and the cervix.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the two parts of the cervix, and their location/color?
back: Endocervix: supravaginal, red. Exocervix (portio vaginalis): pink.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What epithelium lines the endocervix vs the exocervix?
back: Endocervix: columnar epithelium. Exocervix: stratified squamous epithelium.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the transformation zone, and why is it clinically important?
back: The junction of the endocervix and exocervix (squamocolumnar junction), located at the external os; it is the most common site for pre-invasive and invasive cervical lesions.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What conditions cause the transformation zone to move outward?
back: Puberty, OCP use, and pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is cervical ectropion, and key clinical points about it?
back: Endocervix seen extending outward over the exocervix; physiological in pregnancy; biopsy should not be taken from it; it can cause post-coital bleeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the blood and nerve supply of the cervix?
back: Blood supply: descending cervical artery (a branch of the uterine artery). Nerve supply: S2-S4.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the lymphatic drainage of the cervix, and its sentinel node?
back: Hypogastric/internal iliac, obturator, paracervical, and external iliac lymph nodes (mnemonic HOPE); the cervix does not drain to the superficial inguinal nodes. The sentinel node is the paracervical node.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is Duhrssen's incision, and where is it made?
back: An incision made for an entrapped head in breech delivery, placed above or below the 3 o'clock and 9 o'clock positions (avoiding the arteries located there).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the normal cervical length in non-pregnant vs pregnant women, and what defines a short cervix in pregnancy?
back: Non-pregnant: 2.5cm. Pregnant: 4cm. A short cervix in pregnancy is <2.5cm, raising the risk of second-trimester abortion and preterm labor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When and in what gestational window does ACOG recommend TVS cervical length screening?
back: In women with a history of second-trimester abortion or preterm labor, measured between 16-24 weeks of gestation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What causes pain in the first vs second stage of labor, and their spinal levels?
back: First stage: uterine contraction, level T10-L1. Second stage: cervical dilation, level S2-S4.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What spinal level does epidural analgesia block, and what is it used for?
back: T10; used for painless labor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What spinal level is required for LSCS (cesarean section) anesthesia?

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is a pudendal block used for, and where is it administered?
back: Used for instrumental delivery; given at the ischial spine, piercing the sacrospinous ligament.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is a paracervical block used for, and which positions must be avoided?
back: Used for suction and evacuation procedures; never given at the 3 o'clock or 9 o'clock positions, due to vessels located there.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How are sponge-holding forceps, Allis forceps, and Kocher's forceps distinguished?
back: Sponge-holding forceps hold the cervix of a pregnant woman. Allis forceps have no curve. Kocher's forceps have no gap between the blades.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the features and use of the vulsellum forceps?
back: Used to hold the cervical lip in a non-pregnant woman; has a ratchet lock, curved blades with a gap, and rat-toothed tips.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the features and use of a uterine sound?
back: Has calibrations and an olive tip; used to assess uterine size and position.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does a bladder sound differ from a uterine sound?
back: A bladder sound has no calibrations.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What epithelium lines the vagina?
back: Stratified non-keratinized squamous epithelium.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What bacteria inhabit the vagina, their function, and when do they appear/disappear?
back: Lactobacilli (Doderlein bacilli), which convert glycogen into lactic acid; they appear at puberty and disappear at menopause.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the vagina develop from embryologically, and does it have glands?
back: Upper part from the Mullerian duct; lower part from the urogenital sinus. The vagina has no glands.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe De Lancey's Level 1 support of the vagina.
back: Formed by the uterosacral and cardinal ligaments; supports the upper third of the vagina. Defects cause apical/vault prolapse, cervical elongation, or enterocele.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe De Lancey's Level 2 support of the vagina.
back: Formed by the arcus tendineus fascia; supports the middle third of the vagina. Defects cause cystocele (most common) and rectocele.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe De Lancey's Level 3 support of the vagina.
back: Formed by the perineal body and its attached muscles; supports the lower third of the vagina. Defects cause urethrocele, rectocele, and a lax perineum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What surgical repairs correct cystocele/urethrocele vs rectocele/lax perineum, and what is the standard procedure for enterocele?
back: Anterior colporrhaphy for cystocele/urethrocele; posterior colpoperineorrhaphy for rectocele/lax perineum (together termed pelvic floor repair). McCall culdoplasty is the most common procedure for enterocele/vault prolapse (Moschowitz and Halban's repairs are outdated). Management does not depend on age or parity.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does vaginal pH change across life stages?
back: Acidic at birth; alkaline (6-8) during childhood; shifts from alkaline to acidic at puberty; 4-4.5 in reproductive age; <4 during pregnancy; 6-8 during menstruation and after menopause.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which vaginal cells predominate under estrogen, under progesterone, and with no hormonal predominance?
back: Superficial cells predominate under estrogen; intermediate cells under progesterone; parabasal cells with no hormone predominance.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What causes the vagina's normal acidic pH, and when is vaginal defense lost after birth?
back: Estrogen (provides glycogen for conversion to lactic acid) and Doderlein bacilli maintain acidity; vaginal defense is lost 10 days after birth.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What infections occur when vaginal pH rises above 4.5, and which organism tolerates acidic pH?
back: Bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis occur above pH 4.5. Candida tolerates acidic pH (<=4.5).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the cytological features of superficial, intermediate, and parabasal vaginal cells, and when each predominates.
back: Superficial cells: eosinophilic/pink with pyknotic nuclei, predominate in the proliferative phase. Intermediate cells: blue with a hazy outline and big nuclei, predominate in the secretory phase and pregnancy. Parabasal cells: blue with small nuclei, predominate at menopause, before puberty, and immediately postpartum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the length of the fallopian tube, and what epithelium lines it?
back: 10-12 cm; lined by ciliated columnar epithelium.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the parts of the fallopian tube from medial to lateral.
back: Interstitium -> isthmus -> ampulla -> infundibulum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What three cell types line the fallopian tube?
back: Secretory cells, ciliated cells, and resting (peg) cells.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the nerve supply and blood supply of the fallopian tube?
back: Nerve supply: T11, T12, L1. Blood supply: medial third from the uterine artery, lateral two-thirds from the ovarian artery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which part of the tube is narrowest, and which segments are the anatomical vs physiological sphincter?
back: Interstitium is the narrowest part and the anatomical sphincter; the isthmus is the second narrowest and the physiological sphincter.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where is tubal ligation performed, and why is that site chosen?
back: At the isthmus, because it is uniformly thick.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which part of the tube is widest, and where is the Essure device inserted?
back: The ampulla is widest; Essure is inserted at the interstitial/intramural part of the tube.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common site of fertilization, ectopic pregnancy, and genital TB within the tube?
back: The ampulla.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the eligibility conditions for tubal ligation in India?
back: Age 22-49 years, at least one child over 1 year old, and ever married. Spousal vasectomy status and spousal consent are not required.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How effective is tubal ligation, and is it reversible?
back: Failure rate <0.5%; it is a permanent method - not reversible, and not classified as a LARC (long-acting reversible contraceptive).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common method of laparoscopic tubal ligation, and its material?
back: Application of Fallope rings, made of silicone and barium sulphate.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When can laparoscopic tubal ligation be done, when is it avoided, and what is a relative contraindication?
back: Can be done as interval ligation, after 6 weeks postpartum; not used for immediate postpartum ligation. Relative contraindication: severe cardiac/pulmonary disease.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When and by what method is postpartum tubal ligation performed?
back: Between 24 hours and 1 week after delivery, via mini-laparotomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the Pomeroy and modified Pomeroy tubal ligation techniques.
back: Pomeroy technique: single ligature using chromic catgut. Modified Pomeroy: double ligature using plain catgut.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is Essure, its material, and its effectiveness?
back: A hysteroscopic tubal ligation device made of nickel and titanium; effective after 3 months (confirmed by HSG); success rate 99%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What determines sex differentiation at the genital ridge?
back: The SRY gene, located on the distal short arm of the Y chromosome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How do Sertoli and Leydig cells drive male internal duct development?
back: Sertoli cells (active during intrauterine life) secrete AMH (Mullerian-inhibiting substance) and inhibin B, causing Mullerian duct regression. Leydig cells (stimulated first by hCG, then LH) produce testosterone, which promotes Wolffian duct development into the seminal vesicle, epididymis, ejaculatory duct, and vas deferens.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is testosterone converted for male external genitalia formation, and what enzyme is responsible?
back: 5-alpha-reductase converts testosterone to DHT (dihydrotestosterone), which forms the male external genitalia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the Mullerian duct remnants that persist in males?
back: Prostatic utricle and appendix of testis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What happens by default (without Sertoli/Leydig cell activity) - the female developmental pathway?
back: Without AMH, the Mullerian duct develops into the fallopian tubes, uterus, cervix, and upper vagina. Without testosterone/DHT, female external genitalia forms. The Wolffian duct regresses, leaving remnants (epoophoron, paroophoron, Gartner's duct) in the broad ligament.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where do Leydig cells lie relative to the blood-testis barrier?
back: Outside the blood-testis barrier.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the referred pain pathway from the ovary?
back: To the medial thigh, via the cutaneous branch of the obturator nerve.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation of choice for an adnexal mass?
back: TVS (transvaginal sonography).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which gynecologic organs typically give rise to adenocarcinoma vs squamous cell carcinoma?
back: Adenocarcinoma: fallopian tube, uterus, endocervix. Squamous cell carcinoma: cervix, vagina, vulva.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the lymphatic drainage of the vulva and of the clitoris?
back: Vulva: sentinel node is the superficial inguinal node, draining next to the deep inguinal (femoral) nodes. Clitoris: drains to the lymph node of Cloquet/Rosenmuller.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What structure gives rise to the external genitalia?
back: The urogenital sinus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the developmental timeline of external genitalia from 8 to 14 weeks.
back: 8 weeks: Leydig cells begin producing testosterone. 9 weeks: Mullerian or Wolffian duct regression occurs (depending on sex). 12 weeks: fetal sex can be differentiated. 14 weeks: external genitalia are fully developed.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What testosterone/DHT levels produce male genitalia, female genitalia, or ambiguous genitalia?
back: Adequate testosterone with DHT conversion produces male genitalia; absent testosterone produces female genitalia; inadequate/intermediate testosterone produces ambiguous genitalia (clitoromegaly, labioscrotal folds).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the homologous structures between male and female external genitalia?
back: Genital tubercle becomes the penis (male) or clitoris (female); genital fold becomes the scrotum (male) or labia majora (female); genital swelling becomes the penile urethra (male) or labia minora (female).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where do Bartholin's gland ducts open, and what named criteria are used for its carcinoma?
back: Ducts open into the vestibule outside the hymen, at the junction of the anterior two-thirds and posterior one-third, at the 4 and 8 o'clock positions. Honan's criteria are used to diagnose Bartholin gland carcinoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What structures bound the vestibule, and what openings does it contain?
back: Bounded anteriorly by the clitoris, posteriorly by the fourchette, and laterally by the labia minora. Contains the openings of Skene's glands, the urethra, the vagina, and the Bartholin gland ducts.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is a Bartholin's cyst managed, based on size and symptoms?
back: <3cm and asymptomatic: expectant management. >=3cm and asymptomatic, or symptomatic/abscess: incision and drainage with Word catheter insertion. Recurrent cyst: marsupialization.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the primary pathology of PCOS?
back: Excessive androgen production by the ovaries.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are normal androgen levels in females, and typical levels in PCOS?
back: Normal is <70 ng/dL; in PCOS it is raised to 70-200 ng/dL (an alternate source narrows this to 70-120 ng/dL).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Why does raised androgen lead to anovulation and infertility in PCOS?
back: Excess androgen is folliculotoxic, preventing follicles from maturing to the Graafian stage, so no ovulation and no corpus luteum/progesterone form - leading to infertility, oligo/amenorrhea, and increased miscarriage risk.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What additional features commonly occur in PCOS?
back: Insulin resistance (with acanthosis nigricans) and obesity (the most common additional feature).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In obese PCOS patients, how is the E1:E2 ratio altered, and what is a clinical consequence?
back: E1:E2 becomes 2:1 (normal is 1:2) because adipose tissue converts androgens to E1; this predisposes to menometrorrhagia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is gonadotropin secretion abnormal in PCOS?
back: LH is raised while FSH stays normal; the normal LH:FSH ratio of 1:1 becomes 2:1 or 3:1 in PCOS. Testing is done on day 2-3 of the cycle.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is HAIR-AN syndrome?
back: Hyperandrogenism, Insulin Resistance, and Acanthosis Nigricans.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: State the Rotterdam criteria for diagnosing PCOS.
back: Any 2 of 3: (1) raised androgen - biochemical hyperandrogenism or clinical hirsutism; (2) ovulatory dysfunction - oligomenorrhea (<9 cycles/year) or amenorrhea; (3) polycystic ovaries on USG - >=12 follicles of 2-9mm size and/or ovarian volume >=10cc.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which USG features are notably NOT part of the Rotterdam criteria?
back: The 'necklace pattern' (follicles arranged around the ovarian periphery); the European/American society instead considers >=20 follicles diagnostic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the lab findings that are increased, decreased, and unchanged in PCOS.
back: Increased: testosterone, DHEA(S), LH, LDL, estrogen (in obese patients), AMH. Decreased: progesterone, SHBG. Unchanged: FSH, prolactin.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the result of the progesterone challenge test in PCOS?
back: Positive - withdrawal bleeding occurs because estrogen is present.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is PCOS distinguished from late-onset CAH when hirsutism/acanthosis is present?
back: Check 17-hydroxyprogesterone: <200 ng/dL rules out late-onset CAH; 200-800 ng/dL requires an ACTH stimulation test; >=800 ng/dL is diagnostic of late-onset CAH (rare).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare PCOS and OHSS on ultrasound.
back: PCOS: small follicles (2-9mm) with visible, hyperechoic stroma. OHSS: large follicles with no visible stroma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the first step in PCOS management, and the general treatment principle?
back: Lifestyle modification and weight management first; thereafter, treat whichever complaint the patient presents with.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is first-line therapy for anovulation in PCOS, and its precondition?
back: Letrozole is preferred over clomiphene citrate; both require an intact HPO axis (normal FSH).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is second-line anovulation therapy in PCOS with insulin resistance vs raised DHEA?
back: Insulin resistance: clomiphene citrate + metformin. Raised DHEA: clomiphene citrate + corticosteroid (cortisol).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How are OCPs used prior to clomiphene in PCOS, and what is hMG?
back: OCPs given for 2-3 cycles lower androgen levels before starting clomiphene citrate. hMG (human menopausal gonadotropin) is synthetic LH and FSH obtained from the urine of menopausal women.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the drugs of choice for irregular cycles, hirsutism, insulin resistance, and PCOS overall?
back: Irregular cycles: OCP. Hirsutism: OCP. Insulin resistance: metformin. PCOS overall: OCP.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which two drugs should be avoided in a PCOS patient who wants to conceive?
back: Spironolactone and OCPs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is third-line anovulation management in PCOS, and the older surgical option?
back: Third-line is pulsatile GnRH; the outdated surgical option is laparoscopic ovarian drilling.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the principle, advantage, and disadvantage of laparoscopic ovarian drilling?
back: It burns ovarian stroma to destroy theca cells and lower androgen. Advantage: no increased risk of twin pregnancy or OHSS. Disadvantage: may cause premature ovarian insufficiency (POI).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the long-term complications of PCOS from androgen excess, estrogen excess, and obesity.
back: Androgen excess: dyslipidemia (raised LDL, lowered HDL), coronary artery disease. Estrogen excess: endometrial hyperplasia, endometrial carcinoma, non-alcoholic steatohepatitis, possibly ovarian carcinoma. Obesity: anxiety/depression, sleep apnea syndrome, metabolic syndrome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which complication is notably NOT seen in PCOS, and why?
back: Osteoporosis - because estrogen levels in PCOS are never decreased.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which chromosomes and gene loci are associated with PCOS?
back: Chromosomes 2 and 9; loci THADA and DENND1A.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In managing PCOS-related infertility, what is the first step and the folic acid dose used?
back: Lifestyle modification first; folic acid 400 mcg daily (about 5-10% of patients ovulate with lifestyle change/folic acid alone).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: PCOS and androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS) both show raised LH with normal FSH - how are they told apart clinically?
back: PCOS presents with secondary amenorrhea; AIS presents with primary amenorrhea.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the three sources of androgen in females, and their approximate contribution?
back: Ovary (~25%), adrenal gland (~25%), and peripheral conversion from androstenedione (~50%).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where is the androgen receptor gene located?
back: On the long arm of the X chromosome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is OHSS, and which drug carries the highest risk vs which does not cause it?
back: An iatrogenic complication of ovulation induction; hMG carries the highest risk (more than clomiphene citrate); letrozole does not cause it.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What triggers OHSS, and what is the key inflammatory mediator involved?
back: Injection hCG (given to trigger ovulation, functioning like LH) triggers it; VEGF is released, increasing capillary permeability.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: At what follicle criteria is hCG given to trigger ovulation, and when should it be withheld?
back: Given when there are >=2 follicles each >=18mm. Withheld if follicle count >=20 and/or estradiol (E2) >3500 pg/mL (each follicle contributes ~200pg of E2). Note: Rapid Revision separately lists serum estradiol >2500 pg/mL as a general OHSS risk-factor threshold - the two figures likely reflect different decision points (risk factor vs contraindication to triggering), so both are recorded here rather than merged.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the USG findings in OHSS?
back: Large follicles, an increased number of follicles, and no visible ovarian stroma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How do you distinguish OHSS from a molar pregnancy presenting with theca lutein cysts?
back: OHSS: history of infertility treatment/IVF with hMG use. Molar pregnancy: positive UPT with 1st-/early 2nd-trimester bleeding and markedly raised beta-hCG.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the risk factors for OHSS.
back: PCOS, thin body habitus, pregnancy, young age, serum estradiol >2500 pg/mL, AMH >3.3, and >20 follicles each >10mm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is metformin indicated in the context of PCOS/OHSS?
back: For insulin resistance, and to reduce OHSS risk when hMG is used.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is OHSS prevented?
back: Monitor follicle number/size and estrogen levels; stop gonadotropins if needed; avoid triggering with hCG if estrogen is too high; give cabergoline (lowers VEGF); withhold hCG or replace it with a GnRH agonist trigger; in PCOS, consider frozen embryo transfer or metformin pretreatment.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Classify OHSS severity from mild to critical.
back: Mild: abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting. Moderate: ascites or ovarian enlargement on USG. Severe: clinically evident ascites and hypovolemia. Critical: end-organ damage (ARDS, DIC, acute renal failure).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How are early-onset and late-onset OHSS defined, and what causes each?
back: Early onset: within 9 days of the hCG trigger injection, due to the exogenous hCG. Late onset: at or after 9 days, due to endogenous hCG from an early pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What drugs reduce OHSS severity?
back: Bromocriptine (prevents VEGF release), IV albumin (volume expansion), and heparin (thromboprophylaxis).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How do PCOS and OHSS compare on follicle size and stroma appearance?
back: OHSS shows big follicles with reduced/absent stroma (seen after IVF/hMG use); PCOS shows small follicles with visible stroma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the underlying pathology of Asherman syndrome?
back: Intrauterine adhesions plus a thin, defective endometrium.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common cause of Asherman syndrome, and when is risk highest?
back: Excessive curettage is the most common cause; risk is highest in the postpartum period. Genital TB is another cause.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the most common symptoms of Asherman syndrome?
back: As a group: menstrual irregularities. Individually, in order: infertility (most common), secondary amenorrhea, then hypomenorrhea (scanty bleeding).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the first investigation and the investigation of choice for Asherman syndrome?
back: First investigation: HSG, showing a filling defect. Investigation of choice: hysteroscopy, which is both diagnostic and therapeutic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the HSG appearance of Asherman syndrome versus a polyp/fibroid.
back: Asherman syndrome: multiple filling defects with an irregular margin ('moth-eaten' appearance). Polyp/fibroid: a single filling defect with smooth, regular margins.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the hormone and challenge test findings in Asherman syndrome?
back: LH, FSH, and estrogen are normal. The progesterone challenge test is negative (no withdrawal bleeding), and the combined estrogen + progesterone challenge test is also negative - indicating an endometrial rather than hormonal problem.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of Asherman syndrome?
back: Hysteroscopic adhesiolysis, plus insertion of a pediatric Foley's catheter (to prevent re-adhesion), plus estrogen + progesterone for 3 months to rebuild the endometrium.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define secondary amenorrhea.
back: Absence of menstruation for 3 months (90 days) in a woman with previously normal cycles.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the causes of secondary amenorrhea, in order of frequency?
back: Pregnancy (most common), thyroid disorders, and raised prolactin levels.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation workup for secondary amenorrhea?
back: Urine pregnancy test, prolactin level, TSH level, and LH/FSH/estrogen levels.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What hypothalamic causes lead to secondary amenorrhea, and the typical MRI finding?
back: Anorexia, bulimia, excessive physical exertion, and stress; MRI is normal in these cases.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Summarize the secondary amenorrhea workup by FSH level.
back: FSH decreased (1-3 IU): hypothalamic (excessive exercise, bulimia, anorexia, stress) or pituitary (Sheehan's, prolactinoma) causes. FSH increased (>10 IU): ovarian cause (POI). FSH normal (4-10 IU): PCOS (progesterone challenge test positive, LH raised) or Asherman syndrome (progesterone and E+P challenge tests both negative, LH normal).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How do menstrual irregularities differ in hyperthyroidism versus hypothyroidism?
back: Hyperthyroidism causes amenorrhea/oligomenorrhea; hypothyroidism causes increased bleeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In secondary amenorrhea with low FSH, what does each MRI finding suggest?
back: Empty sella turcica suggests Sheehan's syndrome; a space-occupying mass suggests prolactinoma; a normal MRI suggests a hypothalamic cause.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What causes Sheehan's syndrome?
back: Necrosis of the anterior pituitary due to postpartum hemorrhage.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the most and second most common symptoms of Sheehan's syndrome?
back: Inability to breastfeed (most common), followed by persistent amenorrhea (second most common).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the hormonal sequence of loss in Sheehan's syndrome?
back: Growth hormone falls first; LH, FSH, and estrogen fall next; prolactin falls; TSH falls last.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the characteristic MRI finding in Sheehan's syndrome?
back: Empty sella turcica.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is a prolactinoma?
back: A prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the most and second most common symptoms of prolactinoma, and other features?
back: Secondary amenorrhea is most common, followed by infertility; other features include galactorrhea, headache, and visual disturbances.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the hormonal and MRI picture of prolactinoma?
back: LH, FSH, and estrogen are decreased; GnRH is decreased; prolactin is increased; MRI shows a space-occupying lesion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the drug of choice for hyperprolactinemia generally, and in infertile women wanting to conceive?
back: Cabergoline is the general drug of choice; bromocriptine is preferred in infertile women, and treatment is continued in pregnancy/lactation only if there is visual disturbance.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the normal, mildly elevated, and markedly elevated serum prolactin thresholds, and the next steps?
back: Normal up to 20-25 ng/mL; mild elevation <50 ng/mL warrants repeat testing; >50 ng/mL warrants MRI.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is pituitary apoplexy?
back: Acute infarction or hemorrhage of the pituitary gland in a patient with an undiagnosed pituitary adenoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the precipitating factors for pituitary apoplexy?
back: Postpartum hemorrhage, acute shock, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the clinical presentation of pituitary apoplexy?
back: History of postpartum shock; headache and visual disturbance (due to the adenoma); hypoglycemia (due to decreased cortisol).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What do investigations show, and what is the management of pituitary apoplexy?
back: All pituitary hormones are decreased. First-line treatment is corticosteroid supplementation, followed by thyroid hormone, sex steroids, vasopressin, and growth hormone; surgery is indicated for progressive visual loss, cranial nerve involvement, or loss of consciousness.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are normal AMH levels, and how do they change in POI versus PCOS?
back: Normal is 1-3 ng/mL; decreased in POI (<1 suggestive, <0.5 diagnostic); increased in PCOS (>3 ng/mL).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which cells secrete AMH?
back: Granulosa cells of small growing (preantral and antral) follicles.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the hormonal and clinical picture of POI?
back: Absent follicles with no ovulation, causing decreased estrogen and progesterone; symptoms include hot flushes and osteoporosis; LH and FSH are raised.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the fertility management option in POI, and an associated metabolic cause?
back: Donor egg plus IVF; galactosemia is an associated metabolic cause.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What chromosomal associations are seen with POI?
back: Turner mosaicism and Fragile X syndrome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the treatment for decreased libido in POI?
back: Testosterone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the LH, FSH, estrogen, and other hormone findings across AIS, PCOS, Turner's syndrome, Kallmann syndrome, Sheehan syndrome, and Asherman syndrome.
back: AIS: LH raised, FSH normal, androgens very high (male range) - differentiated from PCOS by karyotyping. PCOS: LH raised, FSH normal. Turner's syndrome: LH raised, FSH raised, estrogen decreased. Kallmann syndrome: LH decreased, FSH decreased, estrogen decreased, GnRH decreased. Sheehan syndrome: LH decreased, FSH decreased, estrogen decreased. Asherman syndrome: LH and FSH normal, estrogen normal (defect is uterine, not hormonal).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is a normal FSH level, and what defines a low FSH?
back: Normal FSH is 4-10 IU/L; low FSH is <4 IU/L (typically 1-3 IU/L).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the best time in the cycle to test LH and FSH?
back: Day 2-4 of the cycle.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does raised LH with normal FSH indicate, versus raised LH with raised FSH plus amenorrhea?
back: Raised LH with normal FSH is seen in PCOS and androgen insensitivity syndrome. Raised LH and raised FSH with primary or secondary amenorrhea indicates a gonadal disorder (ovarian or testicular).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What effect does prolactin have on GnRH, and the downstream hormonal consequence?
back: Negative feedback on GnRH, leading to decreased LH and FSH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common histological type of endometrial cancer, the most malignant subtypes, and the typical age and complaints?
back: Most common: adenocarcinoma. Most malignant: clear cell and papillary serous tumors. Typical age 50-70 years (mean 60). Most common complaint: irregular bleeding (AUB-M). Most specific complaint: postmenopausal bleeding. Most common route of spread: direct extension, then lymphatic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the risk factors for endometrial cancer.
back: Familial syndromes (Lynch syndrome, BRCA1/2, Cowden syndrome), hypertension, obesity, late menopause/early menarche, diabetes, atypical endometrial hyperplasia, unopposed estrogen (PCOS/HRT/granulosa cell tumor), nulliparity, and tamoxifen use.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the gatekeeper gene for endometrial cancer, and what causes Cowden syndrome?
back: PTEN gene; Cowden syndrome is caused by a PTEN gene mutation on chromosome 10.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare Type 1 and Type 2 endometrial cancer.
back: Type 1 (endometrioid, grade 1-2 adenocarcinoma): good prognosis, associated with excess estrogen. Type 2 (grade 3, papillary serous, clear cell): poor prognosis, associated with endometrial atrophy rather than excess estrogen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outline the FIGO staging of endometrial cancer.
back: Stage 1: confined to uterus (1A <50% myometrial invasion, 1B >=50%). Stage 2: cervical stromal involvement. Stage 3: 3A serosa/adnexa, 3B vagina/parametrium, 3C lymph nodes (C1 pelvic, C2 para-aortic). Stage 4: 4A regional (bladder/bowel), 4B distant metastasis or superficial inguinal node involvement.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which lymph node, if involved, upstages endometrial, ovarian, or cervical cancer directly to stage 4B?
back: The superficial inguinal lymph node.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outline surgical management of endometrial cancer by stage, and the rule for lymph node dissection.
back: Stage 1: TAH+BSO. Stage 2/3: Wertheim's hysterectomy. Stage 4: debulking surgery. Pelvic and para-aortic LND is done in all stages except stage 1A, where LND is skipped if the tumor is <2cm and pelvic-only LND is done if >=2cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What defines low-risk, high-risk, and intermediate-risk endometrial cancer, and their adjuvant treatment?
back: Low-risk (adenocarcinoma, grade 1-2, stage 1A): no postoperative therapy. High-risk (grade 3 adenocarcinoma, papillary serous/clear cell tumor, or stage 3/4): chemotherapy (cisplatin + paclitaxel) plus radiotherapy. Intermediate-risk (everything else): external beam radiotherapy (EBRT).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is CIN, and how is it diagnosed versus screened for?
back: A premalignant dysplastic lesion of the cervix, diagnosed by biopsy - not by Pap smear, which is only a cytologic screening test.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Grade CIN 1, 2, and 3 by depth of dysplasia and malignancy risk, and relate to LSIL/HSIL.
back: CIN 1 (LSIL): dysplasia in <1/3 of epithelial thickness, 1% malignancy risk. CIN 2 (HSIL): 1/3-2/3 thickness, 5% risk. CIN 3 (HSIL): >=2/3 thickness but not full-thickness, 15-30% risk.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How do carcinoma in-situ and invasive cervical cancer differ from CIN 3?
back: Carcinoma in-situ: the entire epithelium is dysplastic but the basement membrane is intact. Invasive cancer: the basement membrane is disrupted.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the typical age ranges for CIN, carcinoma in-situ, and invasive cervical cancer?
back: CIN: 21-30 years. Carcinoma in-situ: 30-35 years. Invasive cancer: bimodal, at 35-39 years and 60-65 years.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is CIN 1 managed versus CIN 2/3?
back: CIN 1: observe for 2 years (often regresses spontaneously); if unresolved, cryoablation. CIN 2/3: LEEP or LLETZ, regardless of age or parity. Hysterectomy is never done for CIN; laser is used if CIN extends onto the vagina.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare cryoablation and LEEP/LLETZ for treating CIN.
back: Cryoablation: OPD procedure, no anesthesia, uses cold CO2/N2O gas, no bleeding, destroys 5mm of epithelium, most common complication is persistent watery discharge; only usable when the entire lesion is visible on the exocervix and covers <75% of it. LEEP/LLETZ: OPD procedure, short, destroys up to 10mm of epithelium, minimal bleeding, preferred as it yields tissue for histology.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: HPV & Risk Factors for Cervical Cancer
front: List the risk factors for CIN/cervical cancer, and what is notably NOT a risk factor.
back: Risk factors: HPV (most important), early coitarche (<18y), early first pregnancy (<20y), multiple partners, multiparity, HIV, low socioeconomic status, smoking, and OCP use (linked especially to adenocarcinoma). NOT risk factors: early menarche/late menopause, familial inheritance, and HRT.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: HPV & Risk Factors for Cervical Cancer
front: Which HPV proteins are needed for viral replication versus malignant transformation?
back: Replication: E1 and E2. Malignant transformation: E6 (degrades p53) and E7 (degrades the retinoblastoma/RB gene).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: HPV & Risk Factors for Cervical Cancer
front: Which HPV subtypes are low-risk versus high-risk, and what does each cause?
back: Low-risk: HPV 6 and 11, causing genital warts (condyloma acuminata) and laryngeal papillomas. High-risk: HPV 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58, causing cervical, vulval, and vaginal cancer in females and penile, anal, and oral cancer in males. HPV 16 and 18 together cause about 70% of cervical cancers; HPV 16 is the most common cause of squamous cell cancer, HPV 18 of adenocarcinoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: HPV & Risk Factors for Cervical Cancer
front: What is Cervavac, and its dosing schedule?
back: India's first HPV vaccine, made by the Serum Institute of India, Pune; recommended for ages 9-26; 2 doses (0, 6 months) if <15 years old, 3 doses (0, 2, 6 months) if >=15 years old.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: HPV & Risk Factors for Cervical Cancer
front: Compare bivalent, quadrivalent, and nonavalent HPV vaccines by subtype coverage and protection.
back: Bivalent (Cervarix): HPV 16, 18; protects against CIN/cervical cancer. Quadrivalent (Gardasil/Cervavac): HPV 6, 11, 16, 18; protects against genital warts plus CIN/cervical cancer. Nonavalent (Gardasil-9): HPV 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52 (an alternate source adds 58); approved up to 45 years; protects against genital warts plus CIN/cervical, vulval, and vaginal cancer.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: HPV & Risk Factors for Cervical Cancer
front: What is the SAGE dosing recommendation for HPV vaccine by age and HIV status?
back: Under 21 years: 1 or 2 doses. 21 years or older: 2 doses. HIV-positive: 3 doses.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: HPV & Risk Factors for Cervical Cancer
front: What are the recommended age group, ideal age, and key rules for HPV vaccination?
back: Age group 9-26 years (high-risk women may extend to 27-45 years); ideal age 11-12 years; contraindicated in pregnancy. It can be given to boys, sexually active females, and HPV/HIV-positive females; HPV testing is not required beforehand; it does not treat existing CIN 1 and does not eliminate the need for continued screening.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: HPV & Risk Factors for Cervical Cancer
front: What cells does HPV typically infect, and what cytologic/histologic change does it cause?
back: HPV is epitheliotropic, typically infecting parabasal cells, and produces koilocytosis with a perinuclear halo on cytology/biopsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outline the ACOG cervical cancer screening protocol for resource-rich settings.
back: Begin at 21 years regardless of sexual activity onset; Pap smear alone every 3 years until age 30; from 30 years, co-test (Pap + HPV DNA) every 5 years; continue until 65 years; if any abnormal Pap in the prior 10 years, continue to 75 years; stop screening after hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outline the WHO cervical cancer screening protocol for resource-limited settings.
back: Begin at 30 years, stop at 50 years. 'See and treat': if HPV DNA or VIA is positive, treat with LLETZ without biopsy. 'See, triage and treat' (preferred): HPV DNA as primary screen, VIA as secondary triage, then treat if both positive. Preference ranking: HPV DNA + VIA > HPV DNA alone > VIA alone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In the WHO protocol, how often is screening repeated based on test results?
back: HPV DNA negative: repeat in 5-10 years (general population) or 3 years (HIV-positive). VIA negative: repeat in 3 years. HPV DNA positive but VIA negative: repeat HPV DNA in 1-2 years.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the WHO 2030 goals for cervical cancer elimination (90-70-90)?
back: 90% of girls vaccinated by age 15; 70% of women aged 30-50 screened; 90% of those testing positive receive treatment.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the conventional Pap smear technique - sampling sites, fixative, and contraindications.
back: Ayre's spatula samples the transformation zone near the external os (for SCC); cytobrush samples the endocervix (for adenocarcinoma). Fixative is 95% ethyl alcohol +/- 5% ether. No absolute contraindication; active bleeding is a relative contraindication; the slide should not be air-dried; best performed in the periovulatory phase.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is liquid-based cytology, and its advantages over conventional Pap smear?
back: Uses a cervical brush/broom; can be used during menstruation, gives a better pick-up rate, and the same sample can be used for HPV DNA testing.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is HPV DNA testing, when is it done, and what can/cannot it determine?
back: Begins at 30 years (can be done as early as 25); detects high-risk HPV but cannot identify the specific subtype (subtyping requires the Onclarity test, Cobas test, or partial genotyping).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define co-testing and reflex HPV testing.
back: Co-test: Pap smear plus HPV DNA done together. Reflex HPV testing: HPV DNA testing performed after an abnormal Pap smear.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank cervical cancer screening tests by specificity, sensitivity, and cost-effectiveness.
back: Most specific: Pap smear. Most sensitive: HPV DNA. Most cost-effective: VIA.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the VIA (visual inspection with acetic acid) test.
back: 3-5% acetic acid is applied to the cervix; normal epithelium stays pink (unstained); dysplastic areas turn white (acetowhite); metaplastic areas appear grey.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe colposcopy - magnification, what it can/cannot visualize, and technique.
back: Magnifies 30x; visualizes the exocervix and transformation zone but not the endocervix; 3-5% acetic acid is applied and white (acetowhite) areas are biopsied; a green filter highlights abnormal blood vessels (mosaic, punctate, reticular patterns) for biopsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is cone biopsy (conization) indicated, and how is it performed?
back: Indicated when adenocarcinoma-in-situ/endocervical cancer is suspected and not visualized by colposcopy; performed in the operating room under general anesthesia; if it confirms adenocarcinoma-in-situ, hysterectomy follows.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Summarize the next steps by Pap smear report: LSIL, ASC-H, ASCUS by age, and AGCUS.
back: LSIL (age >=25): colposcopy + biopsy. ASC-H (HSIL cannot be excluded): colposcopy + biopsy + endocervical curettage.
