ASCUS (<25 years): repeat Pap in 1-2 years; ASCUS (>=25 years): colposcopy or reflex HPV DNA testing, with colposcopy if positive. AGCUS: colposcopy + biopsy + ECC + endometrial biopsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the workup for a woman presenting with post-coital bleeding?
back: Per speculum exam first; if the cervix looks normal, do a Pap smear; if barrel-shaped/indurated/thick/abnormal, do colposcopy + biopsy; if a growth is visible, do a punch biopsy; if bleeding persists despite a normal cervix, do an endometrial biopsy to rule out endometrial cancer.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which infections can and cannot be reliably detected by Pap smear?
back: Pap smear can detect some infections but does NOT reliably detect chlamydia or gonorrhea.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most and second most common histological type of cervical cancer, and their typical sites of origin?
back: Most common: squamous cell carcinoma (arising at the transformation zone/external os). Second most common: adenocarcinoma (arising in the endocervix).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the most common and most specific symptoms of cervical cancer, and the most common cause of death?
back: Most common symptom: abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB-M). Most specific: post-coital bleeding. Most common cause of death: renal failure (from ureteric obstruction).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common route of spread of cervical cancer, and its typical age distribution?
back: Direct extension, then lymphatic spread (hematogenous spread is rare); bimodal age peaks at 35-39 years and 60-65 years.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does clinical/imaging staging assign cervical cancer stage for parametrial, pelvic side wall, ureteric/renal, bladder, and lymph node involvement?
back: Parametrial involvement: stage IIB. Pelvic side wall or ureteric/renal involvement (hydroureter/hydronephrosis): stage IIIB. Bladder involvement: stage IVA. Pelvic lymph nodes: stage IIIC1. Para-aortic nodes: stage IIIC2. Superficial inguinal nodes: stage IVB.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the investigations of choice for assessing lymph nodes, parametrium, and renal/ureteric involvement in cervical cancer?
back: Lymph nodes: PET-CT. Parametrium: MRI. Renal/ureteric involvement: CT.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outline the FIGO staging of cervical cancer by tumor size/depth for stages I-IV.
back: Stage I (confined to cervix): IA microscopic (IA1 <=3mm deep, IA2 4-5mm deep), IB macroscopic (IB1 <=2cm, IB2 <=4cm, IB3 >4cm). Stage II (upper vagina): IIA parametrium not involved (IIA1 <=4cm, IIA2 >4cm), IIB parametrium involved. Stage III (lower vagina/pelvic wall): IIIA no pelvic wall involvement, IIIB pelvic wall/hydroureter/hydronephrosis, IIIC lymph nodes (IIIC1 pelvic, IIIC2 para-aortic). Stage IV (metastatic): IVA regional (bladder/rectum), IVB distant (including superficial inguinal nodes).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which stages of cervical cancer are managed with surgery versus chemoradiation?
back: Surgery up to stage IIA with tumor <4cm (IB1, IB2, IIA1); chemoradiation for tumors >=4cm (IB3, IIA2) through stage IVA, and is the treatment of choice for stage IIB-IV.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the surgical management ladder for early-stage cervical cancer, comparing young/fertility-sparing versus older approaches?
back: Stage IA1: conization (young) or type 1/simple hysterectomy (older), no LND. Stage IA2: radical trachelectomy (young) or type 2/Wertheim's hysterectomy (older), with pelvic + para-aortic LND. Stage IB1/IB2: type 2/3 hysterectomy (young) or type 3/radical hysterectomy (older), with LND.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is radical trachelectomy, and its limitations?
back: Removal of the cervix plus 80% of the parametrium, with the uterus stitched to the vagina; contraindicated if tumor size >=2cm; subsequent pregnancies must deliver by cesarean section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the radiosensitizer of choice in cervical cancer chemoradiation, and a newer addition?
back: Cisplatin (more effective than 5-fluorouracil); pembrolizumab may be added, given once weekly.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe brachytherapy versus external beam radiotherapy (EBRT) in cervical cancer.
back: Brachytherapy places the source inside the body: intracavitary (tandem in the uterus, ovoid in the vagina) targets the tumor and paracervical nodes; interstitial therapy targets vaginal metastases. EBRT places the source outside the body, targeting the tumor and pelvic +/- para-aortic nodes, given as IMRT to a total dose of 45-50 Gy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3 hysterectomy.
back: Type 1 (simple/total abdominal): removes uterus and cervix, most common procedure. Type 2 (Wertheim's/modified radical): done for malignancy, carries the maximum risk of ureteric injury. Type 3 (radical): most extensive.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common cancer in pregnancy, in females overall, and in the female reproductive tract?
back: In pregnancy: cervical cancer. In females overall: breast cancer. In the female reproductive tract: cervical cancer.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Classify ovarian tumors by cell of origin (WHO classification) with the most common type in each category.
back: Epithelial tumors (90% of all ovarian tumors; most common subtype is serous; others - mucinous, Brenner, endometrioid, clear cell). Germ cell tumors (most common - teratoma/dermoid cyst). Sex cord-stromal tumors (estrogen-secreting - granulosa cell tumor; androgen-secreting - Sertoli/Leydig/hilus cell tumor, i.e. arrhenoblastoma; stromal - fibroma, thecoma, fibrothecoma). Metastatic tumors (Krukenberg tumor).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is ovarian cancer screening indicated, and what does it involve?
back: Not done universally; indicated with a family history of ovarian cancer (1st-degree relative) or a known BRCA1/2 mutation. Screening is annual TVS + CA-125; TAH+BSO is performed once childbearing is complete or the patient is over 40.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What TVS features on an adnexal mass suggest malignancy?
back: Solid component, bilaterality, papillary excrescences, thick septa, increased vascularity in septa, and ascites/lymphadenopathy/matted bowel loops.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: For an ovarian cyst with no malignant features on USG, how does management differ by age?
back: Postmenopausal: check CA-125; if >=35 IU, further evaluate and consider surgery. Reproductive age: based on cyst size; if >=7cm, operate due to high torsion risk.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the epidemiological features of epithelial ovarian tumors?
back: Most common ovarian tumor type overall; most common subtype is serous cystadenocarcinoma; typically occurs at >=60 years; usually unilateral; low-grade tumors show KRAS/PTEN mutations, high-grade tumors show p53 mutations; prognosis is poor since symptoms are non-specific and present late.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which ovarian tumor is associated with pseudomyxoma peritonei, and what are its other causes?
back: Mucinous ovarian tumor; other causes are appendiceal cancer (most common overall cause) and mucocele of the appendix.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is Meigs' syndrome, and pseudo-Meigs' syndrome?
back: Meigs' syndrome: ascites and right-sided pleural effusion associated with a granulosa cell tumor, Brenner tumor, thecoma, or fibroma (mnemonic: Government Banned Tobacco Forever). Pseudo-Meigs' syndrome: the same picture associated with any other condition (e.g., fibroids, mucinous ovarian tumors).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which ovarian tumors are most associated with endometriosis, and with endometrial cancer?
back: Endometriosis: clear cell carcinoma > endometrioid tumor. Endometrial cancer: endometrioid tumor > granulosa cell tumor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe Brenner's tumor - features, histology, and epithelium.
back: Epithelial, solid, unilateral, usually benign; histology shows Walthard cell nests with 'coffee bean' nuclei; lined by transitional epithelium.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the epidemiological features of germ cell tumors?
back: Most common in ages 10-30 years; usually unilateral (yolk sac tumor is always unilateral; 2-10% of dermoid cysts and 15-20% of dysgerminomas are bilateral); generally good prognosis due to early detection.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank germ cell tumors by prognosis (best to worst), and identify the only radiosensitive ovarian tumor.
back: Best prognosis: dysgerminoma - also the only radiosensitive ovarian tumor (otherwise ovarian cancers are radioresistant, despite the ovary being the most radiosensitive organ). Worst prognosis and most rapidly progressing (can present as acute abdomen): yolk sac tumor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Match ovarian tumors to their tumor markers.
back: Epithelial tumor: CA-125. Mucinous tumor: CA 19-9 (plus CEA). Dysgerminoma: mainly LDH (also hCG; never AFP). Yolk sac tumor: mainly AFP (also LDH; never hCG). Embryonal cancer: AFP + hCG. Choriocarcinoma: hCG. Granulosa cell tumor: inhibin B and AMH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the dermoid cyst (mature cystic teratoma): epidemiology, components, malignancy, and USG findings.
back: Most common ovarian tumor in reproductive age and pregnancy; usually unilateral (bilateral in ~10-15%); contains all 3 germ layers (ectodermal predominates); malignant transformation risk 0.2-2%, most often at the Rokitansky protuberance, usually to squamous cell carcinoma. USG shows the 'tip of the iceberg' sign, 'dot-and-dash' appearance, and the Rokitansky protuberance.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is a dermoid cyst managed, and when does torsion most often occur?
back: Cystectomy, due to the high risk of torsion (most common in the 2nd trimester of pregnancy); oophorectomy if childbearing is complete.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe dysgerminoma - gross and histological features, and laterality.
back: Solid, fleshy, lobulated, tan-colored tumor; usually unilateral but has the highest bilaterality rate among germ cell tumors (15-20%, up to ~30%); histology shows nests of cells separated by fibrous septa infiltrated by lymphocytes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the yolk sac (endodermal sinus) tumor.
back: The most malignant and most rapidly progressing germ cell tumor, can present as acute abdomen; always unilateral; worst prognosis among GCTs; histology shows Schiller-Duval bodies (resembling glomeruli).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the granulosa cell tumor - gene defect, hormone secreted, and presentation by age.
back: Associated with a FOXL2 gene mutation; estrogen-secreting, raising endometrial cancer risk (requires endometrial biopsy); presentation varies by age - precocious puberty/menorrhagia in children, AUB in reproductive/perimenopausal age, postmenopausal bleeding after menopause. Histology shows Call-Exner bodies.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the Krukenberg tumor - origin, spread, laterality, and histology.
back: A metastatic ovarian tumor, most commonly from gastric (pyloric end) cancer, also breast and colon cancer; spreads via retrograde lymphatics; bilateral in ~80% of cases; causes symmetrical ovarian enlargement with the ovarian shape retained, waxy and mobile, with an intact capsule; histology shows signet ring cells (mucin-filled, nucleus pushed to the periphery).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation of choice for an adnexal mass, and when is CA-125 not indicated?
back: TVS is the investigation of choice. CA-125 is not needed if USG shows no signs of malignancy, since it can also be raised in benign conditions (fibroid, PID, cervical TB); a level >=35 IU is considered significant and >200 IU suggests malignancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare clinical features of a benign versus malignant adnexal mass on pelvic exam.
back: Benign: painful, unilateral, cystic, tender, typically in reproductive age. Malignant: painless, bilateral, solid/firm, non-tender, typically at extremes of age.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is an asymptomatic adnexal mass managed by size in a reproductive-age woman?
back: <7cm: OCP and USG follow-up. >=7cm: surgery, due to increased torsion risk.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the ORADS scoring system for adnexal masses.
back: ORADS 1: follicular/corpus luteal cyst, no malignancy risk. ORADS 2: <1% malignancy risk (endometrioma, dermoid cyst, hemorrhagic cyst, hydrosalpinx, all <10cm). ORADS 3: 1-10% risk (any of the above >=10cm). ORADS 4/5: 10-50% risk (USG signs of malignancy present).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the characteristic USG appearance of a hemorrhagic cyst, chocolate cyst, hydrosalpinx, and dermoid cyst.
back: Hemorrhagic cyst: reticular/fish-net appearance. Chocolate cyst (endometrioma): homogeneous internal echoes. Hydrosalpinx: retort-shaped tube. Dermoid cyst: dot-and-dash/tip-of-the-iceberg appearance.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In a young reproductive-age/adolescent woman versus a menopausal woman, which ovarian tumor type is more likely?
back: Reproductive age/adolescent: germ cell tumor. Menopausal: epithelial cell tumor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the proven risk factors for ovarian cancer, grouped by mechanism.
back: Excess estrogen: early menarche, late menopause, obesity, endometriosis, PCOS, HRT. Excess ovulation (incessant ovulation theory): nulliparity, infertility, ovulation-inducing drugs. Genetic: Lynch syndrome, BRCA1, BRCA2. Carcinogen exposure: smoking (raises mucinous adenocarcinoma risk specifically, though protective for other subtypes).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the protective factors for ovarian cancer.
back: Related to reduced estrogen: physical exercise. Related to reduced ovulation (anovulation): multiparity, OCP use (most protective), breastfeeding. Certain surgeries: hysterectomy, tubal ligation, salpingectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does staging laparotomy for ovarian cancer involve?
back: Collection of ascitic fluid/peritoneal washings, TAH+BSO, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, multiple peritoneal biopsies, infracolic omentectomy (not just an omental biopsy), and appendicectomy (only for the mucinous variety).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outline management of early-stage (1-2), resectable ovarian cancer.
back: CT scan to confirm early stage, then staging laparotomy (TAH+BSO+LND), followed by adjuvant chemotherapy - carboplatin + paclitaxel for 6 cycles; dysgerminoma stage 1 may be managed with unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy alone, without chemotherapy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outline management of advanced-stage (3-4) resectable versus non-resectable ovarian cancer.
back: Resectable: debulking/cytoreductive surgery followed by adjuvant chemotherapy (carboplatin + paclitaxel, 6 cycles) and maintenance (PARP inhibitor + bevacizumab). Non-resectable (peritoneal caking or involvement of stomach/liver/great vessels/intestine/mesentery): neoadjuvant chemotherapy (carboplatin + paclitaxel, 3 cycles), then debulking surgery, then the remaining 3 cycles of chemotherapy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the maintenance therapy after chemotherapy for ovarian cancer, based on BRCA status?
back: BRCA-negative: olaparib + bevacizumab. BRCA-positive: olaparib (a PARP inhibitor) alone - bevacizumab is contraindicated if BRCA-positive.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is ovarian cancer relapse classified and treated based on the platinum-free interval?
back: Relapse within 6 months of stopping chemotherapy: platinum-resistant, treated with paclitaxel + bevacizumab. Relapse at >=6 months: platinum-sensitive, treated with carboplatin + paclitaxel. Rising CA-125 during treatment despite therapy: platinum-refractory, managed with pegylated liposomal doxorubicin (PLD). Note: partial platinum resistance does not exist as a category.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In familial ovarian cancer (BRCA/Lynch), what preventive strategy is used, and at what age does it typically occur?
back: Best: TAH+BSO once childbearing is complete or by age 40 (familial ovarian cancer occurs about a decade earlier, at 50-60 years, versus the general population). Second best (if surgery deferred): endometrial biopsy plus annual CA-125 monitoring - also used for surveillance in BRCA-negative women with a first-degree relative who died of ovarian cancer.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank familial associations of ovarian cancer by frequency.
back: BRCA1 > BRCA2 > Lynch syndrome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Match histological/gross findings to their associated ovarian tumor.
back: Tip-of-the-iceberg/dot-and-dash appearance/Rokitansky protuberance: dermoid cyst. Hobnail nucleus: clear cell carcinoma. Schiller-Duval body: yolk sac tumor. Call-Exner body: granulosa cell tumor. Signet ring cell: Krukenberg tumor. Nests of cells separated by fibrous septa: dysgerminoma. Reinke's crystal: hilus cell tumor. Walthard cell nests: Brenner's tumor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the lymphatic drainage pathway of the vulva, and the exception for direct drainage?
back: Superficial inguinal nodes drain to deep inguinal/femoral nodes; the exception is the glans of the clitoris, which drains directly to the deep inguinal/Cloquet's/Rosenmuller's node.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In vulval cancer lymph node dissection, are pelvic nodes removed, and which nodes drain bilaterally regardless of tumor laterality?
back: Pelvic lymph nodes are not dissected - only inguinofemoral nodes are. Structures located within 2cm of the midline drain bilaterally.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the risk factors for vulval cancer, and conditions that are NOT risk factors.
back: Risk factors: HPV (especially HPV 16), VIN/vulval atrophy or dystrophy, vulval warts (condyloma acuminata), smoking, and lichen sclerosus. NOT risk factors: condyloma lata, herpes simplex virus, and vulval hamartoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most and second most common histological type of vulval cancer, and typical age/site/symptom?
back: Most common: squamous cell carcinoma (keratinizing type more common than warty type). Second most common: melanoma (more common than adenocarcinoma). Typical age 50-70 years (mean 65); most common site is labia majora > labia minora; most common symptom is pruritus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outline the staging of vulval cancer by size/depth, and when lymph nodes are involved.
back: Stage IA: size <2cm and depth <1mm. Stage IB: size >=2cm or depth >=1mm. Lymph nodes are involved in stage III and IV.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the lymph node dissection strategy for vulval cancer?
back: No LND in stage IA. If nodes are palpable or positive, bilateral inguinofemoral LND is always done. If nodes are impalpable, LND is skipped only in stage IA; in all other stages bilateral inguinofemoral LND is done, except when tumor size is <2cm AND it is >2cm from the midline AND there is no local extension - then unilateral dissection may suffice.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Outline surgical management of vulval cancer by stage.
back: Stage IA: simple partial vulvectomy. Stage IB/II: radical vulvectomy. Stage III/IV: chemoradiation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the estrogen-dependent gynecological conditions.
back: Fibroid, endometriosis, endometrial cancer, and ovarian cancer.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the 'theory of incessant ovulation'?
back: More lifetime ovulations increase the risk of ovarian cancer.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is corpus cancer syndrome?
back: Increased risk of endometrial cancer in an obese woman with diabetes and hypertension, presenting with postmenopausal bleeding; a TVS endometrial thickness >=4mm warrants endometrial sampling.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which ovarian tumor is classically described as 'virilizing', and what is the most common cause of postmenopausal bleeding?
back: Arrhenoblastoma is the virilizing ovarian tumor; the most common cause of postmenopausal bleeding is an endometrial/cervical polyp.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the risk factors and protective factors for estrogen-dependent conditions overall.
back: Risk factors: nulliparity, obesity, early menarche, late menopause, PCOS, ovulation-inducing drugs. Protective factors: multiparity, pregnancy, physical exercise, smoking (inhibits aromatase), OCP use, breastfeeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which genes and minerals are needed for testis formation?
back: The SRY gene (more important than the SOX gene), located on the distal short arm of the Y chromosome; zinc is needed as a cofactor for SRY gene function.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What do Sertoli cells secrete, and what are the functions of each secretion?
back: AMH/Mullerian-inhibiting substance, causing Mullerian duct regression in males, and inhibin B, giving negative feedback on FSH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What do Leydig cells secrete, and how does it masculinize the fetus?
back: Testosterone, which promotes Wolffian duct growth into the seminal vesicles, epididymis, ejaculatory duct, and vas deferens; 5-alpha-reductase (present only in males) converts testosterone to DHT, which masculinizes the external genitalia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In the absence of Sertoli/Leydig cell activity (the default pathway), what develops?
back: The Mullerian duct persists and forms the fallopian tubes, uterus, cervix, and upper vagina; the Wolffian duct regresses and there is no masculinization of the external genitalia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the first stimulus for testosterone secretion, and how does the male HPG axis work thereafter?
back: hCG is the first stimulus; later, LH (from the anterior pituitary at puberty) stimulates Leydig cells, while FSH stimulates Sertoli cells, which secrete inhibin B to give negative feedback on FSH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When do gonads develop, and which develops first - testis or ovary?
back: Gonads develop from the genital ridge at 5 weeks of intrauterine life; the testis develops before the ovary.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the embryological origins of the upper versus lower vagina?
back: Upper part: Mullerian duct. Lower part: sinovaginal bulb of the urogenital sinus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the male Mullerian duct remnants, and the female Wolffian duct remnants?
back: Male Mullerian remnants: appendix of testis (hydatid of Morgagni) and prostatic utricle. Female Wolffian remnants: epoophoron (proximal mesonephric tubule) and paroophoron (distal mesonephric tubule), both in the broad ligament, plus Gartner's duct (mesonephric duct) in the anterolateral vaginal wall.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the homologous external genital structures between males and females?
back: Genital tubercle: penis (male)/clitoris (female). Labioscrotal swelling: scrotum (male)/labia majora (female). Genital/urethral fold: penile urethra (male)/labia minora (female).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation of choice for ambiguous genitalia?
back: Karyotyping.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common cause of female-appearing external genitalia in an XY karyotype?
back: Complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does testosterone/DHT level determine external genitalia appearance, regardless of karyotype?
back: Sufficient testosterone/DHT produces male external genitalia; insufficient/intermediate levels produce ambiguous genitalia; absent levels produce female external genitalia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is Barr body count calculated, and what are the normal counts in males and females?
back: Barr body count equals the number of X chromosomes minus 1; normal is 0 in males and 1 in females.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In which conditions is a false-positive versus false-negative Barr body result seen?
back: False-positive: Klinefelter syndrome. False-negative: Turner syndrome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define male and female pseudohermaphroditism, with examples.
back: Male pseudohermaphrodite: 46XY genotype with female external genitalia (e.g., complete AIS - most common, Swyer syndrome, 5-alpha-reductase deficiency). Female pseudohermaphrodite: 46XX genotype with ambiguous external genitalia (e.g., CAH).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In a child with ambiguous genitalia, how does gonad palpability guide the diagnostic workup?
back: Palpable gonads (undescended testes) suggest a 46XY karyotype, most commonly partial androgen insensitivity syndrome. Non-palpable gonads suggest a 46XX karyotype, most commonly congenital adrenal hyperplasia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the structures of the vulva/pudendum.
back: Mons pubis, clitoris, labia majora, labia minora, fourchette, posterior commissure, prepuce and frenulum of the clitoris, termination of the round ligament, Bartholin's gland, and the vestibule.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What structures open into the vestibule?
back: The urethral opening, paraurethral (Skene's) gland openings, the vaginal introitus (covered by the hymen), and the Bartholin gland duct openings.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When does pubic hair appear, and what Tanner stage and hormone are responsible?
back: Appears at pubarche, corresponding to Tanner stage 3, and is driven by androgen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What defines clitoromegaly, and what are the male-homologous structures of Skene's and Bartholin's glands?
back: Clitoromegaly is a clitoral length >=4cm. The prostate gland is homologous to Skene's gland; Cowper's gland is homologous to Bartholin's gland.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is imperforate hymen, and its complication?
back: A hymen with no opening; it causes cryptomenorrhea, as menstrual blood is trapped behind the hymen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where is the Bartholin's gland located, and is it normally palpable?
back: Between the labia majora and minora, at the 5 and 7 o'clock positions; it is not normally palpable.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the location of a Skene's (paraurethral) cyst, Gartner's cyst, and Bartholin's cyst.
back: Skene's cyst: adjacent to the urethra. Gartner's cyst: anterolateral wall of the vagina. Bartholin's cyst: between the labia majora and minora (gland), or in the vestibule outside the introitus (duct).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the presentation and management of a Bartholin's cyst.
back: Usually small and asymptomatic, but can cause vulval pain during activity; on exam it is unilateral, round/oval, and fluctuant. Management: <3cm - conservative; >=3cm asymptomatic, or symptomatic/abscess - incision and drainage with Word catheter insertion; recurrent cyst - marsupialization.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the incubation period and causative organism of genital herpes, syphilis, chancroid, donovanosis, and LGV.
back: Genital herpes: 2-7 days, HSV-2 (more than HSV-1). Syphilitic chancre: 10-90 days, Treponema pallidum. Chancroid (soft chancre): 1-14 days, Haemophilus ducreyi. Donovanosis: 1-12 weeks, Klebsiella granulomatis. LGV: 3 days to 6 weeks, Chlamydia trachomatis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the clinical appearance and lymphadenopathy of genital herpes, syphilis, chancroid, donovanosis, and LGV.
back: Genital herpes: clustered vesicles on an erythematous base, painful/pruritic nodes. Syphilis: single painless ulcer with a clear base and firm raised border, painless nodes. Chancroid: painful ulcer with undermined edges, painful nodes. Donovanosis: painless papule/nodule becoming a beefy-red granulomatous ulcer with rolled edges, 'pseudobuboes' rather than true lymphadenopathy. LGV: painless ulcer, but painful lymphadenopathy (buboes).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the diagnostic test and treatment for genital herpes, syphilis, chancroid, donovanosis, and LGV.
back: Genital herpes: Tzanck smear (multinucleated giant cells)/PCR/serology; treated with acyclovir/famciclovir. Syphilis: dark-field microscopy; treated with penicillin/ceftriaxone. Chancroid: culture; treated with azithromycin/ceftriaxone. Donovanosis: biopsy showing Donovan bodies; treated with azithromycin. LGV: NAAT; treated with doxycycline.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List estrogen's actions during puberty.
back: Breast development, reproductive tract (uterine) development, initiation of menstruation, pubertal growth spurt, vaginal cornification, cervical mucus production, epiphyseal closure, increased bone mass, and increased clotting factors (relevant to OCP contraindications with a history of thrombosis/CAD/CVA, or puerperium <21 days).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the average ages of puberty onset in females versus males, and the first sign in each?
back: Females: 10.5 years, first sign is the growth spurt (most specific early sign is thelarche/breast budding). Males: 11.5 years, first sign is testicular enlargement.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the sequence of pubertal events in females?
back: Growth spurt -> thelarche (breast budding, Tanner stage 2) -> pubarche (pubic/axillary hair, Tanner stage 3) -> peak height velocity -> menarche (typically within 2 years of thelarche, around 12.5 years, about 6 months after peak height velocity).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define precocious puberty in females and males, and the most common overall cause.
back: Females: thelarche before age 8 (more common in females than males). Males: testicular enlargement before age 9. Most common cause overall is central precocious puberty, usually idiopathic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What defines precocious menstruation?
back: Menarche occurring before age 10.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe central precocious puberty - mechanism, hormones, causes, and treatment.
back: Due to premature activation of the HPO axis; LH, FSH, and estrogen are all raised; most common cause is idiopathic (80%), second most common is a brain hamartoma (10%, investigated with MRI brain); it is always isosexual (breast development occurs) and can be associated with precocious menstruation; drug of choice is continuous GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe peripheral precocious puberty - mechanism, hormones, causes, and association with menstruation.
back: Due to an exogenous source of estrogen or androgen; LH and FSH are low while estrogen/androgen is raised; can be isosexual (estrogen-secreting ovarian tumor, hypothyroidism, McCune-Albright syndrome) or heterosexual (androgen-secreting tumor, CAH); it is NOT associated with precocious menstruation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is McCune-Albright syndrome?
back: A triad of precocious puberty, polyostotic fibrous dysplasia, and cafe-au-lait spots.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define delayed puberty in females and males, the most common cause, and treatment.
back: Females: no thelarche/breast development by 13 years. Males: no testicular enlargement by 14 years. Most common cause is constitutional delay; drug of choice is pulsatile GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What investigations are done in all cases of precocious puberty?
back: Bone age estimation, LH/FSH/estrogen levels (to differentiate central from peripheral), MRI brain (to rule out hamartoma), TSH (to rule out hypothyroidism), and TVS (to rule out an ovarian tumor).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What additional investigations are done specifically for heterosexual precocious puberty?
back: Testosterone, DHEA-S (to rule out an adrenal tumor), and 17-hydroxyprogesterone (to screen for CAH).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which neurotransmitters regulate the onset of puberty?
back: GABA keeps the hypothalamus dormant before puberty; kisspeptin triggers pulsatile GnRH release at puberty; leptin has a permissive role.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which hormones are mainly responsible for breast development versus pubic/axillary hair development?
back: Estrogen drives breast development; androgen drives pubic and axillary hair development.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is ovotestis, and its typical karyotype?
back: Previously called true hermaphroditism - an individual with both testicular and ovarian tissue (most commonly testis on one side and ovary on the other, occasionally bilateral). Karyotype is most commonly 46XX, less commonly 46XY.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In ovotestis, how do the internal and external genitalia develop, and how is the child typically raised?
back: Internal genitalia follow the local gonad - Mullerian duct structures develop on the ovarian side, Wolffian duct structures on the testicular side. External genitalia are ambiguous but resemble male genitalia more closely, so the child is usually assigned male at birth.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In ovotestis, what proportion develop breast tissue and what proportion menstruate?
back: About three-quarters develop gynecomastia/breast development (due to estrogen from the ovarian side); about one-quarter menstruate.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What conditions cause ambiguous genitalia due to a primary problem in the gonads themselves?
back: Ovotestis and Turner syndrome (45X0, streak ovaries).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What causes ambiguous genitalia in a 46XX female due to excess androgen in intrauterine life?
back: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), aromatase deficiency in the placenta (so maternal androgens cannot be converted to estrogen), or androgenic drugs taken by the pregnant mother.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the methods of sex determination, ranked by common use, and how is a Barr body count calculated?
back: Most common method: assessment of external genitalia. Best/gold standard method: karyotyping. Other method: Barr body count (number of X chromosomes minus 1; normal is 0 in males, 1 in females).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which enzyme deficiencies cause ambiguous genitalia in BOTH XX and XY karyotypes?
back: 3-beta-HSD deficiency and P450 oxidoreductase (OR) deficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What syndrome is associated with P450 oxidoreductase deficiency, and its feature?
back: Antley-Bixler syndrome, associated with skeletal anomalies such as craniosynostosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common cause of ambiguous genitalia in females, and of female pseudohermaphroditism?
back: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most and second most common enzyme deficiency causing CAH?
back: 21-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency (most common); 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency (second most common).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which enzyme deficiencies cause ambiguous genitalia specifically in a 46XY karyotype?
back: 17-alpha-hydroxylase deficiency and 17,20-lyase deficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is CAH screened for and diagnosed using serum 17-OH progesterone?
back: <200 ng/dL rules out CAH; 200-800 ng/dL requires an ACTH stimulation test; >=800 ng/dL is diagnostic of CAH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In 21-alpha-hydroxylase or 11-beta-hydroxylase deficiency, what are the effects of decreased aldosterone and cortisol?
back: Decreased aldosterone causes hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, salt-wasting, metabolic acidosis, and hypotension; decreased cortisol causes hypoglycemia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In this CAH presentation, what is the effect of increased androgen on external genitalia in females versus males?
back: Females develop ambiguous genitalia; males have normal male genitalia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is CAH managed, and should treatment await karyotyping?
back: Management (corticosteroids as drug of choice, correction of fluid/electrolyte imbalance, and later surgical correction of clitoromegaly) should begin without waiting for karyotype/genetic test results.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If a mother had hirsutism during pregnancy and her XX-karyotype infant is born with ambiguous genitalia, what is the likely defect?
back: Aromatase deficiency in the placenta - maternal androgens cannot be converted to estrogen, so they masculinize the fetus and also cause maternal hirsutism.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What investigations are done in all 46XX cases of suspected CAH?
back: 17-OH progesterone, testosterone, and serum electrolytes, with karyotyping as the investigation of choice.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If a mother has a prior pregnancy affected by CAH, what drug is given in the next pregnancy, and why?
back: Dexamethasone, because it crosses the placenta and suppresses fetal adrenal androgen production.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where is the androgen receptor gene located, and what is the normal androgen level in females?
back: Long arm of the X chromosome; normal androgen level in females is <70 ng/dL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In a newborn with ambiguous genitalia, what is done first, and if gonads are palpable, what further tests follow?
back: Physical examination first; investigation of choice is karyotyping. If gonads are palpable (46XY confirmed), further check testosterone, DHT, LH/FSH, pelvic USG, and AMH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: List the causes of 46XY DSD presenting with female-looking genitalia versus ambiguous genitalia.
back: Female-looking genitalia: complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS), Swyer syndrome, 5-alpha-reductase deficiency, LH receptor defect. Ambiguous genitalia: partial AIS, CAH (due to 17-alpha-hydroxylase, 17,20-lyase, 3-beta-HSD, or P450 OR deficiency), and ovotestis (46XY).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: Compare the etiopathogenesis of complete AIS versus Swyer syndrome.
back: Complete AIS: complete insensitivity to testosterone despite normal testes and normal Leydig/Sertoli cell function. Swyer syndrome: a point mutation in the SRY gene causing dysgenetic testes with dysfunctional Sertoli and Leydig cells.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: Compare internal genital organs and uterus status in complete AIS versus Swyer syndrome.
back: Complete AIS: internal genital organs absent (Wolffian duct regresses due to testosterone resistance; may have a blind-ending vagina), uterus absent. Swyer syndrome: internal genital organs female (Mullerian duct grows since dysgenetic Sertoli cells make no AMH), uterus present.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: Compare pubertal breast and pubic hair development in complete AIS versus Swyer syndrome.
back: Complete AIS: breast fully develops (testosterone is aromatized to estrogen), pubic hair scanty (androgen resistance). Swyer syndrome: dysgenetic testes produce no testosterone, so breast development is absent and pubic hair is scanty/absent.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: What are the common presenting features shared by complete AIS and Swyer syndrome?
back: Primary amenorrhea, and bilateral inguinal hernia (due to undescended testes, which carry increased malignancy risk).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: How is vaginoplasty performed and timed in these DSD conditions?
back: Using the McIndoe technique or laparoscopic Vecchietti technique, performed just before or just after marriage.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: Are streak ovaries or dysgenetic/undescended testes at risk of malignancy, and how is each managed?
back: Streak ovaries have no malignancy risk and are left in place. Dysgenetic or undescended testes have increased malignancy risk and require gonadectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: Compare LH, FSH, and androgen levels in complete AIS versus Swyer syndrome.
back: Complete AIS resembles PCOS with raised LH and normal FSH, but androgens are raised to male levels. Swyer syndrome has raised LH, raised FSH, and decreased androgens.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: How is complete AIS managed to retain female phenotype, and the timing?
back: Gonadectomy between 14-16 years (after full breast development), followed by estrogen replacement therapy and vaginoplasty.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: How is Swyer syndrome managed, and the timing?
back: Gonadectomy at the time of diagnosis (dysgenetic testes carry malignancy risk), followed by estrogen alone for 1 year (for breast development), then lifelong estrogen plus progesterone (since the uterus is present).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: What is the most common gonadal tumor and most common gonadal malignancy in these dysgenetic gonad conditions?
back: Most common tumor: gonadoblastoma. Most common malignancy: dysgerminoma/seminoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: What is the differential diagnosis for primary amenorrhea with an absent uterus, and its eponym?
back: Mullerian agenesis, also known as Mayer-Rokitansky-Kuster-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: Compare complete AIS and partial AIS on testosterone sensitivity and genitalia.
back: Complete AIS: complete insensitivity to testosterone, female-looking external genitalia, no internal male genital organs. Partial AIS: partial sensitivity to testosterone, ambiguous external genitalia, partially developed internal male genital organs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: What karyotype is found in Mullerian agenesis, and what associated anomalies should be investigated?
back: 46XX (karyotyping is done to rule out AIS). Both Mullerian ducts are absent; associated with renal anomalies (evaluated with IVP) and skeletal anomalies.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
topic: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome vs Swyer Syndrome
front: In Mullerian agenesis, what is the state of the ovaries, breast development, pubic/axillary hair, and typical symptoms?
back: Ovaries are normal with normal ovulation; breast and pubic/axillary hair develop normally (Tanner stage 4/5); typical symptoms are primary amenorrhea and coital difficulties.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the enzymatic defect in 5-alpha-reductase deficiency?
back: Failure to convert testosterone to DHT (dihydrotestosterone).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare Sertoli/Leydig cell function and external genitalia at birth in 5-alpha-reductase deficiency.
back: Both Sertoli cells (normal AMH, Mullerian duct regression) and Leydig cells (normal testosterone, Wolffian duct development) function normally, but since DHT cannot form, external genitalia appear female at birth, with undescended testes and scanty pubic/axillary hair.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What happens at puberty in 5-alpha-reductase deficiency?
back: Virilization occurs - deepening of voice, increased muscle mass, clitoromegaly (absent at birth), and hirsutism; breast development remains absent since testosterone is not converted to estrogen. FSH, LH, and testosterone remain normal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the onset of clitoromegaly and degree of breast/internal genital development between partial AIS and 5-alpha-reductase deficiency.
back: Partial AIS: clitoromegaly present since birth, breast well developed (Tanner 4/5), internal genital organs partially male. 5-alpha-reductase deficiency: clitoromegaly develops only at puberty, breast development absent (Tanner stage 1), internal genital organs well-developed male.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Give the three equivalent definitions of primary amenorrhea.
back: Absence of menstruation by age 13 without breast budding (Tanner stage 2); OR absence of menstruation by age 15 with breast budding present; OR absence of menstruation within 3 years of thelarche.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Give the compartment-wise causes and LH/FSH/estrogen pattern for hypothalamic and pituitary causes of primary amenorrhea.
back: Hypothalamus: Kallmann syndrome (GnRH decreased). Pituitary: craniopharyngioma. Both show FSH decreased, LH decreased, estrogen decreased - hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Give the compartment-wise cause and hormone pattern for ovarian causes of primary amenorrhea.
back: Gonadal dysgenesis - Turner syndrome (45XO) or Swyer syndrome (46XY); FSH raised, LH raised, estrogen/androgen decreased - hypergonadotropic hypogonadism.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Give the compartment-wise cause and hormone pattern for uterine-absent causes of primary amenorrhea.
back: Mullerian agenesis (46XX): FSH, LH, and estrogen all normal. Androgen insensitivity syndrome (46XY): FSH normal, LH raised, androgen raised to male levels.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the standard workup for primary amenorrhea?
back: Physical exam (breast development, pubic hair, palpable gonads) to assess likely uterine status, plus a urine pregnancy test, pelvic USG (confirms uterus presence/absence), FSH/LH/estrogen levels, and karyotyping (investigation of choice).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is cryptomenorrhea, and its clinical presentation?
back: 'Hidden' menstruation - monthly cyclical abdominal pain with no external bleeding since the outflow tract is obstructed; rectal exam shows a bulky uterus (hematometra) and hematocolpos.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Differentiate imperforate hymen from transverse vaginal septum on local exam and by frequency of hematocolpos/hematometra/endometriosis.
back: Imperforate hymen: tense, bulging hymen with a cough impulse present; more commonly causes hematocolpos, less commonly hematometra/endometriosis; bulge felt on the pulp of the examining finger. Transverse vaginal septum: normal-looking hymen, no bulging or cough impulse; more commonly causes hematometra/endometriosis, less commonly hematocolpos; bulge felt on the fingertip (deeper). MRI is the investigation of choice to distinguish the two.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is imperforate hymen managed?
back: Cruciate incision on the hymen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the named types of hymen?
back: Normal hymen of a sexually active/multiparous woman, imperforate hymen, cribriform hymen, and septate hymen (variants of the virgin hymen).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Why does a lower obstruction more often cause hematocolpos while a higher obstruction causes hematometra and endometriosis?
back: A lower obstruction (imperforate hymen) traps blood mainly in the vagina (hematocolpos). A higher obstruction (transverse vaginal septum) more often causes hematometra and is more associated with endometriosis, from retrograde menstruation through the patent upper tract.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is Mullerian agenesis, and which structures are absent versus present?
back: Both Mullerian ducts fail to develop, so the uterus is absent (sometimes with distal fallopian tube remnants); the upper vagina is typically absent while the lower vagina (from the urogenital sinus) is present. Ovaries, ovulation, external genitalia, breast, and pubic hair are all normal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What conditions are associated with Mullerian agenesis, and how are infertility and coital difficulty managed?
back: Associated with renal and skeletal anomalies. Coital difficulty is managed with vaginoplasty (McIndoe or Vecchietti technique, timed just before/after marriage); infertility is managed with IVF followed by surrogacy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the LH/FSH/estrogen pattern of gonadal dysgenesis versus Kallmann syndrome.
back: Gonadal dysgenesis: FSH raised, LH raised, estrogen decreased. Kallmann syndrome: FSH decreased, LH decreased, estrogen decreased.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the embryological defect in Kallmann syndrome, and its clinical presentation?
back: GnRH neurons fail to migrate from the olfactory epithelium to the hypothalamus; presents with primary amenorrhea, infertility, delayed puberty, and anosmia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which sex is more commonly affected by Kallmann syndrome, which gene is involved, and how is stature affected?
back: Can occur in both sexes but is more common in males; the KAL-1 gene is involved; stature is normal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the treatment for Kallmann syndrome?
back: Pulsatile GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare Swyer syndrome to Turner syndrome on karyotype, height, and additional features.
back: Swyer syndrome: karyotype 46XY, normal height, breast development absent, pubic hair decreased/absent, uterus small/hypoplastic/infantile. Turner syndrome: karyotype 45XO, short stature, plus features such as webbed neck, shield chest, and low posterior hairline.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What karyotype defines Turner syndrome, and what is seen in mosaic Turner syndrome?
back: 45XO (monosomy X); mosaic Turner syndrome shows two cell lines - 45XO/46XY - with a streak ovary on one side and a dysgenetic testis on the other.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Are streak ovaries or dysgenetic/undescended testes in Turner syndrome premalignant, and how is each managed?
back: Streak ovaries are not premalignant and are left alone. Dysgenetic or undescended testes carry increased malignancy risk and require gonadectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is short stature and hormone replacement managed in Turner syndrome?
back: Growth hormone until age 8 for short stature; estrogen alone for 1 year (for breast development), then combined estrogen + progesterone until the normal age of menopause. Pregnancy is a relative contraindication due to associated cardiac disease.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What baseline investigations are done in Turner syndrome, and why?
back: Karyotyping (confirms diagnosis), echocardiogram (cardiac defects), renal USG (horseshoe kidney), TSH/T3/T4 (Hashimoto's thyroiditis), CBC/lipid profile/fasting glucose, RFT/LFT, audiometry (sensorineural hearing loss), and anti-endomysial antibodies (celiac disease risk).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the associated clinical features of Turner syndrome.
back: Short stature (SHOX gene mutation), webbed neck, low posterior hairline, shield-shaped chest, widely spaced nipples, sensorineural hearing loss, autoimmune diseases (diabetes, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, celiac disease), cubitus valgus, and cardiac defects (bicuspid aortic valve more common than coarctation of the aorta).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common tumor and most common malignancy in dysgenetic gonads (as in Turner mosaicism)?
back: Most common tumor: gonadoblastoma. Most common malignancy: dysgerminoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare Mullerian agenesis (MRKH) and complete AIS on gonad type, serum testosterone, and LH level.
back: Mullerian agenesis: gonads are ovaries, serum testosterone at normal female levels, LH normal. Complete AIS: gonads are testes, serum testosterone at normal male levels, LH raised; also associated with bilateral inguinal hernia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is endometriosis, and its most and least common sites?
back: Presence of ectopic endometrial tissue outside the uterus. Most common site: ovary (chocolate cyst/endometrioma), then the pouch of Douglas, broad ligament, and uterosacral ligament. Least common sites: spleen and CNS.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the theories of spread of endometriosis to the ovary/POD, the umbilicus, and distant sites like the lungs?
back: Ovary/POD: Sampson's theory of implantation/retrograde menstruation (most accepted). Umbilicus: Halban's theory of lymphatic spread. Distant sites (e.g., lungs): Ivanoff's theory of coelomic metaplasia (an alternate source describes this instead as lymphatic spread, but coelomic metaplasia is the standard description).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is scar endometriosis, and when does it commonly or rarely occur?
back: Direct implantation of endometrial tissue in a surgical scar; common after episiotomy, cesarean section, hysterotomy, or myomectomy; least likely after hysterectomy. Presents as pain at the scar site that worsens during periods.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the typical age group and parity for endometriosis, and when should Mullerian malformations be suspected?
back: Reproductive age (25-35 years), most common in nulliparous women; if endometriosis occurs in a pubertal girl, suspect an obstructive Mullerian malformation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the most, second, and third most common symptoms of endometriosis?
back: Most common: pain (secondary dysmenorrhea > chronic pelvic pain > dyspareunia). Second: infertility. Third: adnexal mass (chocolate cyst). Dysmenorrhea plus dyspareunia together should raise suspicion for endometriosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the pelvic exam findings in endometriosis?
back: Uterus is retroverted and fixed (cannot be manually corrected), normal size, non-tender; adnexa show bilateral masses (chocolate cysts) with tenderness; the uterosacral ligament shows nodularity.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation of choice, gold standard, and typical CA-125 level in endometriosis?
back: Investigation of choice: laparoscopy. Gold standard: histopathological examination. CA-125 is raised, typically >100 IU.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What MRI finding is seen in deep rectosigmoid endometriosis, and when is MRI generally used?
back: The 'mushroom cap' sign; MRI is used particularly for bladder and bowel endometriosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the laparoscopic appearance of newer versus older endometriotic lesions.
back: Newer lesions: red flame lesions. Older lesions: gunshot/powder-burn appearance.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does the presence of a chocolate cyst/endometrioma relate to disease severity?
back: It indicates severe endometriosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is dysmenorrhea managed in mild versus moderate-severe endometriosis?
back: Mild: managed as primary dysmenorrhea (1st line OCPs, or NSAIDs if the patient wants to conceive), 2nd line progesterone, 3rd line GnRH (continuous agonist or antagonist), laparoscopy if all fail. Moderate-severe: 1st line continuous GnRH (agonist or antagonist), laparoscopy if it fails. Other usable drugs: letrozole, danazol (side effect: hirsutism); mifepristone can theoretically be used but is not FDA-approved for this.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the mnemonic for drugs that reduce estrogen in endometriosis management?
back: 'Dominos Pizza Got Late': Danazol, Progesterone, GnRH, Letrozole.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is an endometrioma (chocolate cyst) managed based on fertility desire, size, and symptoms?
back: If the patient desires pregnancy, the cyst is managed conservatively (not treated). If she does not: asymptomatic and <5cm - follow up with USG; asymptomatic >=5cm, or any symptomatic cyst - laparoscopic cystectomy with histopathology.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is endometriosis-related infertility managed?
back: Minimal/mild or unexplained infertility: IUI plus clomiphene citrate. Moderate/severe or tubal-block infertility: IVF.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does laparoscopy accomplish in managing endometriosis-related pain?
back: Confirms diagnosis, allows sampling for histopathology and disease grading, and permits pain management via fulguration of implants, adhesiolysis, or laparoscopic uterosacral nerve ablation (LUNA).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does pain severity relate to lesion depth in endometriosis, and what is the surgical last resort?
back: Pain severity is proportional to the depth of the lesion. The last resort is total abdominal hysterectomy, which stops retrograde menstruation (though pain may neither increase nor decrease after surgery if disease is extra-uterine).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is a fibroid, and key facts about its origin and hormone dependence?
back: A smooth muscle tumor (leiomyoma) arising from the myometrium, monoclonal in origin (from a single myocyte); estrogen and progesterone dependent; size is unchanged by pregnancy or OCPs, but decreases after menopause.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common fibroid location, and when should an asymptomatic fibroid be treated?
back: Intramural is the most common location. Asymptomatic fibroids are treated only if pedunculated (torsion risk), bilateral cornual (causing infertility), or if a postmenopausal fibroid enlarges (raising malignancy suspicion).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common symptom of fibroids, and can they cause dyspareunia or irregular bleeding?
back: Heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia) is most common; fibroids can cause dysmenorrhea but not dyspareunia, and never cause irregular bleeding (irregular bleeding instead suggests a polyp).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which fibroid subtype most commonly causes heavy bleeding, torsion, urinary symptoms, recurrent pregnancy loss, and infertility?
back: Heavy bleeding: submucosal fibroid. Torsion: subserosal fibroid. Urinary symptoms: cervical fibroid. Recurrent pregnancy loss and infertility: submucous fibroid.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the FIGO classification of submucosal fibroids (types 0, 1, 2).
back: Type 0: entirely within the uterine cavity. Type 1: <50% within the myometrium (>50% in the cavity). Type 2: >=50% within the myometrium (<50% in the cavity). Types 0 and 1 can be removed hysteroscopically.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is a fibroid's pseudocapsule, where is vascularity greatest/least, and where do degenerations begin?
back: The pseudocapsule covers the fibroid and houses the vessels supplying it; the periphery is most vascular and the center is least vascular; degenerations begin centrally.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common fibroid degeneration, and which degeneration begins peripherally?
back: Most common: hyaline degeneration. Calcareous degeneration begins peripherally, is most common in subserosal fibroids, and shows 'popcorn calcification'/tombstone appearance on X-ray. Malignant transformation is the least common degeneration.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe red degeneration of a fibroid - timing, cause, and management.
back: Occurs in the second half of pregnancy; caused by aseptic thrombosis of vessels supplying the fibroid, leading to necrosis; presents with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting, with raised WBC and ESR. Managed conservatively with analgesics/antiemetics - never with antibiotics, termination of pregnancy, or myomectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Give the full FIGO fibroid classification from type 0 to 8.
back: Types 0, 1, 2: submucosal. Types 3, 4: intramural. Types 5, 6: subserosal. Type 7: pedunculated subserosal. Type 8: extrauterine (e.g., cervical, broad ligament).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is a true broad ligament fibroid distinguished from a pseudo-broad ligament fibroid, by ureter position?
back: A true broad ligament fibroid arises directly from the broad ligament, with the ureter lying medial to it. A pseudo-broad ligament fibroid is actually a subserosal fibroid growing toward the broad ligament, with the ureter lying lateral to it.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare fibroid, polyp, and adenomyosis on symptoms and bimanual exam findings.
back: Fibroid: heavy menstrual bleeding is most common; an irregularly enlarged, firm, non-tender uterus (up to 20 weeks size) is found, moving with the cervix. Polyp: causes irregular bleeding/metrorrhagia (never seen with fibroids alone); the uterus feels normal on exam. Adenomyosis: menorrhagia plus dysmenorrhea is most common; a globular, symmetrically enlarged, tender uterus (Halban sign), size 10-12 weeks (never >=14 weeks).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the gross appearance of a fibroid, polyp, and adenomyosis.
back: Fibroid: whorled appearance, off-white color, with a pseudocapsule, arising from a broad base. Polyp: red, fleshy/mucosal outgrowth with a narrow base, supplied by a single feeder vessel. Adenomyosis: myometrial hypertrophy causing globular, symmetric enlargement, with multiple petechial hemorrhages on cut section (from menses of ectopic endometrial tissue).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the ultrasound/Doppler findings distinguishing fibroid, polyp, and adenomyosis?
back: Fibroid: increased peripheral vascularity on Doppler. Polyp: 'feeder vessel sign' on Doppler (investigation of choice: hysteroscopy). Adenomyosis: myometrial cysts and a 'Venetian blind' appearance (alternating dark/light bands); investigation of choice is MRI.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What MRI transition zone (TZ) thickness confirms or rules out adenomyosis?
back: >=12mm confirms adenomyosis; <8mm rules it out.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is an endometrial polyp versus an endocervical polyp managed?
back: Endometrial polyp: hysteroscopic polypectomy. Endocervical polyp: polypectomy hook.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is adenomyosis diagnosed and definitively managed?
back: Diagnosis is clinical; investigation of choice is MRI; management is total abdominal hysterectomy, with histopathology as the gold standard confirmatory test.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the workup for AUB suspected due to fibroid, polyp, or adenomyosis?
back: Urine pregnancy test and TVS; if age >=40, add endometrial biopsy to rule out hyperplasia/cancer. TVS is the investigation of choice for fibroid, saline infusion sonography (SIS) for submucosal fibroid specifically, and hysteroscopy is diagnostic plus therapeutic for polyp; MRI is not routinely needed for fibroid.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How are symptomatic fibroids managed medically?
back: 1st line (does not reduce size): tranexamic acid, OCP, progesterone. 2nd line (reduces size and bleeding): estrogen-lowering drugs (letrozole, danazol, continuous GnRH agonist/antagonist) or progesterone-lowering drugs (mifepristone/ulipristal).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How are submucosal fibroids managed surgically by FIGO type?
back: Type 0/1: hysteroscopic myomectomy. Type 2: laparoscopic myomectomy (if family not complete); total abdominal hysterectomy if family is complete, or Mirena (LNG-IUCD) if the patient refuses surgery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is subserosal/intramural fibroid managed surgically if medical therapy fails?
back: If family not complete: laparoscopic myomectomy. If family complete: uterine artery embolization (UAE), high-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU), MRI-guided focused ultrasound surgery (MRgFUS), or hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is uterine artery embolization (UAE) - route, material, and contraindications?
back: Performed via the femoral artery using polyvinyl alcohol particles; contraindicated in women wanting future pregnancy and in menopausal women.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: After myomectomy, how should a subsequent pregnancy be delivered?
back: By cesarean section (LSCS).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What structures does PID involve, and what is the most and second most common causative pattern?
back: Infection of the upper female genital tract - uterus, fallopian tubes, ovaries, and peritoneum; PID is sexually transmitted. Most common cause overall is polymicrobial; second most common is chlamydia (more common than gonococcus).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common cause of acute PID, of PID in virgin females, of PID in IUCD users, and of PID in OCP users?
back: Acute PID: gonorrhea. Virgin females: genital TB (hematogenous spread). IUCD users: Actinomyces. OCP users: chlamydia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the drug of choice for chlamydial versus gonococcal PID?
back: Chlamydia: azithromycin 2g single dose, or doxycycline 100mg twice daily for 7 days (treat the symptomatic partner too). Gonorrhea: injection ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose, or tablet cefixime 800mg single dose (treat the symptomatic partner too).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What USG signs suggest PID?
back: Adhesions inside the tube and in the peritoneal cavity, hydrosalpinx (retort-shaped tube), 'beads on a string' appearance, waist sign, and cogwheel sign (more typical of acute PID).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the gold standard investigation for PID, and what score assesses conception prognosis?
back: Laparoscopy is the gold standard (allows direct visualization of tubes/adnexa and specimen collection); the Boer-Meisel score assesses conception prognosis (it does not include organism type).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome?
back: Perihepatitis with 'violin-string' adhesions between the liver and anterior abdominal wall, more commonly caused by chlamydia than gonorrhea.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the CDC minimum criteria for diagnosing PID?
back: Lower abdominal pain plus at least one of: cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, or uterine tenderness.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the additional and specific CDC criteria for PID?
back: Additional: fever, mucopurulent discharge, abundant WBC on microscopy, raised ESR/CRP, positive lab test for chlamydia/gonorrhea. Specific: endometrial biopsy showing endometritis, TVS/MRI findings, or laparoscopy showing pus oozing from the fimbrial end.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the Gainesville staging of PID.
back: Stage 1: no peritonitis. Stage 2: peritonitis present. Stage 3: tubo-ovarian mass/abscess. Stage 4: ruptured tubo-ovarian mass.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the long-term consequences of PID?
back: Infertility (most common), ectopic pregnancy, hydrosalpinx, chronic pelvic pain, recurrent PID, and Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome; ectopic pregnancy should always be ruled out when PID is suspected.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What complications are notably uncommon with PID?
back: Pyometra and malignancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is an IUCD managed in a woman diagnosed with PID?
back: Routinely, antibiotics are given without removing the IUCD; the IUCD is removed only if PID is persistent or the patient requests removal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common primary site of infection leading to genital TB, and the route of spread?
back: Lungs (then lymph nodes, then bones) as the primary site; spreads via the hematogenous route, since genital TB is usually a secondary infection.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most and second most common site of genital TB involvement, and the least common sites?
back: Most common: fallopian tube (specifically the ampulla, then the isthmus). Second most common: endometrium (uterus). Least common: vulva and vagina.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common route of spread to the endometrium, and what complications can result?
back: Direct spread (from the tubes); can cause pyometra or Asherman syndrome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the most, second, third, and fourth most common symptoms of genital TB?
back: Most common: infertility (due to bilateral cornual/tubal block). Second: pain (chronic pelvic pain/dysmenorrhea). Third: menstrual irregularity (menorrhagia occurring first, progressing to secondary amenorrhea as the most common overall pattern, via endometritis then Asherman syndrome). Fourth: adnexal mass.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the exam findings in genital TB?
back: Per abdomen: doughy abdomen with ascites. Per vaginal: usually normal in reproductive-age women (pelvic tenderness, retroverted fixed uterus, and rarely an adnexal mass); in pubertal girls, a bilateral adnexal mass may be found.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation of choice for genital TB, and how is a virgin female tested differently?
back: Endometrial biopsy for histopathology and AFB, done in the premenstrual phase; in a virgin female, menstrual blood from day 1 is tested by PCR instead (endometrial biopsy is avoided).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the treatment for genital TB, and for resulting infertility?
back: Anti-tubercular therapy (ATT) for 6 months, which can improve fertility; if tubes remain distorted, IVF is used for infertility.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When should HSG be performed relative to starting ATT for genital TB, and why?
back: Only after starting ATT, to avoid spreading the infection during the procedure.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What dye and instrument are used for HSG, and when in the cycle is it performed?
back: Urografin (iodinated, water-soluble dye), using a Leech-Wilkinson cannula; performed in the pre-ovulatory phase, days 7-10 of the cycle.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the contraindications to HSG?
back: Pregnancy, active PID, and active genital TB.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the classic HSG appearances of fallopian tube involvement in genital TB?
back: Bilateral cornual block, beaded appearance, cotton wool appearance, golf-stick appearance, lead pipe appearance, and tobacco pouch appearance.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What HSG appearance is seen in the uterine cavity in Asherman syndrome, versus fibroid/polyp?
back: Asherman syndrome: multiple filling defects with irregular borders ('moth-eaten' appearance). Fibroid/polyp: a single, smooth, regular filling defect.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the two causes of bilateral cornual block on HSG, and which is more common?
back: Physiological block due to pain/spasm (most common) versus pathological block due to genital TB.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the three main uses of HSG?
back: Assessing tubal patency (spillage of dye), diagnosing Asherman syndrome/other filling defects, and incidentally detecting Mullerian malformations.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is physiological leucorrhea, and its characteristics?
back: Normal vaginal discharge - colorless, odorless, and non-pruritic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe bacterial vaginosis - cause, discharge, pH, and pruritus.
back: Caused by replacement of Doderlein bacilli with Gardnerella and Mobiluncus; discharge is greyish-white with a fishy odor, pH >=4.5, without significant pruritus or burning.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the whiff test, and Amsel's diagnostic criteria for bacterial vaginosis?
back: Whiff test: adding 10% KOH produces an amine/fishy odor if positive. Amsel's criteria (any 3 of 4): thin homogeneous grey-white discharge, pH >=4.5, >=20% clue cells, and a positive whiff test.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation of choice and gold standard for bacterial vaginosis, and its treatment?
back: Investigation of choice: saline microscopy (showing clue cells). Gold standard: Gram stain, yielding a Nugent score (>=7 diagnostic). Treatment: metronidazole (DOC); partner treatment is not required.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe Trichomonas vaginitis - organism, discharge, pH, and exam finding.
back: Caused by Trichomonas vaginalis (a flagellated protozoan); discharge is frothy, yellow-green, pH >=4.5, with pruritus; associated with dysuria; per speculum exam shows a 'strawberry cervix.'

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation of choice, gold standard, treatment, and partner management for Trichomonas vaginitis?
back: Investigation of choice: saline microscopy (motile organisms). Gold standard: culture (Stuart's media). Treatment: metronidazole (DOC). Partner treatment is required, since it is sexually transmitted.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe candidiasis - discharge, pH, main complaint, and exam finding.
back: Curdy white/cottage-cheese discharge, pH <4.5, with intense pruritus as the main complaint, associated with 'splash dysuria'; per speculum exam may show white flakes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation of choice, gold standard, and treatment for candidiasis (including in pregnancy)?
back: Investigation of choice: saline microscopy (pseudohyphae). Gold standard: culture. Treatment: oral fluconazole in non-pregnant women; topical clotrimazole (imidazole) in pregnancy, since oral azoles are contraindicated in pregnancy. Partner treatment only if symptomatic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In which settings is candidiasis vaginitis most common?
back: Pregnancy (more acidic pH), diabetics, and antibiotic/steroid/OCP users or immunocompromised patients.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does the epithelial cell to polymorphonuclear neutrophil ratio differ between bacterial vaginosis and Trichomonas/candidiasis?
back: Bacterial vaginosis: ratio >1. Trichomonas and candidiasis: ratio <1.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is a 'clue cell'?
back: A vaginal epithelial cell with bacteria (Gardnerella) adhered to its surface, seen in bacterial vaginosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Across vaginitis types, what is the general investigation of choice and gold standard, and the exception?
back: Investigation of choice is generally saline microscopy, and gold standard is generally culture - except bacterial vaginosis, whose gold standard is Gram stain (Nugent score).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the general rule for vaginal discharge pH across vaginitis types, and the exception?
back: Discharge pH is usually >4.5 in vaginitis, except candidiasis, where it is <4.5.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the syndromic management approach, and the kit used for vaginal discharge with a normal cervix and uterus/adnexa?
back: A symptom-based treatment protocol given without waiting for lab confirmation. Kit 2 (green) is used for vaginal discharge with a healthy or strawberry cervix and normal uterus/adnexa: secnidazole 2g single dose (covers trichomonas/bacterial vaginosis) plus fluconazole 150mg single dose (covers candidiasis); partner treatment is not given.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the kit for cervical discharge with an unhealthy/eroded/ulcerated/friable cervix but normal pelvic exam?
back: Kit 1 (grey): tablet azithromycin 1g single dose plus tablet cefixime 400-800mg single dose (covers chlamydia and gonorrhea); partner treatment is given with the same kit.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the kit for suspected PID (lower abdominal pain with uterine/cervical motion/adnexal tenderness)?
back: Kit 6 (yellow): doxycycline 100mg twice daily for 14 days, plus metronidazole 400mg twice daily for 14 days, plus cefixime 400-800mg single dose; partner treatment is given with kit 1.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If a patient tests positive for trichomonas specifically, is partner treatment given?
back: Yes, partner treatment is given for a confirmed trichomonas infection.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define infertility.
back: Inability to conceive despite 1 year of regular unprotected intercourse.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What proportion of infertility cases are due to female factors, male factors, and unexplained causes?
back: Female causes: 40-50%. Male causes: 20-30%. Unexplained: 15-30%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What basic investigations are done in every infertile couple?
back: Semen analysis, serum progesterone on day 22 (test for ovulation), and HSG (test for tubal patency), plus TSH and serum prolactin in all patients.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When should infertility evaluation begin, based on female age and risk factors?
back: <35 years: after 1 year. 35-40 years: after 6 months. >=40 years, or with known uterine/tubal pathology, prior chemo/radiotherapy, or oligomenorrhea/amenorrhea: in less than 6 months (an alternate source specifies 3 months specifically for >=45 years).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the WHO classification of ovarian causes of infertility by gonadotropin/estrogen pattern.
back: Class I: hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (e.g., Kallmann syndrome) - FSH and estrogen both low. Class II (most common, most easily treatable): normogonadotropic normogonadism (e.g., anovulation/PCOS) - FSH and estrogen both normal. Class III: hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (e.g., premature ovarian insufficiency) - FSH raised, estrogen low. Hyperprolactinemia is kept as a separate, unclassified cause.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the categories of female infertility causes, and which is most common?
back: Ovarian (most common), tubal (second most common), uterine, and cervical/unexplained causes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the uterine causes of infertility.
