back: Submucosal fibroid, endometrial polyp, Asherman syndrome, DES exposure, and Mullerian malformation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the cervical/immunological causes of infertility?
back: Antisperm antibodies in the cervical mucus and cervicitis - largely managed the same as unexplained infertility.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common and most common reversible cause of male infertility?
back: Most common: testicular causes (abnormal spermatogenesis). Most common reversible cause: varicocele.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What historical/clinical features suggest ovulatory versus anovulatory cycles?
back: Ovulatory: regular cycles, Mittelschmerz (mid-cycle pain), dysmenorrhea, premenstrual syndrome. Anovulatory: painless, irregular, heavy bleeding (due to estrogen breakthrough, not progesterone withdrawal).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What basal body temperature pattern indicates ovulation?
back: A biphasic temperature graph, with a mid-cycle rise in temperature.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare cervical mucus characteristics in the estrogen-dominant versus progesterone-dominant phase.
back: Estrogen phase: profuse, watery, elastic mucus (spinnbarkeit/stretching present), with ferning seen microscopically from day 8. Progesterone phase: thick, scanty, viscous mucus ('tack,' stretching absent), with ferning disappearing by day 18.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the best hormonal test for ovulation, its timing, and cutoff?
back: Serum progesterone on day 22 of the cycle; >10 ng/mL confirms ovulation (an alternate source cites a >=3 ng/mL threshold).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the vaginal epithelial cell types seen with estrogen, progesterone, and no hormone predominance.
back: Superficial cells (estrogen, follicular phase): pink, polygonal, pyknotic nuclei. Intermediate cells (progesterone, luteal phase): blue, polygonal, regular outline, small nuclei. Parabasal cells (no hormone - prepubertal, menopause, puerperium): blue, hazy outline, big nuclei.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the maturation index, and give example ratios for different life stages.
back: Out of 100 counted vaginal cells, written as parabasal/intermediate/superficial (A/B/C). Examples: 100/0/0 at menopause; 0/40/60 in the first half of the cycle; 0/60/40 in the second half of the cycle/pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is endometrial biopsy done for ovulation assessment, and is it commonly used?
back: Day 26 (premenstrual phase); not commonly done specifically to assess ovulation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the timing of the urinary LH surge relative to ovulation, versus the blood LH surge?
back: Urinary LH surge occurs about 24 hours before ovulation; blood LH surge occurs 32-36 hours before ovulation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe follicular monitoring for ovulation prediction.
back: The most commonly used method; TVS is performed from day 10 on alternate days; the follicle grows 1-2mm/day; ovulation is confirmed by a follicle reaching >=17mm followed by sudden collapse, plus fluid in the pouch of Douglas and a trilaminar (triple-layered) endometrium.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the endometrial histology in early proliferative, early secretory, and late secretory phases.
back: Early proliferative: simple tubular glands, telescoping, pseudostratification. Early secretory: subnuclear vacuolation (first sign of ovulation on biopsy, appears day 17-18). Late secretory: corkscrew/sawtooth glands with active secretion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the USG endometrial appearance in the early proliferative, late proliferative/ovulatory, and secretory phases.
back: Early proliferative: a single white line. Late proliferative/ovulatory: trilaminar (triple-layered) endometrium. Secretory: thick endometrium with posterior acoustic enhancement.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the drug of choice for anovulation overall, in PCOS, in pituitary ablation, and in Kallmann syndrome?
back: Overall: clomiphene citrate. PCOS: letrozole (preferred over clomiphene). Pituitary ablation: synthetic LH+FSH (hMG). Kallmann syndrome: pulsatile GnRH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the physiology of FSH and LH action on granulosa and theca cells?
back: FSH acts on granulosa cells to produce inhibin B, estrogen, and AMH. LH acts on theca cells to produce androgens.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What tests assess ovarian reserve, and when are they performed?
back: On day 2-3 of the cycle: inhibin B (decreased), FSH (increased), and antral follicle count (decreased, <10 in both ovaries combined). AMH can be tested on any day and is the best single test.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which tests are outdated or not used for ovarian reserve assessment?
back: The clomiphene citrate challenge test is outdated; serum progesterone and serum estradiol levels are not used for ovarian reserve (progesterone is instead used to test for ovulation).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the indications for ovarian reserve testing?
back: Female age >=35 years, family history of POI, history of radiotherapy/chemotherapy, or unexplained infertility.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the normal AMH level, and its levels in poor ovarian reserve versus PCOS?
back: Normal 1-3 ng/mL; low (<1, diagnostic <0.5) in poor ovarian reserve/POI; high (>3) in PCOS.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is infertility due to premature ovarian insufficiency managed?
back: Donor eggs plus IVF.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the causes of tubal infertility?
back: Genital TB, pelvic inflammatory disease, and endometriosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the investigation of choice and gold standard for tubal patency?
back: Investigation of choice: HSG (using urografin dye). Gold standard: laparoscopic chromopertubation (using methylene blue or indigo carmine dye), always performed after HSG.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is a unilateral versus bilateral tubal block on HSG managed?
back: Unilateral block: IUI plus clomiphene citrate (managed like unexplained infertility). Bilateral block: management depends on the specific site of block.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common and most common pathological cause of bilateral cornual block, and its management pathway?
back: Most common cause: physiological spasm. Most common pathological cause: genital TB. Management: hysteroscopic cannulation (passing a guidewire to relieve spasm) followed by laparoscopic chromopertubation; if the block persists due to genital TB, IVF is used.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is bilateral distal tubal block managed based on severity?
back: Laparoscopic chromopertubation first; mild block - fimbrioplasty; severe block or hydrosalpinx - IVF.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Why is salpingectomy needed before IVF in a patient with hydrosalpinx?
back: Fluid within the hydrosalpinx is embryotoxic, so salpingectomy is performed before proceeding with IVF.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is a tubo-ovarian abscess, and its PID stage and imaging findings?
back: Seen in stage 3 (Gainesville staging) PID; features include poor general condition, raised TLC, and local tenderness; USG shows an ovarian mass with heterogeneous echoes and increased vascularity on Doppler.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What conditions cause a 'frozen pelvis'?
back: Genital TB, endometriosis, and severe PID.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the cervical/immunological cause of infertility, its test, and management?
back: Antisperm antibodies in cervical mucus; tested with the Sims/post-coital/Huhner test (day 12-14 of cycle) - now considered outdated since treatment mirrors unexplained infertility; managed with clomiphene citrate plus IUI.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the uterine causes of infertility, and the most common?
back: Most common: submucosal fibroid; also Mullerian malformation (septate uterus is the most common subtype) and Asherman syndrome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the mechanism of action of clomiphene citrate, its dosing, and ovulation/pregnancy rates?
back: A SERM that blocks estrogen receptors in the brain, raising LH and FSH to drive folliculogenesis and ovulation; starting dose 50mg, maximum 150mg; ovulation rate ~80% but pregnancy rate only 10-15% (due to blocking estrogen receptors in the cervix/endometrium too, making them less suitable for sperm transport and implantation).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the side effects of clomiphene citrate?
back: Hot flashes (most common), ovarian cyst formation, vaginal dryness, twin pregnancy (5-8%), OHSS (~1%), and visual disturbances (an indication for immediate discontinuation).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does letrozole's mechanism and side-effect profile compare to clomiphene?
back: Letrozole acts peripherally with no effect on the endometrium/cervix, giving better live birth and pregnancy rates than clomiphene, without causing hot flashes; both require an intact HPO axis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the prerequisites and indications for IUI?
back: Minimum sperm concentration 10 million/mL with at least 14% normal morphology; an OPD procedure using processed/centrifuged sperm (0.4-0.8mL); indications include cervical factor infertility, unexplained infertility (with clomiphene), male ejaculatory problems (hypospadias/epispadias), oligospermia (10-16 million/mL), and vaginismus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the prerequisites, procedure, and success rate of IVF?
back: Minimum 5 million sperm/mL, 50,000-100,000 sperm per oocyte; the female is hyperovulated with hMG, follicular monitoring from day 10, hCG trigger at >=17mm (2 follicles), egg retrieval 32-36 hours later, embryo transfer on day 3 (cleavage) or day 5 (blastocyst), 2cm below the fundus; success rate 20-30%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the indications for IVF, and when is it contraindicated?
back: Indications: tubal block, Mullerian agenesis (with surrogacy), decreased ovarian reserve (with donor eggs), severe endometriosis, genital TB with distorted anatomy, and oligospermia (5-10 million/mL). Not done for sperm count <5 million/mL, azoospermia, or asthenospermia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is ICSI, its procedure, and indications?
back: Same procedure as IVF except a single sperm is injected directly into each oocyte; indicated for the indications and contraindications of IVF, plus severe oligospermia and testicular azoospermia (after surgical sperm extraction).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What surgical techniques retrieve sperm for ICSI in testicular azoospermia?
back: TESA (testicular sperm aspiration), TESE (testicular sperm extraction), PESA (percutaneous epididymal sperm aspiration), and MESA (microsurgical epididymal sperm aspiration).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the pre-analysis requirements for semen analysis?
back: 2-7 days of abstinence; sample must reach the lab within 1 hour; analysis is performed after liquefaction (liquefaction time 20-30 minutes).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Does the WHO semen analysis give average or minimum values, and what is the most important parameter?
back: It gives minimum criteria needed for conception, not average values; the most important parameter is morphology, then motility, then concentration.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the updated (2022) WHO reference values for semen volume, sperm count, concentration, motility, viability, and morphology.
back: Semen volume 1.4mL; total sperm count 39 million/ejaculate; sperm concentration 16 million/mL; total motility 42% (progressive motility 30%, non-progressive 1%, immotile 20%); viability 54%; morphology 4% (strict Tygerberg criteria).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define the abnormal semen analysis terms: aspermia, oligospermia, severe oligospermia, azoospermia, asthenospermia, necrozoospermia, teratozoospermia.
back: Aspermia: absent semen. Oligospermia: concentration <16 million/mL. Severe oligospermia: <5 million/mL. Azoospermia: no sperm in semen. Asthenospermia: decreased motility. Necrozoospermia: increased non-viable sperm. Teratozoospermia: abnormal morphology.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the male reproductive tract pathway from testis to ejaculation, and which gland contributes most to semen volume?
back: Testis (spermatogenesis) -> epididymis -> vas deferens -> seminal vesicle -> ejaculatory duct -> prostate -> bulbourethral (Cowper's) gland -> urethra. The seminal vesicle contributes the most to semen volume (fructose-rich, alkaline secretions), followed by the prostate (acidic secretions), then the bulbourethral gland (secretes only during intercourse).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is azoospermia confirmed, and what is the next step in workup?
back: Repeat semen analysis after a gap of at least 1 week (up to 1 month); if confirmed, check FSH, LH, and testosterone (FSH is the single best test).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the hormonal pattern and organ involved in pretesticular (hypogonadotropic) azoospermia, with causes.
back: FSH decreased, LH decreased, testosterone decreased; hypothalamus/pituitary involved; causes include Kallmann syndrome, hypothyroidism, and hyperprolactinemia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the hormonal pattern and organ involved in testicular (hypergonadotropic) azoospermia, with causes.
back: FSH increased, LH increased, testosterone decreased; testis involved; causes include Klinefelter syndrome and mumps orchitis (also heat exposure, e.g., in miners).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the hormonal pattern in obstructive azoospermia, and its causes.
back: FSH, LH, and testosterone all normal; the sperm pathway (epididymis, vas deferens, ejaculatory duct) is affected; causes include vasectomy, cystic fibrosis, varicocele, and retrograde ejaculation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the two important causes of azoospermia, and their specific pathology?
back: Cystic fibrosis (bilateral absence of the vas deferens and seminal vesicles) and Klinefelter syndrome (hypoplastic testes).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is OATS, and its risk factors?
back: OATS = oligospermia, asthenospermia, and teratozoospermia occurring together; risk factors include varicocele and smoking.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In obstructive azoospermia with normal hormones, how does transrectal USG guide further workup based on seminal vesicle status?
back: If the seminal vesicle is dilated: ejaculatory duct obstruction. If the seminal vesicle is absent: congenital bilateral absence (cystic fibrosis, confirmed with CFTR gene testing). If the seminal vesicle is normal: retrograde ejaculation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If seminal vesicles are present and normal but azoospermia persists, what does scrotal USG assess?
back: It localizes an obstruction in the epididymis or vas deferens, when semen volume and fructose are normal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the genotype, phenotype, and testicular findings in Klinefelter syndrome?
back: Genotype 47XXY (an example of a false-positive Barr body); phenotype - tall stature, long legs, gynecomastia, decreased IQ, decreased lifespan; testes are small and hypoplastic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What instrument measures testicular size, and what condition is the classic example of a false-negative Barr body?
back: The Prader orchidometer measures testicular size; Turner syndrome is the classic example of a false-negative Barr body.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Match male infertility causes/findings to their management.
back: Oligospermia 10-15 million/mL: IUI. Oligospermia 5-10 million/mL: IVF. Severe oligospermia <5 million/mL: ICSI. Non-obstructive azoospermia: surgical sperm retrieval followed by ICSI. Obstructive azoospermia: resection and anastomosis. Erectile dysfunction: sildenafil. Premature ejaculation: an SSRI (fluoxetine). Retrograde ejaculation/ejaculatory dysfunction (e.g., hypospadias): IUI.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the best single hormonal test to distinguish obstructive from non-obstructive azoospermia, and what defines proximal versus distal epididymal function?
back: Serum FSH is the best differentiator. The proximal epididymis is the site of sperm maturity; the distal epididymis is the site of sperm motility.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What structures do the Mullerian ducts form, and how?
back: The right and left Mullerian ducts fuse (from below upward) to form the fallopian tubes (unfused upper portions), uterus, cervix, and upper half of the vagina; a septum forms during fusion and normally resolves by 20 weeks of intrauterine life, leaving a dome-shaped fundus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is Class I Mullerian malformation (Mullerian agenesis), and the state of ovarian function?
back: Both Mullerian ducts are absent (no uterus/cervix/upper vagina); ovaries, ovulation, and estrogen levels remain normal since the ovaries develop independently.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe Class II (unicornuate uterus) - structures present and key associations.
back: Only one Mullerian duct develops (+/- a rudimentary horn on the other side): 1 fallopian tube, half a uterus, half a cervix, half the upper vagina. On HSG: a single fallopian tube and banana-shaped uterus. It is the uterine malformation most associated with renal anomalies, and is also associated with ectopic ovary, ectopic pregnancy, and unilateral dysmenorrhea; transverse lie is not seen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe Class III (uterus didelphys) - structures present and distinguishing feature.
back: Both Mullerian ducts are present but fail to fuse (or fuse only in the vagina): 2 fallopian tubes, 2 uteri, 2 cervices (the distinguishing feature), and 1 or 2 vaginas. On HSG, two Leech-Wilkinson cannulae are used. Transverse lie is not seen.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe Class IV (bicornuate uterus) - fusion defect and structures.
back: Both Mullerian ducts fuse in the cervix and vagina but not in the uterus; 2 fallopian tubes, 2 uteri, 1 cervix, 1 vagina; the angle between the uterine horns is obtuse and the distance between horns is >=4cm; the fundus of the uterus is divided (separated), distinguishing it from a septate uterus, though HSG alone cannot make this distinction since it can't visualize the external fundal contour.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe Class V (septate uterus) - fusion defect and structures.
back: Both Mullerian ducts fuse and a septum forms, but the septum fails to resorb; 2 fallopian tubes, 2 uterine cavities, 1 cervix, 1 vagina; the angle between uterine horns is acute and the distance between horns is <4cm; the fundus is undivided/fused (unlike bicornuate uterus) though it appears normal from outside.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe Class VI (arcuate uterus) and its reproductive outcome.
back: The mildest anomaly - the Mullerian ducts fuse and the septum resorbs almost completely, leaving only a flat/dimpled fundus; it has the best reproductive outcome of all the malformations.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe Class VII (DES-exposure related anomaly) - cause and associated findings.
back: Caused by in-utero exposure to diethylstilbestrol (DES), a synthetic estrogen once given to pregnant women (now discontinued) - it is teratogenic. Most common associated uterine anomaly is a hypoplastic uterus; most specific anomaly is a T-shaped uterus; most common associated malignancy is clear cell cancer of the cervix and vagina. DES exposure does not cause renal anomalies in the female fetus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does HSG distinguish unicornuate, didelphys, bicornuate, and septate uterus?
back: One fallopian tube: unicornuate uterus. Two fallopian tubes with 2 Leech-Wilkinson cannulae needed: didelphys uterus. Two fallopian tubes with 1 cannula and an obtuse angle (>=4cm apart) between horns: bicornuate uterus. Two fallopian tubes with 1 cannula and an acute angle (<4cm apart): septate uterus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common Mullerian anomaly, and the anomaly with best/worst reproductive outcome?
back: Most common: septate uterus (more common than bicornuate). Best outcome: arcuate uterus (better than didelphys uterus). Worst outcome: septate uterus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common clinical feature of Mullerian malformations, and which is most associated with 2nd-trimester abortion?
back: Recurrent abortion is the most common overall feature; septate uterus is specifically associated more with 2nd-trimester abortion than 1st-trimester.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which malformation is most associated with infertility, and which is uncommonly associated with transverse lie?
back: Septate uterus is most associated with infertility; transverse lie is uncommon in uterus didelphys.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which malformations are most associated with renal anomalies?
back: Unicornuate uterus (most), then Mullerian agenesis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What surgery is done for septate uterus, and the outdated alternative?
back: Hysteroscopic resection of the septum; the outdated surgery was Jones metroplasty.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What surgery is done for bicornuate/didelphys uterus, and the outdated alternative for bicornuate?
back: Unification surgery; the outdated surgery for bicornuate uterus specifically was Strassman metroplasty.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the general indication for surgery in Mullerian malformations?
back: Recurrent abortion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What malpresentations are associated with which Mullerian malformations?
back: Transverse lie: septate uterus, sub-septate uterus, bicornuate uterus (mnemonic SSB). Breech presentation: uterus didelphys.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the first investigation for recurrent abortion versus infertility in suspected Mullerian malformation, and the investigation of choice/gold standard?
back: Recurrent abortion: TVS first. Infertility: HSG first. Investigation of choice overall: 3D ultrasound. Gold standard: MRI. Last resort (combined diagnostic/therapeutic): laparoscopy plus hysteroscopy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What gynecological and obstetric complications are associated with Mullerian malformations?
back: Gynecological: infertility (rare), outflow tract obstruction leading to hematometra, endometriosis (should prompt ruling out a Mullerian malformation in a pubertal girl), and dysmenorrhea. Obstetric: recurrent pregnancy loss (most common overall complication), preterm labor, and malpresentation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What specific complications are seen with unicornuate uterus?
back: Unilateral dysmenorrhea, ectopic pregnancy, ectopic ovary, and unilateral renal anomalies.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What ligaments and muscles support the uterus?
back: Ligaments (together called the triradiate ligament): cardinal/Mackenrodt's/transverse cervical, pubocervical, and uterosacral ligaments. Muscles (mnemonic BLSD): bulbospongiosus, levator ani (most important), and superficial/deep transverse perinei.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the mechanical/angular supports of the uterus, and how do they appear on pelvic exam?
back: Angle of anteversion (~90 degrees, between cervix and vagina) and angle of anteflexion (~120 degrees, between uterus and cervix). On exam: in an anteverted uterus, the fundus is felt before the anterior lip of the cervix; in a retroverted uterus, the posterior lip of the cervix is felt first.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the first step/stage of prolapse, and can it be corrected?
back: Retroversion of the uterus, which can typically be manually corrected.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank the round ligament and uterosacral ligament by their roles in anteversion versus preventing retroversion.
back: The round ligament is the primary support keeping the uterus anteverted (uterosacral ligament plays a secondary role here); the uterosacral ligament is more important than the round ligament in preventing retroversion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which condition causes a fixed (non-correctable) retroverted uterus, and which ligament does not support the uterus at all?
back: Endometriosis causes a fixed retroverted uterus; the broad ligament does not support the uterus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which part of levator ani, if damaged, carries the highest risk of prolapse?
back: Puborectalis (more than pubococcygeus).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is a decubitus ulcer in prolapse, its cause, and management?
back: Caused by venous congestion in a severely prolapsed, exposed cervix/vagina; managed with acriflavine (antiseptic) and glycerine packing.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the POP-Q classification's reference point, and how is uterine prolapse graded?
back: The hymen is the reference point. 1st degree: prolapse >1cm above the hymen. 2nd degree: within 1cm of the hymen (above or below). 3rd degree: >1cm below the hymen. Procidentia: the entire cervix and uterus lie outside the vagina.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the best method to prevent prolapse, and the main risk factors?
back: Kegel's/perineal exercises. Risk factors: elderly/menopausal age, birth trauma, multiparity/multigravida, and instrumental delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe De Lancey's three levels of vaginal support, their supporting structures, and resulting defects.
back: Level 1 (upper 1/3 of vagina): uterosacral and cardinal ligaments; defects cause apical/vault prolapse, supravaginal cervical elongation, and enterocele. Level 2 (middle 1/3): paravaginal tissue/arcus tendineus fascia; defects cause cystocele and rectocele. Level 3 (lower 1/3): perineal body and its muscles; defects cause urethrocele and a lax perineum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List and manage the five types of vaginal wall prolapse.
back: Cystocele (anterior wall, upper 2/3) and urethrocele (anterior wall, lower 1/3): anterior colporrhaphy. Enterocele (posterior wall, upper 1/3): McCall culdoplasty. Rectocele (posterior wall, middle 1/3) and lax perineum (posterior wall, lower 1/3): posterior colpoperineorrhaphy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Does management of vaginal prolapse depend on age or parity, and what are the outdated surgeries for enterocele?
back: No, management is the same across all ages and parity. Outdated surgeries: Moschowitz repair and Halban repair.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is vault prolapse, and how is it prevented after hysterectomy?
back: Prolapse of the vaginal stump/cuff after hysterectomy; prevented by prophylactic uterosacral ligament suspension at the time of hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What vaginal surgeries treat vault prolapse?
back: Uterosacral suspension (both prophylactic and therapeutic - most commonly performed), sacrospinous fixation, and Le Fort colpocleisis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What abdominal surgery treats vault prolapse, and its technique?
back: Sacral colpopexy - technically difficult but gives the best results; a mesh is attached on one end to the sacral promontory and on the other to the vaginal vault/stump.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Is Burch colposuspension used for vault prolapse?
back: No - Burch colposuspension is a surgery for stress urinary incontinence (SUI), not vault prolapse.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is uterine prolapse managed in a reproductive-age woman who wants future childbearing?
back: Sling surgery/colpopexy; Shirodkar sling surgery gives the best results (uses a hook around the psoas muscle to avoid complications on the left side); Purandare sling surgery has the fewest complications. Prerequisite: uterocervical length <12.5cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is uterine prolapse with an elongated uterocervical length managed in a woman not desiring future childbearing?
back: Fothergill's/Manchester surgery (best option) - a two-step procedure of cervical amputation followed by plication of the cardinal ligaments; risks include recurrent 2nd-trimester abortion (from cervical incompetence), cervical stenosis, and cervical dystocia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is prolapse managed in a menopausal woman or one who has completed childbearing, especially with cystocele/rectocele?
back: Vaginal hysterectomy; if combined with 3rd-degree uterine prolapse plus cystocele and rectocele, the combined procedure (vaginal hysterectomy plus anterior colporrhaphy plus posterior colpoperineorrhaphy) is called Ward-Mayo hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is prolapse managed in an older woman (around 55-65 years) with contraindications to surgery (e.g., diabetes, hypertension)?
back: Le Fort colpocleisis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is prolapse managed in an elderly woman (>=65 years) with comorbidities, or in a pregnant/postpartum woman?
back: Ring pessary, which should be changed every 3 months.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common site of ureteric injury during hysterectomy, and the procedure carrying the highest risk?
back: Most common site: 'water under the bridge' (2cm lateral to the internal os, where the uterine artery crosses the ureter), more common than the pelvic brim (second most common). Highest risk procedure: Wertheim's (radical) hysterectomy; the most common overall injury occurs during simple total abdominal hysterectomy given its higher frequency of use.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Differentiate ureterovaginal, vesicovaginal, and urethrovaginal fistula by symptoms.
back: Ureterovaginal fistula: continuous urinary dribbling from the vagina with normal bladder emptying. Vesicovaginal fistula: continuous dribbling with impaired/absent normal bladder emptying. Urethrovaginal fistula: urine comes from both the urethra and vagina during micturition.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the methylene blue 3-swab test and its interpretation for each fistula type.
back: Methylene blue dye is instilled into the bladder and three vaginal swabs are checked. Upper swab wet with urine but not blue: ureterovaginal fistula. Middle and lowermost swabs wet and blue: vesicovaginal fistula. Only the lowermost swab wet and blue: urethrovaginal fistula.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common urinary fistula, and its most common cause in developed versus developing countries?
back: Vesicovaginal fistula (VVF) is most common; developed countries: hysterectomy; developing countries: obstructed labor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the investigation of choice and gold standard for VVF?
back: Investigation of choice: methylene blue 3-swab test. Gold standard: cystoscopy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the vaginal and abdominal surgical techniques for VVF repair?
back: Vaginal: Latzko technique. Abdominal: Chassar Moir technique/O'Connor's surgery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is VVF repair performed - immediate versus delayed?
back: If diagnosed at the time of surgery or within 24 hours of symptoms: immediate repair. If diagnosed after 24 hours, or following obstructed labor: repair is delayed 6 weeks to 3 months.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the postoperative instructions after VVF repair?
back: Continuous bladder catheter drainage and antibiotics for about 2 weeks; no per-speculum/per-vaginal exam or intercourse for 3-6 months; no pregnancy for 1 year; conception after VVF repair should be delivered by classical cesarean section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is stress urinary incontinence (SUI), and its gold standard versus most commonly performed surgery?
back: Involuntary urine leakage with increased intra-abdominal pressure (coughing, sneezing). Gold standard: Burch colposuspension (abdominal surgery, suspending the proximal urethra to Cooper's/iliopectineal ligament). Most commonly performed: TOT (tension-free transobturator tape) more than TVT (transvaginal tape), both suspending the mid-urethra.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What drug may help SUI, and what does cyclical hematuria suggest?
back: Duloxetine. Cyclical hematuria suggests a fistula between the bladder and uterus, or bladder endometriosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are peg cells, and where are they normally found?
back: Normal cells of the fallopian tube (one of its three epithelial cell types).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the Tanner staging system, and Gainesville staging?
back: Tanner staging: 5 stages describing breast and pubic hair development. Gainesville staging: stages of pelvic inflammatory disease severity.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the Ferriman-Gallwey score, Prader score, and Nugent score used for?
back: Ferriman-Gallwey: scores hirsutism across 9 body sites, each scored 0-4. Prader score: grades the degree of virilization. Nugent score: grades bacterial vaginosis on Gram stain, with a score of 7-10 being diagnostic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the Boer-Meisel score, Rotterdam criteria, and Amsel's criteria used for?
back: Boer-Meisel: estimates chances of conception in PID patients (does not include organism type). Rotterdam criteria: diagnoses PCOS. Amsel's criteria: diagnoses bacterial vaginosis (any 3 of 4: greyish-white discharge, pH >=4.5, clue cells >=20%, positive whiff test).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is splash dysuria a feature of, and what do 'feeder vessels' and 'mushroom cap' signs indicate?
back: Splash dysuria: candidiasis. Feeder vessel sign (on Doppler): endometrial polyp. Mushroom cap sign (on MRI): deep rectosigmoid endometriosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does a 'Venetian blind' USG appearance indicate, and what is the Honan criteria for?
back: Venetian blind appearance: adenomyosis. Honan criteria: diagnoses Bartholin gland carcinoma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Match histopathological findings to their tumors: Schiller-Duval bodies, Call-Exner bodies, Psammoma bodies, Walthard cell nests, signet ring cells.
back: Schiller-Duval bodies: yolk sac tumor. Call-Exner bodies: granulosa cell tumor. Psammoma bodies: epithelial ovarian tumors (especially serous). Walthard cell nests: Brenner's tumor. Signet ring cells: Krukenberg tumor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the named surgeries for vault prolapse, SUI, VVF, uterine inversion, vaginoplasty, and outdated Mullerian malformation surgeries.
back: Vault prolapse: sacral colpopexy, uterosacral suspension, sacrospinous fixation. SUI (gold standard): Burch colposuspension; most commonly done: TOT/TVT. VVF: Chassar Moir technique, Latzko technique. Uterine inversion: Haultain's and Halban's surgery. Vaginoplasty: McIndoe vaginoplasty, laparoscopic Vecchietti technique. Bicornuate uterus (outdated): Strassman metroplasty. Septate uterus (outdated): Jones metroplasty.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the natural contraceptive methods.
back: Rhythm/calendar method, cyclobeads (used by less-educated populations), the Billings (cervical mucus) method, and basal body temperature method.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the barrier contraceptive methods and key facts about each.
back: Male condom (single-use); female condom (FC-2, distributed by NACO to female sex workers, has 2 rings, can be reused up to twice); diaphragm (reusable up to 1 year with washing/drying); 'Today' sponge (contains 1g nonoxynol-9, reusable multiple times within 24 hours).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the LARC (long-acting reversible contraceptive) methods, and the permanent methods?
back: LARC: implants, IUCDs, and injections. Permanent: female tubal ligation (minilaparotomy or laparoscopy) or hysteroscopic Essure; male vasectomy. Tubal ligation itself is permanent, not a LARC.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Match the main contraceptive mechanism to OCPs, POPs, progesterone implant/injection, Cu-IUCD, Mirena, and Centchroman.
back: OCPs (E+P): anovulation (suppresses FSH more than LH), plus thickened cervical mucus and thinned endometrium. POP: thickens cervical mucus. Progesterone implant/injection: anovulation plus thickened cervical mucus. Cu-IUCD: prevents fertilization via a copper-induced inflammatory reaction, and inhibits implantation. Mirena: prevents implantation by thinning the endometrium. Centchroman: makes the endometrium 'out of phase,' inhibiting implantation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Classify OCPs by ethinyl estradiol dose.
back: High dose >=50 mcg; low dose <50 mcg (30-35 mcg); very low dose <20 mcg; minimum effective dose 10 mcg.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the non-contraceptive uses of OCPs.
back: Drug of choice for irregular cycles; reduces blood loss in fibroids/adenomyosis/AUB; drug of choice for primary dysmenorrhea and minimal-mild endometriosis; drug of choice for hirsutism, acne, and hyperandrogenism; helps manage premenstrual syndrome (though the DOC for PMS itself is fluoxetine, an SSRI); thickened cervical mucus from the progesterone component lowers the risk of PID, STDs, and ectopic pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common PID organism and most common vaginitis associated with OCP use?
back: PID: chlamydia. Vaginitis: candidiasis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the cancers whose risk is increased, decreased, or unaffected by OCPs.
back: Increased: hepatic adenoma, breast cancer in perimenopausal women, cervical cancer (a reversible risk). Decreased: endometrial cancer (via endometrial thinning and anovulation), ovarian cancer and ovarian cysts (via anovulation), benign breast disease, colorectal cancer. No effect: liver cancer.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the WHO Category 4 (absolute) contraindications to OCPs.
back: History of breast cancer; severe uncontrolled hypertension (>=160/110; well-controlled hypertension in a non-smoker is not a contraindication); undiagnosed vaginal bleeding; smoker aged >=35; history/family history of thromboembolism, CVA, or MI, or conditions predisposing to thrombosis; pregnancy or history of peripartum cardiomyopathy/cholestasis of pregnancy; severe hypercholesterolemia/hypertriglyceridemia; impaired liver function/liver cancer/cholestatic liver disease; diabetes with vasculopathy; migraine with aura; and postpartum <21 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the standard OCP consumption method.
back: A pack contains 21 hormonal pills (Mala-N/Mala-D add 7 additional ferrous fumarate pills); one pill is taken daily starting between day 1-5 of the cycle; estrogen and progesterone levels are maintained for 21 days, then stopping the pills causes hormone withdrawal that induces menses, and that bleeding day becomes day 1 of the next cycle.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When should OCPs be avoided or delayed postpartum?
back: Absolutely contraindicated before 21 days postpartum; non-breastfeeding women can start at >=3 weeks (ideally >=6 weeks); women at high thrombosis risk should wait 6 weeks; exclusively breastfeeding women should start at >=6 weeks, ideally waiting until 6 months postpartum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What drug interactions reduce OCP efficacy, and which drugs are exceptions?
back: Enzyme-inducing drugs reduce efficacy - most anti-epileptics, rifampicin, griseofulvin, and antiretrovirals; exceptions that can be co-administered are lamotrigine, levetiracetam, and valproate.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the contraceptive patch - composition, mechanism, and application schedule.
back: A combined estrogen-progesterone patch acting by anovulation; same absolute contraindications as OCPs; a new patch is applied weekly for 3 weeks, followed by a patch-free week; it is reversible and gives no STD protection.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is backup contraception needed with patch initiation or patch replacement delay?
back: If started day 1-5 of the cycle, no backup is needed; if started after day 5, backup contraception is needed for 7 days. If a patch is reapplied within 48 hours of removal, no backup is needed; if reapplied after 48 hours, backup contraception is needed for 7 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the action for 1-2 missed OCP pills?
back: Take one pill as soon as possible, continue the normal schedule, and no backup contraception is required.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the action for >=3 missed pills in the 1st or 2nd week?
back: Take one pill immediately, complete the pack as scheduled, use backup contraception for 7 days, and use emergency contraception if intercourse occurred in the previous 72 hours.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What additional step is needed for >=3 missed pills in the 3rd week?
back: All of the above steps, plus start a new pack immediately after finishing the current one (skip the non-hormonal pills, don't wait for menses).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is DMPA's brand name in India's National Family Planning program, dose, route, and dosing interval?
back: Antara; 150mg; intramuscular; repeated every 3 months, with a 4-week window period.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is DMPA's mechanism, advantages, and disadvantages?
back: Mechanism: anovulation. Advantages: decreases seizure frequency and decreases sickling in sickle cell anemia. Disadvantages: delayed return of fertility, decreased bone mineral density, and irregular bleeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If a woman returns for her DMPA injection on the scheduled date, or within the window period (2 weeks early to 4 weeks late), what is done?
back: The injection is given with no UPT and no backup contraception needed.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If a woman returns for DMPA more than 4 weeks after the scheduled date, what is done?
back: Pregnancy must be ruled out first, then the injection is given, plus backup contraception for 7 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare CuT and Mirena on mechanism, main side effect, and PID risk.
back: CuT: prevents fertilization, releases 50mcg copper/day, most common side effect is increased bleeding, and carries an increased infection (PID) risk at insertion. Mirena: prevents implantation, releases 20mcg levonorgestrel/day, reduces bleeding (useful in AUB), and decreases PID risk by thickening cervical mucus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare CuT and Mirena on ectopic pregnancy risk and emergency contraceptive use.
back: Both increase the relative chance of ectopic pregnancy if contraceptive failure occurs. CuT can be used as emergency contraception; Mirena cannot.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the absolute contraindications for both CuT and Mirena?
back: Undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, distorted uterine cavity (e.g., fibroid), active PID, and suspected/confirmed pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the CuT-specific and Mirena-specific contraindications?
back: CuT: absolute - Wilson's disease; relative - molar pregnancy. Mirena: absolute - history of breast cancer; relative - active liver disease.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What conditions are NOT contraindications for IUCD?
back: Previous history of ectopic pregnancy, previous history of PID, nulliparity, and HIV-positive status.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is PID following IUCD insertion managed?
back: Oral antibiotics for 48-72 hours; if no response, IV antibiotics; if still no improvement after 24 hours, remove the IUCD (with continued antibiotics).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What organism is classically associated with PID in IUCD users, and its management?
back: Actinomyces; managed by removing the IUCD and giving antibiotics.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What risks does pregnancy with an IUCD in place increase, and what does it NOT increase?
back: Increases infection, preterm labor, IUGR, and abortion; does not increase teratogenicity risk.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In a pregnant woman with an IUCD, how does management differ if she wants to continue versus terminate the pregnancy, and by thread visibility?
back: If she wants to terminate: remove the IUCD and perform termination of pregnancy. If she wants to continue and the thread is visible: remove the IUCD and continue the pregnancy. If she wants to continue and the thread is not visible: do not remove the IUCD, give antibiotics, and continue the pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If IUCD threads are missing, what is the stepwise workup?
back: First check with USG; if the IUCD is not seen, get a serial X-ray - if still not seen, it is presumed expelled (no intervention). If seen coiled inside the uterus, no intervention is needed; if perforation has occurred with the IUCD partly in the peritoneal cavity, hysteroscopic removal is done; if entirely in the peritoneal cavity, immediate laparoscopic removal is required.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the non-contraceptive advantages of Mirena?
back: Decreases endometrial hyperplasia, decreases endometrial cancer risk, and decreases blood loss (useful in managing AUB).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the mechanism of action of hormonal emergency contraception, versus Cu-IUCD?
back: Hormonal EC mainly works by delaying ovulation; Cu-IUCD works by preventing fertilization.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank emergency contraceptive methods by effectiveness, and identify the most effective hormonal option.
back: Cu-IUCD (most effective overall) > ulipristal > mifepristone > levonorgestrel (LNG). Ulipristal is the most effective hormonal EC.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the time window for emergency contraception, and management if the patient vomits after taking it?
back: Most effective within 72 hours of unprotected intercourse, but can be given up to 5 days (120 hours); if the patient vomits after taking EC, repeat the dose.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the doses of levonorgestrel, the Yuzpe method, and centchroman for emergency contraception?
back: Levonorgestrel: 1.5mg single dose, or 0.75mg followed by another 0.75mg 12 hours later (most commonly used, 'Plan B'). Yuzpe method: 2 OCP tablets (ethinyl estradiol + norgestrel), repeated after 12 hours. Centchroman: 2 tablets (60mg each), repeated after 12 hours.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the doses of ulipristal acetate and mifepristone for emergency contraception?
back: Ulipristal acetate: 30mg single dose. Mifepristone: 10-50mg single dose.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which drugs are NOT used as emergency contraceptives?
back: Progesterone-only pills (POP), Mirena, and misoprostol.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are important counseling points about emergency contraception?
back: Failure rate ~1%; does not protect against STDs; is not an abortifacient; not meant for regular/repeated use; most women bleed within 3 weeks of taking EC - if no bleeding occurs by then, rule out pregnancy with a UPT.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If a woman presents on day 5 after unprotected intercourse, what are the best and second-best EC choices?
back: Best: Cu-IUCD. Second-best: ulipristal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In which situations should specific emergency contraceptives be avoided?
back: Avoid ulipristal in breastfeeding women; avoid hormonal EC (prefer Cu-IUCD) in women on enzyme-inducing drugs; avoid LNG in obese women (prefer ulipristal or Cu-IUCD).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the 'Rule of 3' for starting contraception postpartum?
back: Exclusively breastfeeding women can start hormonal contraception 3 weeks after delivery; non-breastfeeding/partially breastfeeding women should start by 3 months after delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which contraceptives can be started from day 1 postpartum?
back: Condoms, centchroman, progesterone-only pills, and progesterone implants.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the timing for postplacental, postpartum, and interval IUCD insertion?
back: Postplacental: within 10 minutes of delivery. Postpartum: up to 48 hours after delivery. Interval insertion: after 6 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When can emergency contraception and tubectomy be performed postpartum?
back: Emergency contraception: after 4 weeks postpartum. Tubectomy: postpartum ligation between 24 hours and 1 week after delivery (mini-laparotomy, modified Pomeroy technique); interval ligation after 6 weeks; laparoscopic tubal ligation is contraindicated in the immediate postpartum period.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the specific postpartum timing for starting OCPs, based on breastfeeding status?
back: Non-breastfeeding: can start at >=3 weeks postpartum (ideally >=6 weeks). Exclusively breastfeeding: can start at >=6 weeks (ideally >=6 months) postpartum. OCPs are absolutely contraindicated before 21 days postpartum in all women.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the postpartum timing for starting DMPA, based on breastfeeding status?
back: Breastfeeding: give at >=4 weeks postpartum. Non-breastfeeding: can give from day 1.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What position is the patient placed in for hysteroscopy, and what anesthesia is used for diagnostic versus therapeutic procedures?
back: Lithotomy position; diagnostic hysteroscopy uses a paracervical block, while therapeutic hysteroscopy requires general or regional anesthesia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the indications for hysteroscopy?
back: Asherman syndrome, septate uterus, endometrial polypectomy, type 0/1 submucous fibroid, and hysteroscopic tubal ligation (Essure) for permanent sterilization.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is fluid deficit calculated during hysteroscopy, and at what deficit is the procedure stopped for electrolyte-rich versus electrolyte-deficient media?
back: Fluid deficit = fluid instilled minus fluid recovered. Electrolyte-rich media (e.g., normal saline, Ringer's lactate): stop at >=2.5 liters deficit. Electrolyte-deficient media (e.g., mannitol, glycine): stop at >=1 liter deficit.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare electrolyte-rich and electrolyte-deficient distension media on cautery compatibility and complications.
back: Electrolyte-rich media can only be used with bipolar instruments; electrolyte-deficient media can be used with both bipolar and unipolar instruments but risks uterine perforation, water intoxication (nausea, vomiting, headache), and (except with mannitol) hyponatremia/hyposmolar states causing delirium and confusion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is Essure, its mechanism, and contraceptive efficacy timeline?
back: A hysteroscopic tubal ligation device inserted into the intramural/interstitial part of the fallopian tube; it irritates the tube to cause a tissue/inflammatory reaction that blocks it; contraception is not immediately effective, so backup contraception is needed for 3 months, after which an HSG confirms bilateral blockage before backup contraception is stopped; success rate is 99%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What materials make up the Essure device?
back: An outer cylinder of nickel and titanium, with an inner fiber coil that promotes the tissue reaction.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where and when does fertilization occur, and how long do sperm take to reach the site?
back: At the ampulla of the fallopian tube, on day 14 of the cycle; sperm take about 30 minutes to reach the fertilization site.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is capacitation, where does it occur, and how long does it take?
back: The process by which sperm gain the ability to fertilize the ovum; begins in the cervix and is maximal in the fallopian tube; takes about 6-8 hours (average 7 hours); causes no genetic changes, only altered membrane permeability, calcium/bicarbonate influx, and hypermotility.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the sequence of events leading up to fertilization?
back: Capacitation, then binding to the zona pellucida, then the acrosomal reaction (enzymatic penetration of the zona, aided by hyaluronidase), then the cortical reaction (calcium-dependent release of enzymes from the oocyte cortex), then the zona reaction (zona pellucida becomes impermeable to further sperm, preventing polyspermy).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the main sperm receptor on the zona pellucida, and its function?
back: ZP3 (a zona pellucida glycoprotein), the main receptor for sperm binding, which also helps prevent polyspermy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Map the structural transformation of spermatid organelles into mature sperm structures during spermiogenesis.
back: Golgi apparatus becomes the acrosomal cap; the nucleus becomes the head; the mitochondrion becomes the middle piece; the centriole becomes the axial filament/tail. Mature sperm lack endoplasmic reticulum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the Sertoli cell secrete during intrauterine life?
back: AMH (Mullerian-inhibiting substance), inhibin B, and testosterone-binding protein.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Trace the timeline of early embryonic cleavage from fertilization to implantation.
back: 2-celled zygote (20-30 hours after fertilization) -> 4-celled -> 8-celled (still within the zona pellucida) -> 16-celled (day 3) -> morula enters the uterine cavity (day 4) -> morula becomes a blastocyst with zona hatching (day 5) -> implantation as a blastocyst (day 6 after fertilization = day 20 of the cycle).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the two parts of the blastocyst?
back: The inner cell mass (embryoblast) and the trophoblast.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the site of implantation, the implantation window, and when is implantation complete?
back: Upper posterior wall of the uterus; the implantation window (when the uterus is most receptive) is day 20-24 of the cycle (6-10 days after fertilization); implantation is complete by day 10-11 after fertilization (day 24-25 of the cycle).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the endometrial thickness at implantation, and what favors receptivity?
back: 10-12mm; the endometrium develops pinopods and has its highest level of IL-6 at this time.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What three factors are required for implantation, corresponding to apposition, adhesion, and invasion?
back: Apposition: selectin. Adhesion: integrin (most important). Invasion: matrix metalloproteinases.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is Hartmann's/placental sign?
back: Bleeding that occurs at the time of implantation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is pre-implantation genetic diagnosis (PIGD), the best material and timing, and its indications?
back: Genetic testing of an embryo before transfer; best material is the trophectoderm (trophoblast); best time is the day-3 embryo; indications include detecting sickle cell anemia, thalassemia, cystic fibrosis, and aneuploidy - it is not done for sex determination. The embryo is placed in a calcium/magnesium-free medium to soften the zona pellucida for biopsy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the trophoblast differentiate into, 8 days after fertilization?
back: Cytotrophoblast and syncytiotrophoblast.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the cytotrophoblast form, and its function?
back: Forms the chorion (the extravillous cytotrophoblast also helps prevent pregnancy-induced hypertension).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the syncytiotrophoblast form and its role?
back: Forms the chorion frondosum (the fetal side of the placenta) and acts as the 'hormone factory' of the placenta, producing hCG and hPL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the decidua, and how does progesterone influence it?
back: The endometrium after implantation; progesterone favors decidualization. Decidua basalis becomes the maternal side of the placenta.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When do decidua capsularis and parietalis fuse, and what results?
back: At 14-16 weeks, forming a single obliterated uterine cavity.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the Arias-Stella reaction, its cause, and where else is it seen?
back: Hypersecretory endometrial changes on histopathology during implantation, caused by progesterone; also seen in ectopic pregnancy and with OCP use.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What germ layer forms the amnion?
back: Ectoderm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Classify pregnancy signs into presumptive, probable, and positive categories, with examples.
back: Presumptive (felt by the patient): amenorrhea, nausea/vomiting, fatigue, increased urinary frequency, breast changes, quickening, increased pigmentation. Probable (seen by the doctor): Goodell's, Hegar's, Chadwick's, Osiander's, Palmer's, and Piskacek's signs, plus a positive pregnancy test. Positive/definitive (absent in pseudocyesis): fetus seen on USG, hearing fetal heart sounds, palpation of fetal parts, fetal skeleton on X-ray.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe Goodell's sign - what it is and when it appears.
back: Softening of the cervix; the first probable sign to become positive, at 6 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe Hegar's sign - what it is, timing, and exam technique.
back: Softening of the uterine isthmus, seen at 6-10 weeks; on bimanual exam, the vaginal and abdominal fingers can be felt approximating each other through the softened isthmus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe Chadwick's/Jacquemier's sign, Osiander's sign, Palmer's sign, and Piskacek's sign.
back: Chadwick's/Jacquemier's: bluish discoloration of the cervix/vagina/vulva, at 8 weeks. Osiander's: pulsations felt in the lateral vaginal fornix. Palmer's: rhythmic, painless uterine contractions not felt by the patient. Piskacek's: asymmetric/unequal growth of the uterus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When can fetal heart sounds be heard by handheld Doppler versus stethoscope?
back: Doppler: from 10 weeks. Stethoscope: from 20 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is quickening, and its timing in primigravida versus multigravida?
back: The mother's perception of first fetal movement; primigravida 18-20 weeks; multigravida 16-18 weeks (occurs earlier in multigravida, since they recognize the sensation from experience).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is lightening/the 'welcome sign'?
back: Relief of respiratory discomfort late in the third trimester (after 36 weeks) as the fetal head descends into the pelvis and uterine height decreases; before this, at 36 weeks, the uterus reaches the xiphisternum, causing respiratory discomfort.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When do internal and external ballottement occur?
back: Internal ballottement: 16-20 weeks. External ballottement: 20-24 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the intradecidual sign, double decidual sac sign, and double bleb sign on early pregnancy USG.
back: Intradecidual sign: the first USG sign of pregnancy, indicating interstitial implantation. Double decidual sac sign: the gestational sac surrounded by two rings, an inner ring (decidua capsularis) and outer ring (decidua parietalis). Double bleb sign: formed by the yolk sac and amniotic sac, the two 'blebs.'

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: At what uterine height does the uterus sit at 12 weeks, 22 weeks, and immediately after delivery?
back: 12 weeks: at the pubic symphysis. 22 weeks: at the umbilicus. Immediately after delivery: just below the umbilicus (equivalent to ~20 weeks size).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does uterine height change in the days after delivery?
back: No change on day 1; from day 2 onward it decreases by about one fingerbreadth per day (one fingerbreadth below the umbilicus on day 2).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the total duration of pregnancy?
back: 9 months plus 7 days, equivalent to 40 weeks or 280 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: State Naegele's formula for EDD in a regular 28-day cycle, and the exception for LMP in February.
back: EDD = 1st day of LMP + 7 days + 9 months. If the LMP falls in February, add 9 months first, then 7 days (rather than the usual order).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is EDD adjusted for a cycle longer than 28 days?
back: Calculate the approximate EDD by Naegele's formula, find the difference (cycle length - 28 = x), then add x days to the approximate EDD.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is EDD adjusted for a cycle shorter than 28 days?
back: Calculate the approximate EDD by Naegele's formula, find the difference (28 - cycle length = x), then subtract x days from the approximate EDD.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is Naegele's formula not used, and what is the best alternative method?
back: In irregular cycles, if the woman conceived while on OCPs or during lactation, or if she is unsure of her LMP; the best method instead is USG measurement of crown-rump length (CRL).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is EDD calculated for IVF pregnancies (fresh and frozen cycles)?
back: Fresh cycle: date of oocyte retrieval + 266 days. Frozen cycle with day-3 transfer: date of transfer + 263 days. Frozen cycle with day-5 transfer: date of transfer + 261 days.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define preterm, term (with subdivisions), late term, and post-term pregnancy.
back: Preterm: <37 weeks. Term: 37 to 41 weeks 6 days (early term 37-38 weeks 6 days; full term 39-40 weeks 6 days; late term 41 weeks to 41 weeks 6 days). Post-term: >=42 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define gravida and parity.
back: Gravida: total number of times a woman has conceived (past and present pregnancies). Parity: number of pregnancies that reached the period of viability (28 weeks), not including the current pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How are multiple gestations (twins/triplets) counted in gravida/parity versus in 'living children'?
back: Twins/triplets count as 1 pregnancy for gravida and parity purposes, but count individually (e.g., 2 or 3) as living children in the 'L' of GTPAL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define multipara and grand multipara.
back: Multipara: 2 or more previous births. Grand multipara: 5 or more previous births.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the 'c' represent in the Ga Pb+c notation, and what does it include?
back: The number of abortions - pregnancy losses at or before 20 weeks, including spontaneous abortion, MTP, ectopic pregnancy, and molar pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Expand the GTPAL notation.
back: G: gravida. T: number of term deliveries. P: number of preterm deliveries. A: number of abortions. L: number of living children currently.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the normal nuchal translucency measurement, and causes of increased NT?
back: Normal <3mm. Increased NT (>=3mm) is most commonly due to aneuploidy (Down syndrome > Turner syndrome > Trisomy 13/18), also congenital heart defects, and (in twins) an early marker of TTTS; workup includes karyotyping and fetal echo; the differential includes cystic hygroma.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the timing of the standard USG scans in pregnancy.
back: Dating/viability scan: 6-8 weeks. Nuchal translucency scan: 11 weeks to 13 weeks 6 days. Anomaly/target scan (Level 2/TIFFA, mandatory in all pregnancies): 18-20 weeks (extendable to 22 weeks). Growth scan: 32 and 34 weeks. Placental localization: 32 weeks, repeated at 36 weeks. Fetal echo (only if congenital heart disease is suspected): 20-24 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If only one USG can be performed during pregnancy, which should it be, and why?
back: The Level 2/anomaly scan (18-22 weeks), since it provides detailed fetal anatomy to screen for gross congenital anomalies.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which USG parameter is best in each trimester for gestational age?
back: T1: mean sac diameter (MSD)/CRL. T2 (once CRL >=84mm): biparietal diameter (BPD) more than head circumference (HC). T3: femur length (and a combination of all parameters).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the CRL cutoff for use, and the window for most accurate dating?
back: Usable until 13 weeks 6 days (CRL <84mm); most accurate gestational age is determined by CRL measured between 7-9 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the formulas relating CRL and MSD to gestational age, and the smallest measurable CRL?
back: CRL (mm) + 42 = gestational age in days. Mean sac diameter (mm) + 30 = gestational age in days. Smallest measurable CRL is 5mm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the best USG parameter for fetal growth, and its measurement plane?
back: Abdominal circumference (AC); measured in a plane where the fetal stomach, portal sinus, and umbilical vein are all visible together (the 'hockey stick' sign helps identify this plane).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the best clinical and best USG-based methods for estimating fetal weight?
back: Best clinical formula: Johnson's formula. Best USG method: Hadlock or Shepard formula (combining HC, AC, FL, and BPD).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define macrosomia, its risk factors, and USG diagnostic threshold.
back: Fetal weight >=4kg; risk factors include post-term pregnancy, diabetic mother, male fetus, and maternal obesity; diagnosed on USG with AC >=35cm (an alternate source specifies >=350mm).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the recommended mode of delivery for macrosomia, and indications for cesarean section?
back: Vaginal delivery is generally preferred; cesarean section is indicated for a diabetic mother with estimated fetal weight >=4.5kg, or a non-diabetic mother with estimated fetal weight >=5kg; prior cesarean is a relative contraindication to VBAC.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the order of appearance of structures on early pregnancy USG?
back: Gestational sac -> yolk sac -> fetal pole (measurable as CRL) -> cardiac activity.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What distinguishes a true versus pseudo gestational sac, and which parameter best differentiates ectopic from intrauterine pregnancy?
back: A true gestational sac (seen in intrauterine pregnancy) is surrounded by a double decidual ring; a pseudo gestational sac (seen in ectopic pregnancy) lacks this. Serial beta-hCG is the best parameter to differentiate ectopic from intrauterine pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the key hCG and USG cutoffs for visualizing the gestational sac and cardiac activity?
back: hCG needed to see the gestational sac on TVS (discriminatory zone): 2000 IU (an alternate source cites 6500 IU for TAS); minimum hCG at which a gestational sac may be seen: 1000 IU. MSD cutoff to expect a fetal pole: 25mm. CRL cutoff to expect cardiac activity: 7mm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the gestational age at which the gestational sac, yolk sac, fetal pole, and cardiac activity are seen on TVS versus TAS.
back: TVS sees each structure about 1 week earlier than TAS: gestational sac (TVS 4.5-5 weeks vs TAS 5.5-6 weeks), yolk sac (5 vs 6 weeks), fetal pole (5-5.5 vs 6-6.5 weeks), cardiac activity (5.5-6 vs 6.5-7 weeks).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the diagnostic criteria for missed abortion on USG?
back: Either MSD >=25mm with no fetal pole seen (blighted ovum/anembryonic pregnancy), or CRL >=7mm with no cardiac activity; management in either case is MTP. If MSD <25mm with no CRL seen, or CRL <7mm with no cardiac activity, the approach is to wait and repeat USG in 1 week.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe craniorachischisis, and the two categories of neural tube defects it combines.
back: A combined cranial and caudal neural tube defect - the fetus has both anencephaly (cranial) and spina bifida (caudal) together.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish anencephaly from encephalocele.
back: Anencephaly: failure of anterior neuropore closure, with brain tissue exposed with no covering. Encephalocele: herniation of brain tissue through a cranial defect, but covered by scalp, hair, and meninges.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the key features and timing of anencephaly detection?
back: The most common neural tube defect and first anomaly typically detected on USG; can be detected as early as 10 weeks, best diagnosed by 14 weeks; associated with polyhydramnios (due to absent fetal swallowing) and more common in female fetuses; more often leads to post-term than preterm labor. Recurrence risk is 4% after one affected pregnancy, 10% after two.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the screening and diagnostic tests for anencephaly, and the best biochemical marker?
back: Screening: Level 1 USG. Diagnostic: Level 2 USG. Best biochemical marker: acetylcholinesterase levels (most specific); maternal serum AFP is best checked at 16-18 weeks (also raised in NTD).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the classic USG signs of anencephaly?
back: Shower cap sign (irregular head contour from exposed brain tissue), frog eye sign (bulging eyes), and Mickey mouse sign (triangular facial appearance from deep eye sockets).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management for anencephaly once diagnosed?
back: MTP (medical termination of pregnancy).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish meningocele from meningomyelocele, and their USG signs.
back: Meningocele: herniation of the meninges alone. Meningomyelocele: herniation of both the meninges and spinal cord; USG shows the banana sign (downward cerebellar displacement) and lemon sign (frontal bone bossing).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is duodenal atresia's classic feature and USG sign, and its chromosomal association?
back: Presents with defective fetal swallowing (polyhydramnios); associated with Down syndrome; USG shows the 'double bubble' sign.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish omphalocele from gastroschisis.
back: Omphalocele: abdominal contents herniate through the umbilical ring, covered by a sac; associated with aneuploidy, warranting karyotyping. Gastroschisis: abdominal contents herniate without any covering sac; not associated with aneuploidy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is posterior urethral valve associated with, and its USG sign?
back: Associated with oligohydramnios; shows the 'keyhole' sign (dilated posterior urethra with bladder distension) on USG.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Is aneuploidy screening offered to all pregnant women, or only those at high risk?
back: Offered to all pregnant women, regardless of age.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What comprises first-trimester aneuploidy screening?
back: Performed at 11 weeks to 13 weeks 6 days; biochemical: dual test (PAPP-A and hCG, with hCG raised in Down syndrome); USG: nuchal translucency (normal <3mm; raised in Down syndrome > Turner syndrome > Trisomy 18/13, congenital heart disease, TTTS, and syndromes like DiGeorge or Noonan syndrome).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What comprises second-trimester aneuploidy screening biochemical tests?
back: Performed at 16-18 weeks. Triple test (Bart/Kettering test): alpha-fetoprotein, hCG, and unconjugated estriol (E3). Quadruple test: triple test plus inhibin A (raised in Down syndrome).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What USG 'soft markers' are checked in the second trimester for aneuploidy?
back: Nuchal fold thickness >=6mm, absent nasal bone, short femur/humerus, sandal gap deformity, duodenal atresia (double bubble sign), simian crease, echogenic intracardiac focus, echogenic bowel, and choroid plexus cyst.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish the combined test and integrated test for aneuploidy screening.
back: Combined test: dual test plus nuchal translucency (first trimester only). Integrated test: PAPP-A and NT in the first trimester, combined with the quadruple test in the second trimester (higher detection rate, results available later).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is cell-free fetal DNA testing (NIPT), its source, sensitivity, and limitations?
back: A second-tier (best) screening test using cell-free fetal DNA from maternal blood, derived from placental cell apoptosis; can be done any time from 10 weeks onward; highest sensitivity (~99%) among screening tests, but expensive; if negative, no further testing is needed.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the diagnostic test for aneuploidy if screening is positive, and how is tissue obtained by trimester?
back: Karyotyping is diagnostic. First-trimester tissue: chorionic villus sampling (best time 11 weeks to 13 weeks 6 days). Second-trimester: amniocentesis (best time 16-18 weeks).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe chorionic villus sampling - earliest timing, risks if done early, and fetal loss risk.
back: Earliest done at 10 weeks; if performed before 10 weeks, it risks oromandibular and limb defects; overall fetal loss risk is about 1%; placental mosaicism may yield two different cell lines on testing.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe amniocentesis - timing window, risk of early amniocentesis, and fetal loss risk.
back: Performed between 15-20 weeks; early amniocentesis (12-14 weeks) is contraindicated due to fetal loss risk; overall fetal loss risk is about 0.5%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe cordocentesis - source, fetal loss risk, indication, and timing.
back: Blood is obtained from the umbilical vein; fetal loss risk is about 3%; its only real indication is Rh-isoimmunized pregnancy; performed at or after 18 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the key AFP facts - source, testing window, and causes of decreased/increased levels?
back: Synthesized by the fetal yolk sac, liver, and GI tract; tested in maternal serum between 15-20 weeks (best 16-18 weeks). Decreased AFP: diabetic mother, gestational trophoblastic disease, obese mother/overestimated gestational age (mnemonic: 'Diabetic GOAT'). Increased AFP: neural tube defects, abdominal wall defects, threatened abortion, and trisomy - all warranting follow-up USG.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which vaccines are absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy, and why?
back: Mumps, measles, rubella, smallpox, chicken pox, BCG, and HPV vaccine - all live vaccines, and live vaccines are generally contraindicated in pregnancy (with pregnancy also avoided for 1 month after receiving one).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the general principle for vaccine safety in pregnancy, and the exceptions to the 'live vaccines are contraindicated' rule?
back: All killed/dead vaccines (e.g., hepatitis A/B, pneumococcus, meningococcus, rabies, typhoid) are safe. Exceptions where certain live vaccines can still be given if needed: yellow fever, polio, and typhoid (oral live vaccine).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the additional caloric requirement in pregnancy by trimester?
back: National guidelines: T1 - no additional calories; T2 - +300 kcal/day; T3 - +450 kcal/day (an older/alternate source specifies +85, +280, and +470 kcal/day respectively). A separate general estimate suggests +350 kcal in all trimesters.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the recommended total weight gain in pregnancy by pre-pregnancy BMI category?
back: Normal BMI: 11-12.5 kg. Low BMI (thin): 12.5-18 kg. BMI >30 (obese): 7 kg (range 5-9 kg).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the recommended schedule of antenatal visits?
back: Ideal: 12-15 visits total - every 4 weeks until 28 weeks, every 2 weeks from 28-36 weeks, and weekly from 36 weeks onward. Minimum recommended: WHO 8 visits, Government of India 4 visits.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the folic acid dose to prevent NTD in a first pregnancy, and its timing?
back: 400 mcg/day for all pregnant women, started 1 month before conception and continued through 3 months after conception.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the folic acid dose to prevent NTD recurrence, and its timing?
back: 4 mg/day for women with a prior NTD-affected pregnancy, started 3 months before conception (or from when pregnancy is planned) and continued 3 months after conception.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the folic acid dose to treat deficiency, in diabetic pregnant patients, in patients on antiepileptics, and in sickle cell anemia?
back: To treat deficiency: 1 mg/day until corrected. Diabetic pregnant patients: 400 mcg/day. Antiepileptic patients: 400 mcg/day before conception, increasing to 4 mg/day after conception. Sickle cell anemia: 5 mg/day throughout pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the RDA requirements in pregnancy for iodine, calcium, carbohydrate, and fat?
back: Iodine: 250 mcg/day. Calcium: 1000 mg/day (started in the second trimester; keep at least a 2-hour gap between calcium and iron tablets). Carbohydrate: 175 g/day. Fat: 28 g/day.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the additional protein requirement in pregnancy by trimester?
back: Non-pregnant baseline 45 g/day; T1: no additional requirement; T2: +10 g/day; T3: +20 g/day.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the Td vaccine schedule in pregnancy, and when is only a booster given?
back: All pregnant women should receive Td; 1st dose at the first antenatal visit, 2nd dose 4 weeks later; if 2 doses were received within the past 3 years, only a booster dose is needed in the current pregnancy.
