back: HbA1c <6.5%: no additional risk; HbA1c ~6.5%: about 3% risk; HbA1c >9%: about 15% risk (an alternate/older source frames this as: <6.5% no risk, >=10% a 15-20% risk). The best method to reduce malformation risk is strict glycemic control.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What investigations are done in both pregestational and gestational diabetes during antenatal care, and which are done ONLY in pregestational diabetes?
back: Done in both: DIPSI test, NT scan, Level 2 (target/anomaly) USG, urine routine/microculture (for asymptomatic bacteriuria, which is increased in diabetics), PIH screening (BP/dipstick every visit), and antenatal fetal monitoring from 32 weeks (weekly NST and BPP, growth scan every 3 weeks, or at least 2 growth scans if weekly is not feasible). Done ONLY in pregestational diabetes: HbA1c and fetal echocardiography (at 22-24 weeks).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management approach for pregestational diabetes in pregnancy?
back: Medical nutrition therapy (diet modification plus weight counseling), insulin, and low-dose aspirin (started before 16 weeks) to prevent PIH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the stepwise management of gestational diabetes based on response to diet?
back: Start with medical nutrition therapy (diet modification) alone for 2 weeks; recheck 2-hour post-prandial glucose - if <120mg/dL, continue MNT alone (monitoring 2-hour PP every 2 weeks in T2, weekly in T3); if >=120mg/dL, add insulin (initially checking 2-hour PP every third day to titrate the dose, then the same T2/T3 monitoring schedule).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the metabolic glucose targets for a pregnant woman with diabetes on MNT or insulin?
back: Fasting blood sugar <95mg/dL, 1-hour post-prandial <140mg/dL, 2-hour post-prandial <120mg/dL, HbA1c <6% (up to 6.5% acceptable), and average capillary glucose <100mg/dL.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the composition of medical nutrition therapy (MNT) for diabetic pregnancy?
back: Carbohydrate 40%, fat 40% (saturated fat <10%), protein 20%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the timing recommendations for termination of pregnancy in gestational versus pregestational diabetes?
back: Gestational diabetes well-controlled on diet (A1) or well-controlled on insulin/drugs (A2): deliver at 39 weeks (an alternate source rounds this to >=39 weeks). Gestational diabetes A2 not well-controlled on insulin: deliver at 37-38 weeks 6 days. Pregestational diabetes, well-controlled: deliver at >=39 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the drug of choice for diabetes in pregnancy, and key facts about its administration?
back: Insulin (subcutaneous route); most commonly used formulation is human premix insulin (1 vial = 40 IU/mL); a syringe can be reused up to 14 times; stored at 4-8 degrees C. Insulin dose requirement increases as pregnancy advances but decreases during labor (due to fasting/nil-by-mouth status).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Are oral hypoglycemic agents (OHAs) safe in pregnancy, and what are the exceptions?
back: OHAs are generally contraindicated as they cross the placenta and can cause fetal hypoglycemia; exceptions considered relatively safe are metformin (indicated for non-compliance with insulin) and glyburide - both are used only in gestational diabetes if the patient refuses insulin, never in pregestational diabetics.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is glycosuria's significance in pregnancy?
back: It is physiological during pregnancy (due to increased GFR) but also serves as an indication to screen for diabetes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the indications for umbilical artery Doppler in a diabetic pregnancy?
back: Diabetics with vasculopathy, and diabetics with coexisting PIH; not routinely needed otherwise.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the tests for assessing fetal lung maturity, and when is surfactant produced?
back: Phosphatidylglycerol level is the best test; a lecithin/sphingomyelin (L/S) ratio >=2:1 indicates mature lungs. Surfactant is produced by type II pneumocytes starting at 20 weeks, and becomes detectable in amniotic fluid by 28 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What maternal complications result from diabetic pregnancy via placentomegaly and polyhydramnios?
back: Placentomegaly can cause PIH and polyhydramnios; polyhydramnios can in turn cause PPH, abruptio placenta, PROM, preterm labor, cord prolapse, and malpresentation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What fetal and neonatal complications occur in diabetic pregnancy?
back: Fetal: hyperglycemia driving fetal hyperinsulinemia, causing macrosomia (most common) and shoulder dystocia, plus increased chances of abortion, IUD, and stillbirth; if diabetic vasculopathy or coexisting PIH is present, oligohydramnios and IUGR can occur instead. Neonatal: hypoglycemia, hypocalcemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, respiratory distress syndrome (due to reduced surfactant), necrotizing enterocolitis, and hyperviscosity syndrome (with hyperbilirubinemia and polycythemia). Anemia and mental retardation are notably NEVER seen in babies of diabetic mothers.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is macrosomia's definition, best diagnostic USG parameter, and cesarean indications, including the VBAC caveat?
back: Birth weight >=4kg; best USG parameter is abdominal circumference >=35cm; vaginal delivery is generally preferred; cesarean is indicated if a diabetic mother's fetus is estimated at >=4.5kg, or a non-diabetic mother's fetus at >=5kg; VBAC is contraindicated if the current pregnancy has a macrosomic baby and there is a prior cesarean section history.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define shoulder dystocia and its classic sign.
back: Delay of >=1 minute in delivering the fetal shoulders after delivery of the head; an obstetric emergency; the classic sign is the 'turtle sign' (the fetal head retracts back against the perineum).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the HELPERR mnemonic for managing shoulder dystocia.
back: H: call for Help. E: give a liberal Episiotomy (and empty the bladder). L: Legs/McRoberts maneuver (flex and abduct the thighs to straighten the sacrum and increase pelvic space). P: suprapubic Pressure (fundal pressure is contraindicated). E: Enter the hand to rotate the fetal shoulders (Woods corkscrew/Rubin II maneuver). R: Remove the posterior arm (Jacquemier maneuver). R: Roll the patient onto all fours (Gaskin/all-fours maneuver). If all measures fail, the last resort is the Zavanelli maneuver (pushing the head back into the uterus, followed by cesarean section).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common nerve injured during the McRoberts maneuver, and the most common fetal and maternal complications of shoulder dystocia?
back: Most commonly injured nerve: lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh. Most common fetal complication: Erb's palsy (C5-C6 nerve roots, causing a 'waiter's tip'/porter's tip hand deformity). Most common maternal complication: postpartum hemorrhage (PPH).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Is OGTT repeated after delivery in women with gestational diabetes?
back: Yes - OGTT is done in all women with GDM at 6 weeks after delivery, to check for resolution versus persistence of glucose intolerance.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the pathophysiology of Rh alloimmunization?
back: When an Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-positive fetus and fetal blood enters the maternal circulation, the fetal Rh antigen stimulates the maternal immune system to form IgG antibodies against it; these IgG antibodies can cross the placenta and cause fetal hemolysis, fetal anemia, hyperbilirubinemia, hepatosplenomegaly, placentomegaly, PIH, and polyhydramnios.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the initial workup algorithm for an Rh-negative woman at her first antenatal visit?
back: First, check the husband's ABO/Rh status - if he is Rh-positive, there is a 50% chance the fetus will be Rh-positive, making this a high-risk pregnancy. Next, perform an indirect Coombs test (ICT); if negative, this is a non-isoimmunized pregnancy (ICT is repeated at 28 weeks); if positive (at first visit or at 28 weeks), this is an isoimmunized pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the antepartum prophylaxis protocol for Rh non-isoimmunized pregnancy?
back: Given to an Rh-negative pregnant woman if ICT is negative at 28 weeks; dose 300mcg (1500 IU) IM; aims to protect the current pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the prerequisites and timing for postpartum anti-D prophylaxis?
back: Given if the baby is confirmed Rh-positive and the direct Coombs test (DCT) is negative; ideally given within 72 hours of delivery, and can be given up to 28 days postpartum (maximum); dose 300mcg IM; aims to protect future pregnancies.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the other (non-postpartum) indications for anti-D administration, and doses by trimester?
back: First trimester events (dose 50mcg): abortion, ectopic pregnancy, molar pregnancy, MTP, chorionic villus sampling, trauma. Second/third trimester events (dose 300mcg): APH, amniocentesis, external cephalic version (ECV), manual removal of placenta, fetal death. Cordocentesis is NOT an indication for anti-D.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the contraindications to giving anti-D?
back: An Rh-negative husband (implying the fetus is also Rh-negative), or a positive ICT/DCT (indicating the woman is already sensitized).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is cord clamping timing decided in Rh-negative pregnancy based on ICT status?
back: If ICT is positive (indicating fetal anemia risk), delayed cord clamping (1-3 minutes) is done; if ICT is negative, early cord clamping (<1 minute) is done.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management pathway for Rh isoimmunized pregnancy based on antibody titre?
back: Titre <1:16 (i.e., 1:2, 1:4, 1:8): repeat the titre every 4 weeks, begin fetal monitoring at 32 weeks, and deliver at 37-38 weeks (methylergometrine is contraindicated, and delayed cord clamping is done). Titre >=1:16 (the critical titre): proceed to check MCA Doppler peak systolic velocity (PSV).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the significance of MCA Doppler PSV in Rh isoimmunized pregnancy, and the next steps at each result?
back: PSV >=1.5 MoM indicates fetal anemia - proceed to cordocentesis (percutaneous umbilical blood sampling) to determine exact hematocrit, which is the only indication for this procedure in Rh disease. PSV <1.5 MoM (anemia absent): repeat MCA Doppler every 1-2 weeks, begin fetal monitoring at 32 weeks, and deliver at 37-38 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Based on cordocentesis-derived fetal hematocrit, what is the further management?
back: Hematocrit >=30%: repeat Hb/hematocrit weekly, repeat MCA Doppler every 1-2 weeks, begin fetal monitoring at 32 weeks, deliver at 37-38 weeks. Hematocrit <30%: if gestational age >=35 weeks, proceed to delivery; if <35 weeks, perform intrauterine blood transfusion instead.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the recommended mode of delivery in Rh-negative pregnancy?
back: Vaginal delivery is preferred.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the Kleihauer-Betke test - alternate name, principle, reagent, and clinical use.
back: Also known as the acid elution test; based on the principle that fetal hemoglobin (HbF) is resistant to acid/alkali elution (unlike adult Hb); uses citric acid phosphate buffer as the reagent; used to quantify the volume of fetomaternal hemorrhage in order to calculate the required anti-D dose.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How much fetomaternal hemorrhage does a standard 300mcg dose of anti-D neutralize?
back: 30mL of fetomaternal hemorrhage, equivalent to about 15mL of fetal RBCs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the key facts about the Rh antigen and D antigen?
back: The Rh (D) antigen first appears on fetal RBCs by day 38 after fertilization; the D antigen gene is located on chromosome 1 and is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the USG diagnostic findings of hydrops fetalis, and the threshold at which it develops?
back: Diagnostic findings (any >=2 required): pleural effusion, pericardial effusion, ascites, and skin edema (Buddha sign - subcutaneous scalp edema); placentomegaly and polyhydramnios may also be seen but are NOT part of the core diagnostic criteria. Hydrops develops due to fetal heart failure when hemoglobin falls below 5g% and hematocrit falls below 15%.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the immune versus non-immune causes of hydrops fetalis?
back: Immune causes, in order of frequency: Rh incompatibility (most common) > ABO incompatibility. Non-immune causes: cardiovascular disease (most common), anemia, chromosomal disorders, parvovirus infection, syphilis, and TORCH infections. Infections that do NOT cause hydrops include herpes, hepatitis, and HIV.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the classic USG signs of intrauterine fetal death (IUFD)?
back: Robert's sign (gas within the great vessels - the earliest sign), Spalding sign (overlapping of the fetal skull bones), and Ball sign (hyperflexion or hyperextension of the fetal spine).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define chronic hypertension in pregnancy versus pregnancy-induced hypertension (PIH), by onset and postpartum resolution.
back: Chronic HTN: hypertension present or diagnosed before 20 weeks of pregnancy, without proteinuria or end-organ damage, and BP does NOT normalize even 12 weeks after delivery. PIH: a previously normotensive patient develops elevated BP after 20 weeks of pregnancy, with BP returning to normal within 12 weeks postpartum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish gestational hypertension from pre-eclampsia, and chronic hypertension with superimposed pre-eclampsia.
back: Gestational hypertension: PIH without proteinuria or signs of end-organ damage. Pre-eclampsia: PIH WITH proteinuria or signs of end-organ damage. Chronic hypertension with superimposed pre-eclampsia: a chronic hypertensive patient who develops new-onset proteinuria, end-organ damage, or uncontrolled BP at or after 20 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is proteinuria defined for diagnosing pre-eclampsia, and what are the screening versus gold-standard tests?
back: Any one of: urine protein excretion >=300mg in 24 hours (gold standard/diagnostic test), protein:creatinine ratio >=0.3, or urine dipstick >=1+ to 2+ (screening test).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What defines signs of end-organ damage in pre-eclampsia?
back: Any one of: platelet count <=1 lakh (100,000), liver enzymes raised to >=2 times normal, serum creatinine >=1.1mg/dL, pulmonary edema, or cerebral/visual symptoms (severe headache, visual disturbances).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish mild from severe pre-eclampsia by BP and clinical criteria.
back: Mild: BP >=140/90 but <160/110 mmHg, no signs of end-organ damage, no symptoms of impending eclampsia. Severe: BP >=160/110 mmHg, OR signs of end-organ damage present, OR symptoms of impending eclampsia present.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the symptoms and signs of impending eclampsia?
back: Severe headache, epigastric pain, visual disturbances, and clonus; fundal examination may show a silver-wire appearance (hypertensive retinopathy).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which three clinical findings do NOT help differentiate mild from severe pre-eclampsia?
back: Proteinuria, oliguria, and IUGR (all can be present in either severity).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is severely elevated BP (>=160/110) confirmed before starting antihypertensives?
back: Recheck BP after 15 minutes; if it remains >=160/110mmHg, start antihypertensive treatment.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define eclampsia, and its three timing-based types.
back: Occurrence of generalized tonic-clonic seizures in a patient with severe pre-eclampsia. Antepartum eclampsia (most common, worst prognosis), intrapartum eclampsia (during labor), and postpartum eclampsia (within 48 hours of delivery).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What causes eclamptic seizures, and what is the most common MRI finding?
back: Cerebral hypoxia and edema; the most common MRI finding is PRES (posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome - edema in the posterior cerebral hemispheres).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the risk factors for PIH by category.
back: First exposure to placenta: primigravida, new paternity, long interpregnancy interval. Increased placental tissue/big placenta: twin pregnancy, diabetes, Rh-negative pregnancy, molar pregnancy, hydrops fetalis. Autoimmune disease: APLA syndrome. Patient factors: age <18 or >=40 years, obesity, calcium deficiency. Previous history: IVF pregnancy, chronic hypertension, prior PIH/pre-eclampsia. Smoking is notably a PROTECTIVE factor against PIH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which women should receive prophylactic low-dose aspirin to prevent PIH, per ACOG (mnemonic)?
back: Mnemonic 'All Hypertensive Mothers Can Die': APLA syndrome, chronic Hypertension, Multifetal pregnancy, kidney disease, and (pregestational) Diabetes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the dose and timing of prophylactic aspirin for PIH prevention?
back: 75-150mg/day (commonly cited as 75mg once daily), started as early as possible and by 12-16 weeks at the latest, continued until 36 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which interventions have proven versus no proven role in preventing PIH?
back: Proven role: low-dose aspirin, calcium supplementation (only if the woman is calcium-deficient), and pre-pregnancy weight loss. No proven role: absolute bed rest, salt restriction, antioxidants, fish oil, and vitamin E.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the pathophysiology of pre-eclampsia, starting from trophoblastic invasion.
back: Normally, extravillous/endovascular cytotrophoblast invades and replaces the endothelial lining of the myometrial segment of the spiral arteries (aided by maternal natural killer cells); in pre-eclampsia, this trophoblastic invasion is incomplete, causing placental ischemia, which triggers release of vasoconstrictors and reduced vasodilators into the maternal circulation, leading to increased BP, hemoconcentration, endothelial damage, thrombosis, and end-organ damage.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which vasoconstrictor and vasodilator markers are increased versus decreased in PIH?
back: Increased (vasoconstrictors): sFlt-1 (soluble fms-like tyrosine kinase), soluble endoglin, TNF-alpha, thromboxane A2, and increased vascular sensitivity to angiotensin II. Decreased (vasodilators): VEGF, placental growth factor, prostacyclin I2, nitric oxide, and angiotensinase enzyme activity.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the first sign of pre-eclampsia, and the first organ affected?
back: First sign: elevated blood pressure. First organ affected: the kidney (histopathology shows glomeruloendotheliosis).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What classification system grades hypertensive retinopathy in pregnancy?
back: Keith-Wagner classification.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What investigations should be done in all patients admitted with pre-eclampsia?
back: Platelet count, liver function tests, serum creatinine, and fundal (retinal) examination.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the outpatient management of mild pre-eclampsia?
back: Twice-weekly BP monitoring, fetal monitoring from 32 weeks, patient education about signs of impending eclampsia; no proven role for antihypertensives, aspirin, bed rest, salt restriction, or MgSO4; definitive management is termination of pregnancy (TOP) at 37 weeks by vaginal delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the inpatient management of severe pre-eclampsia?
back: First line: MgSO4 (to prevent seizures/eclampsia); second line: IV antihypertensives; corticosteroids given if gestational age is 28-34 weeks (first dose); definitive management is TOP, timing depending on presence of severe complicating features.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When is immediate termination of pregnancy indicated in severe pre-eclampsia, without waiting for a second dose of corticosteroids?
back: In the presence of impending eclampsia, established eclampsia, HELLP syndrome, abruptio placenta, fetal distress, DIC, or pulmonary edema (a related risk-factor mnemonic 'PROM' covers PROM, renal dysfunction, oligohydramnios, and absent/reversed umbilical artery end-diastolic flow, which also warrant immediate TOP consideration).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In an uncomplicated severe pre-eclampsia pregnancy without immediate danger signs, what is the TOP timing?
back: TOP at 34 weeks, after completing the corticosteroid course (giving the second dose before induction of labor).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management sequence for a patient having an eclamptic (convulsive) seizure?
back: Step 1: prevent the patient from falling/injury and secure the airway (raise bed rails). Step 2: give MgSO4 to control convulsions. Step 3: give IV antihypertensives. Definitive management: immediate termination of pregnancy, irrespective of gestational age; preferred mode of delivery is vaginal, with general anesthesia preferred over neuraxial/epidural if seizures are ongoing (neuraxial is preferred over epidural if seizures are controlled).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the indications and target BP for starting antihypertensive therapy in pregnancy?
back: Indicated if BP >=160/110mmHg (even on a single occasion), or per NICE guidelines, if BP is persistently >=150/100mmHg; target BP is systolic 120-130mmHg and diastolic 80-90mmHg.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which antihypertensives are used for severe pre-eclampsia versus chronic hypertension in pregnancy?
back: Severe pre-eclampsia (1st line): IV labetalol (maximum 220mg), IV hydralazine (maximum 30mg), oral nifedipine. Chronic hypertension (1st line, oral): labetalol, nifedipine, alpha-methyldopa (safest, but slow-acting, so not first-line for acute severe pre-eclampsia).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which antihypertensives are absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy, and which drug is reserved as a last resort?
back: Contraindicated: ACE inhibitors, ARBs (e.g., losartan), and diazoxide; diuretics are not absolutely contraindicated (avoided in pre-eclampsia, but usable in chronic hypertension before 20 weeks). Last resort: sodium nitroprusside, which carries the risk of cyanide poisoning as a side effect.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In which conditions is labetalol contraindicated?
back: Asthma and heart failure.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is MgSO4's mechanism, indication, and therapeutic range?
back: A centrally acting drug that blocks NMDA receptors; it is NOT an antihypertensive; drug of choice for prevention and treatment of eclamptic seizures; therapeutic range is 4-7 mEq/L.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the Pritchard regimen for MgSO4 administration.
back: Loading dose: IM 10g of 50% MgSO4 (5g in each buttock, using 10 ampoules) plus IV 4g of 20% MgSO4 (4 ampoules) - no renal function test is required before the loading dose. Maintenance dose: IM 5g of 50% MgSO4 every 4 hours in alternating buttocks, continued for 24 hours after the last seizure or 24 hours after delivery, whichever is later.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the Sibai (IV-only) MgSO4 regimen.
back: Recommended by ACOG; loading dose 4g IV MgSO4, followed by a maintenance dose of 2g/hour IV.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What parameters must be checked before each maintenance dose of MgSO4?
back: Deep tendon reflexes (knee jerk) must be present, respiratory rate >=12 breaths/min, and urine output >=100mL per 4 hours (or >=30mL/hour).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the signs of MgSO4 toxicity at increasing serum levels, and the antidote?
back: At ~10 mEq/L: loss of knee jerk (earliest sign), diaphoresis, and slurred speech. At ~12 mEq/L: respiratory paralysis/arrest. At ~15 mEq/L: cardiac conduction defects/arrhythmias. At ~20-24 mEq/L: cardiac arrest. Oliguria is NOT a sign of MgSO4 toxicity, and serum magnesium levels are not routinely monitored. Antidote: calcium gluconate.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Besides seizure prevention, what other beneficial effects does MgSO4 have in pregnancy?
back: Neuroprotective agent for babies born before 32 weeks (reduces risk of cerebral palsy); decreases the incidence of abruptio placenta; has a tocolytic action (though this occurs near toxic concentrations, so it is not used clinically as a primary tocolytic); it typically causes decreased fetal heart rate variability on CTG.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the choice of anesthesia for cesarean section in a pre-eclamptic/eclamptic patient?
back: If seizures are controlled: neuraxial anesthesia is preferred over epidural. If seizures are ongoing (active eclampsia): general anesthesia is used.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does uterine artery Doppler assess, when is it performed, and what are its two key measured parameters by timing?
back: Predicts risk of PIH. Between 11-13 weeks: pulsatility index (PI) is assessed; an increased PI predicts risk of early-onset pre-eclampsia. Between 22-24 weeks: the diastolic notch is assessed; the notch normally disappears by 24 weeks; persistence of the diastolic notch beyond 24 weeks indicates increased risk of PIH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does umbilical artery Doppler measure, and what is the significance of the S/D ratio, absent, and reversed end-diastolic flow?
back: Assesses fetoplacental circulation via the systolic/diastolic (S/D) ratio; normally the S/D ratio decreases as pregnancy advances (blood flow in diastole increases). S/D ratio >=3 indicates mild PIH/placental insufficiency (manage with TOP at 37 weeks). Absent end-diastolic flow (AEDF): TOP at 34 weeks (or earlier if <34 weeks, after Doppler/NST/BPP surveillance); mode of delivery can be vaginal. Reversed end-diastolic flow (REDF): TOP at 31-32 weeks; mode of delivery is cesarean section; if REDF coexists with late decelerations on CTG, immediate TOP is indicated.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does MCA (middle cerebral artery) Doppler assess, and how is it interpreted?
back: Measures peak systolic velocity (PSV) to detect fetal anemia; PSV >=1.5 MoM indicates fetal anemia, while PSV <0.8 MoM indicates fetal polycythemia. Indications include TTTS, Rh incompatibility, and vasa previa.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does reversal of flow in the ductus venosus indicate?
back: A critical finding requiring immediate termination of pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is done if pregnancy is less than the target gestational age when abnormal Doppler flow (AEDF/REDF) is detected?
back: Continue surveillance with repeat Doppler, NST, and BPP until reaching the appropriate gestational age for termination (34 weeks for AEDF, 31-32 weeks for REDF).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define IUGR/FGR, and distinguish it from small for gestational age (SGA).
back: Estimated fetal weight (EFW) or abdominal circumference (AC) below the 10th percentile for gestational age. SGA: EFW/AC <10th percentile with NORMAL Doppler and normal growth velocity (constitutionally small). IUGR/FGR: EFW/AC <10th percentile WITH abnormal Doppler and/or abnormal growth velocity (pathological growth restriction).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Classify IUGR severity by percentile, and by gestational age of onset.
back: By severity: moderate IUGR is 3rd-10th percentile; severe IUGR is below the 3rd percentile. By onset: early-onset IUGR is diagnosed before 32 weeks; late-onset IUGR is diagnosed at or after 32 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the diagnostic criteria for early-onset IUGR?
back: EFW or AC below the 3rd percentile, OR EFW/AC between the 3rd-10th percentile plus at least one of: absent/reversed umbilical artery end-diastolic flow, or uterine artery pulsatility index >95th percentile.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the diagnostic criteria for late-onset IUGR?
back: Any two of the following: EFW <10th percentile, AC <10th percentile, low growth velocity (growth crossing more than 2 quartiles for gestational age), or abnormal Doppler (cerebroplacental ratio <5th percentile, MCA pulsatility index <5th percentile due to the brain-sparing effect, or umbilical artery PI >95th percentile). Uterine artery Doppler may be normal in late-onset IUGR.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the clinical (bedside) diagnostic clues to IUGR?
back: Fundal height less than gestational age, or symphysiofundal height lagging behind gestational age by more than 3-4cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the fetal, placental, and maternal causes of IUGR, and which pattern each tends to cause?
back: Fetal causes (tend to cause early-onset/symmetrical IUGR): gross congenital anomalies, chromosomal abnormalities, infections, and twin pregnancy. Placental causes: pre-eclampsia, circumvallate placenta, velamentous cord insertion, single umbilical artery. Maternal causes (tend to cause late-onset/asymmetrical IUGR): poor weight gain, chronic disorders (anemia, heart disease, SLE), and smoking.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the follow-up/surveillance protocol for a fetus diagnosed with IUGR?
back: USG for EFW/AC every 4 weeks (every 2 weeks if severe, <3rd percentile). Doppler: repeat weekly if normal, twice weekly if abnormal, thrice weekly if AEDF/REDF present. Antenatal fetal monitoring (NST, BPP) ideally starts at 32 weeks (earliest at 28 weeks) - NST every 6-12 hours and BPP daily if surveillance is intensified. Umbilical artery Doppler is the best surveillance parameter overall.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the termination of pregnancy timing for IUGR based on Doppler findings?
back: Normal Doppler with EFW <3rd percentile: 37 weeks; with EFW >3rd percentile: 39 weeks. Uterine artery Doppler PI >95th percentile: 37 weeks. IUGR with associated oligohydramnios: 34 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Expand the HELLP syndrome acronym, and state its Tennessee diagnostic criteria - are all three required?
back: Hemolysis (microangiopathic), Elevated Liver enzymes, and Low Platelet count - all three must be present for diagnosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the Tennessee criteria to confirm hemolysis in HELLP syndrome?
back: At least 2 of the following: schistocytes/burr cells on peripheral smear, serum bilirubin >=1.2mg/dL, raised LDH, decreased haptoglobin, or severe anemia unrelated to blood loss.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What defines elevated liver enzymes and low platelet count in HELLP syndrome?
back: Elevated liver enzymes: SGOT/SGPT raised to at least 2 times the normal value (normal ~30-35 IU). Low platelet count: below 1 lakh (100,000)/mm3.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the recurrence rate of HELLP syndrome, and its association with severe pre-eclampsia?
back: Recurrence rate 4-7%; it is a complication of severe pre-eclampsia, with about 80% of HELLP patients having elevated blood pressure (though BP itself is not a diagnostic criterion for HELLP).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Is HELLP syndrome more common in primigravida or multigravida, in contrast to PIH?
back: PIH is most common in primigravida, whereas HELLP syndrome is most common in multigravida.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of HELLP syndrome?
back: Initial: MgSO4 (to prevent seizures) plus antihypertensive drugs. Definitive: if gestational age is <34 weeks, give the first dose of corticosteroids, then proceed to immediate termination of pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish dizygotic from monozygotic twins by origin, incidence, and key features.
back: Dizygotic twins: arise from 2 separate ova fertilized by 2 sperm in the same act; account for about two-thirds of twins; incidence varies geographically; sex may be same or different; always dichorionic diamniotic (DCDA). Monozygotic twins: arise from a single fertilized ovum that later divides; account for about one-third of twins (constant incidence worldwide, ~1 in 250 pregnancies); always the same sex, karyotype, HLA type, and blood group, but with different fingerprints.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What factors increase the incidence of dizygotic twinning?
back: Geographic/ethnic distribution, maternal family history of twinning, increased maternal age and parity, and use of ovulation induction drugs (clomiphene citrate: 5-10% chance of twins; hMG: up to 30% chance of multiple pregnancy) or IVF.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does the timing of embryonic division determine chorionicity/amnionicity in monozygotic twins?
back: Division at <=4 days (morula stage): dichorionic diamniotic (DCDA). Division between 4-8 days (blastocyst stage): monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA, most common monozygotic type). Division between 9-12 days (embryonic disk stage): monochorionic monoamniotic (MCMA). Division after 12 days: conjoined twins.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the most and least common types of conjoined twins?
back: Most common: parapagus (joined at the lower abdomen/pelvis), followed by thoracopagus. Least common: rachipagus (joined at the vertebral column), followed by craniopagus. All conjoined twins are MCMA.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare DCDA, MCDA, and MCMA twins by membrane layers, thickness, and vascular connections.
back: DCDA: 4 membrane layers, thickness >=2mm, no vascular connections between twins, 2 separate placentas (or fused appearing as one). MCDA: 2 membrane layers, thickness <2mm, deep vascular connections present, single placenta. MCMA: 0 membrane layers (no dividing membrane), single placenta, superficial vascular connections.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation of choice for determining chorionicity, and the ideal timing?
back: TVS (transvaginal USG), best performed at 10-14 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the USG signs of dichorionicity versus monochorionicity?
back: Dichorionic: twin peak sign/lambda sign positive (placental tissue extending into the intertwin membrane base), 4 membrane layers, membrane thickness >=2mm. Monochorionic diamniotic: T-sign (membrane meets the placenta at a right angle, no lambda sign), 2 membrane layers. Monochorionic monoamniotic: no dividing membrane at all.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank the overall prognosis of DCDA, MCDA, and MCMA twin pregnancies.
back: DCDA has the best prognosis; monochorionic twins (MCDA and especially MCMA) have poorer prognosis, largely due to vascular anastomoses and shared complications.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What complications are specific to monochorionic twins, versus specific to monoamniotic twins?
back: Monochorionic-specific: twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS), twin anemia-polycythemia sequence (TAPS), twin reversed arterial perfusion (TRAP), and selective IUGR. Monoamniotic-specific: cord entanglement and conjoined twins.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common fetal complication of twin pregnancy overall, and which complication is relatively more common in dizygotic than monozygotic twins?
back: Most common fetal complication overall: IUGR (prematurity/preterm labor is also very common). The complication relatively MORE common in dizygotic twins is cumulative risk of fetal anomaly (each fetus carries independent anomaly risk); all other complications are generally more common in monozygotic twins.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the recommended timing and mode of delivery for DCDA, MCDA, and MCMA twins?
back: DCDA: deliver at 38 weeks, vaginal delivery possible if the first twin is cephalic. MCDA: deliver at 36 weeks (range 34-37), vaginal delivery possible if the first twin is cephalic. MCMA and conjoined twins: deliver at 32-34 weeks by cesarean section (after corticosteroid administration), due to risk of cord entanglement.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is internal podalic version (IPV) in twin delivery, its only indication, and its contraindication?
back: A procedure converting the second twin from a transverse lie to breech for delivery; its only indication is when the first twin is cephalic and the second twin is in transverse lie; performed in the operating theater under general anesthesia; contraindicated in a patient with a previous history of cesarean section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Is VBAC contraindicated in twin pregnancy?
back: No - VBAC is not contraindicated in twins and can be attempted even with a prior cesarean section, EXCEPT if the second twin is in transverse lie in a patient with prior cesarean section (since IPV, which would otherwise be used, is itself contraindicated after a prior cesarean).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the delivery approach based on the presentation of both twins?
back: Both cephalic: vaginal delivery for both (do not give methylergometrine after delivery of the first twin). First cephalic, second breech: vaginal delivery for the first twin, followed by assisted breech delivery for the second. First cephalic, second transverse lie: vaginal delivery for the first twin, followed by internal podalic version (converting to breech) and breech extraction for the second twin.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What special considerations apply to active management of the third stage of labor and cervical cerclage in twin pregnancy?
back: Injection methylergometrine is contraindicated in twin delivery (oxytocin is used instead); prophylactic cervical cerclage is contraindicated in twin pregnancy, and progesterone has not been shown to be useful for preventing preterm labor in twins.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is Hellin's rule for estimating the incidence of higher-order multiple pregnancies?
back: If the incidence of twins in a population is 1 in N pregnancies, the incidence of triplets is approximately 1 in N-squared, and quadruplets approximately 1 in N-cubed.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish superfecundation from superfetation.
back: Superfecundation: two ova released in the same cycle, fertilized at different times by two different acts of intercourse; rare but can occur in humans. Superfetation: two ova released and fertilized in two different menstrual cycles; not seen in humans (theoretically possible only until the uterine cavity is obliterated, at 14-16 weeks), because increased estrogen/progesterone suppress FSH/LH and prevent further ovulation once pregnant.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What causes twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS), and what are its USG diagnostic criteria?
back: Caused by unbalanced deep arteriovenous vascular anastomoses within a shared (monochorionic) placenta. USG criteria: MCDA twins, with the donor twin showing oligohydramnios and the recipient twin showing polyhydramnios.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which type of monochorionic twinning most and least commonly develops TTTS?
back: Most common: MCDA. Least common: MCMA.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What complications develop in the donor versus recipient twin in TTTS?
back: Donor twin (decreased renal blood flow/GFR): oligohydramnios, anemia, renal failure, growth restriction, and heart failure with hydrops. Recipient twin (increased renal blood flow/GFR): polyhydramnios, polycythemia, thrombosis, and congestive heart failure (most common complication, requiring fetal echo) with hydrops.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the Quintero staging system for TTTS.
back: Stage 1: donor twin's bladder is still visible on USG (mild oligohydramnios). Stage 2: donor twin's bladder is no longer visible (anuria). Stage 3: abnormal Doppler studies (e.g., donor twin MCA-PSV >=1.5 MoM, recipient twin <=0.8 MoM). Stage 4: hydrops fetalis in one or both twins. Stage 5: death of one or both twins. Overall prognosis of TTTS depends on the status of the recipient twin.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of TTTS based on gestational age?
back: Up to 28 weeks: fetoscopic laser ablation of the vascular anastomoses. Beyond 28 weeks: serial amnioreduction of the larger (polyhydramniotic) twin's sac.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is twin anemia-polycythemia sequence (TAPS)?
back: A variant of TTTS caused by small-caliber vascular anastomoses, where one twin develops anemia (without oligohydramnios) and the other develops polycythemia (without polyhydramnios) - the hemodynamic imbalance occurs without the fluid volume imbalance seen in classic TTTS.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the pathophysiology of twin reversed arterial perfusion (TRAP) sequence.
back: A rare complication where an aberrant artery-to-artery anastomosis allows the structurally normal 'pump' (donor) twin to perfuse deoxygenated blood in a reversed direction through the umbilical artery of an acardiac 'recipient' twin, which lacks a functioning heart.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the two possible outcomes for the acardiac twin in TRAP sequence?
back: Acardiac acephalus: only the lower body/extremities develop, with no head. Acardiac amorphous: a completely undifferentiated mass with no identifiable body parts.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Classify preterm labor by gestational age, and list its risk factors.
back: Early preterm labor: <34 weeks. Late preterm labor: >=34 weeks. Risk factors: previous history of preterm birth, uterine overdistension (twins, polyhydramnios), infection (asymptomatic bacteriuria, bacterial vaginosis), and short cervical length (<25mm).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In which two clinical scenarios can preterm labor potentially be prevented, and how?
back: Short cervix (<=2.5cm) and previous history of preterm labor - both are managed with progesterone (started at 12 weeks, stopped at 36 weeks) if either condition, or a history of T2 abortion, or cervical cerclage placement, is present.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the criteria for placing a cervical cerclage to prevent preterm labor?
back: All three must be fulfilled: singleton pregnancy, history of preterm labor/T2 abortion, and TVS-confirmed cervical length <=2.5cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is ACOG's recommendation for screening women at risk of preterm labor, and what TVS findings predict it?
back: Women with a previous history of preterm labor should have TVS for cervical length done around 16-24 weeks (TVS is the investigation of choice); findings predicting preterm labor are cervical length <2.5cm and a U-shaped cervix. Elevated fetal fibronectin (>=50 ng/mL) in cervicovaginal secretions is another predictor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What defines 'established' preterm labor by uterine contraction frequency and cervical dilatation?
back: Uterine contractions at <37 weeks meeting ACOG criteria (4 in 20 minutes or 8 in 60 minutes) or FOGSI criteria (1 in 10 minutes or 6 in 60 minutes), combined with cervical dilatation >4cm (dilatation of 2-4cm is termed 'suspected' PTL, managed based on cervical length; dilatation >4cm is 'established' PTL, managed regardless of cervical length).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of established preterm labor at <34 weeks versus >=34 weeks?
back: <34 weeks: corticosteroids, tocolytics (for 48 hours), and MgSO4 if <32 weeks; no role for antibiotics or induction of labor. >=34 weeks: corticosteroids only; no role for tocolytics, MgSO4, progesterone, antibiotics, or induction of labor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the corticosteroid regimens used for fetal lung maturity in preterm labor.
back: Betamethasone (overall drug of choice): 2 injections of 12mg each, 24 hours apart, IM. Dexamethasone (most commonly used in India): 4 injections of 6mg each, 12 hours apart, IM. Contraindicated in chorioamnionitis; steroid action begins 48 hours after the first dose.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the role and duration of tocolytics in preterm labor, and the ACOG-recommended agents by gestational age?
back: Used to buy time for corticosteroids to act, given for a maximum of 48 hours, and never given beyond 34 weeks. Up to 32 weeks: indomethacin. 32-34 weeks: nifedipine (tocolytic of choice in India). Atosiban (an oxytocin antagonist) is the tocolytic of choice in women with heart disease.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the side effects of indomethacin as a tocolytic?
back: Premature closure of the ductus arteriosus (if given beyond 32 weeks) and oligohydramnios.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Why is MgSO4 not typically used as a primary tocolytic, despite having tocolytic action?
back: Its tocolytic action and toxicity both occur around the same serum concentration (9-10 mEq/L), making it an unsafe choice for tocolysis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define low birth weight and very low birth weight.
back: Low birth weight: <2.5kg. Very low birth weight: <1.5kg.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define prolonged rupture of membranes, and distinguish PROM from PPROM.
back: Prolonged ROM: membranes ruptured for >=18 hours. PROM (premature rupture of membranes): membrane rupture at or after 37 weeks, before the onset of labor. PPROM (preterm premature rupture of membranes): membrane rupture before 37 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of PROM (at or after 37 weeks)?
back: Sterile speculum exam to confirm (P/V exam is contraindicated), followed by induction of labor; Group B streptococcus prophylaxis is given if membrane rupture has lasted >=18 hours or fever >=4 hours develops during labor; there is no role for corticosteroids or tocolytics at this gestational age.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of PPROM at >34 weeks versus <34 weeks?
back: >34 weeks: steroids, antibiotics, fetal and maternal monitoring, with termination of pregnancy planned at 37 weeks. <34 weeks: steroids, antibiotics, tocolytics, MgSO4 (if <32 weeks), and fetal/maternal monitoring, aiming to prolong pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the absolute and relative contraindications to expectant management of PPROM?
back: Absolute contraindications: preterm labor at >=34 weeks, intrauterine fetal death, fetal anomaly, chorioamnionitis, and maternal hemodynamic instability. Relative contraindication: cervical dilatation >=3cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define cord prolapse, occult cord prolapse, and funic presentation.
back: Cord prolapse: the umbilical cord lies below the presenting part and outside the introitus, occurring after rupture of membranes. Occult prolapse: the cord lies alongside the presenting part after ROM but is not visible or palpable outside; diagnosed based on abnormal fetal heart rate. Funic presentation: the cord lies between the presenting part and the cervix with membranes still intact (before ROM).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the clinical features and management of funic presentation?
back: On P/V exam, it feels like a 'bag of worms'; artificial rupture of membranes is contraindicated (to avoid converting it to true cord prolapse); managed by emergency cesarean section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the CORD mnemonic for managing cord prolapse.
back: C: Consider the diagnosis (suspect if fetal distress develops immediately after ROM). O: Organize help. R: Relieve pressure on the cord (by manually elevating the presenting part, positioning the patient in knee-chest/steep Trendelenburg/exaggerated Sims position, or retrograde bladder filling). D: immediate Delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What steps are taken while transporting a patient with cord prolapse to the operating theater?
back: Maintain the position that relieves cord compression, stop any oxytocin infusion, give a tocolytic (terbutaline) to reduce contractions, and if the cord is protruding outside the vagina, gently reposition it inside (without applying pressure to it).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What determines the mode of delivery in cord prolapse - when is vaginal/instrumental delivery possible versus emergency cesarean?
back: Vaginal or instrumental delivery is possible only if the cervix is fully dilated and the fetal head is low (station >=+2). Otherwise, emergency cesarean section is performed. If fetal heart tones/cord pulsations are absent, vaginal delivery may be allowed (since the fetus is likely already deceased) rather than proceeding to emergency cesarean.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define hyperemesis gravidarum, and its main causes.
back: Excessive nausea and vomiting in pregnancy leading to >=5% weight loss or ketonuria; causes include conditions with elevated hCG (molar pregnancy, twin pregnancy) and H. pylori infection.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the complications of hyperemesis gravidarum?
back: Hypochloremia, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, vitamin B1 (thiamine) deficiency (risking Wernicke's encephalopathy), and vitamin K deficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management approach for hyperemesis gravidarum, from first-line to severe cases?
back: Investigation: USG (to rule out molar/twin pregnancy). First step: dietary modification (small frequent meals, avoiding spicy/fatty food, avoiding lying down immediately after meals). First-line drug: pyridoxine (vitamin B6); if this fails, add doxylamine. For severe cases: hospital admission, nil per oral, IV fluids, and ondansetron.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare HELLP syndrome, intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy, and acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP) by underlying mechanism/risk group.
back: HELLP syndrome: a complication of severe pre-eclampsia (about 80% of patients have high BP), recurrence rate 4-7%. Intrahepatic cholestasis: most common in twin pregnancy and with a female fetus, associated with elevated estrogen, with a high recurrence rate of about 70%. Acute fatty liver of pregnancy: occurs when the pregnant woman has a heterozygous deficiency of the LCHAD enzyme and the fetus has a homozygous deficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the clinical presentation of HELLP, cholestasis, and AFLP.
back: HELLP: abdominal pain in T3. Cholestasis: itching of the palms and soles, WITHOUT abdominal pain. AFLP: abdominal pain in T3, PLUS altered sensorium and hepatorenal failure.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the fetal/maternal complications of HELLP versus AFLP?
back: HELLP: fetal complications include stillbirth, cardiac arrhythmia, and meconium aspiration syndrome. AFLP: maternal complications include hypoglycemia, DIC, pancreatitis, and renal failure.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What laboratory findings characterize intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy?
back: Elevated bile acids and bile salts.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy?
back: Drug of choice is ursodeoxycholic acid (reduces itching and improves fetal outcomes); fetal monitoring starts at 32 weeks; termination of pregnancy is planned at 37-38 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of AFLP?
back: Similar to severe pre-eclampsia - MgSO4, antihypertensives, and definitive management by immediate termination of pregnancy (after the first dose of corticosteroids if applicable).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the volume of the placenta, and how is it divided between fetal and maternal blood compartments?
back: Total placental volume is about 500mL; the villi contain about 350mL of fetal blood, and the intervillous space (IVS) contains about 150mL of maternal blood; pressure within the IVS is about 10mmHg.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the functional unit of the placenta, and its normal location in the uterus?
back: Functional unit: the cotyledon. Normal location: the upper uterine segment.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When are the fetoplacental and uteroplacental circulations established?
back: Fetoplacental circulation (via the umbilical artery and vein) is established by day 17-21 after fertilization. Uteroplacental circulation (via the spiral arteries opening into the intervillous space) is established by day 15 after fertilization.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which estrogen is most specific to pregnancy, and which is most abundant?
back: Most specific: estriol (E3). Most abundant/common: estradiol (E2).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the substrate for placental progesterone synthesis, and what are progesterone's key functions?
back: Substrate: maternal LDL cholesterol. Functions: prepares the endometrium for implantation and decreases myometrial contractility.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What causes placentomegaly (thickness >=4cm), and what causes a small placenta?
back: Placentomegaly: twin pregnancy, Rh incompatibility, diabetes, molar pregnancy, and infections (parvovirus, syphilis). Small placenta: PIH, IUGR, and uteroplacental insufficiency.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is vasa previa, its most common cause, and its management?
back: The umbilical cord vessels run through the fetal membranes over the internal os (unprotected by placental tissue or Wharton's jelly); most commonly caused by velamentous cord insertion (Type 1); also caused by placenta succenturiata (Type 2). Membrane rupture can tear these vessels, causing severe fetal blood loss and fetal distress disproportionate to the blood loss. IOC: USG Doppler; managed by emergency cesarean section; artificial rupture of membranes is contraindicated when vasa previa is suspected.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How can fetal versus maternal origin of blood be distinguished, and by what test?
back: Singer's alkali denaturation test (also known as the APT test) differentiates fetal from maternal blood, based on the principle that fetal hemoglobin (HbF) resists denaturation by acids/alkalis; the reagent used is potassium or sodium hydroxide (KOH/NaOH).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the blood flow through the uteroplacental circulation at term?
back: Total uterine blood flow at term is about 750mL/minute (about 10% of cardiac output); utero-placental flow specifically is 500-750mL/minute; blood within the intervillous space is completely replaced about 3 times per minute.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does cord prolapse versus vasa previa present as causes of fetal distress after artificial rupture of membranes?
back: Cord prolapse: fetal distress without vaginal bleeding. Vasa previa: fetal distress WITH vaginal bleeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What tests differentiate amniotic fluid from vaginal discharge, and what proportion of amniotic fluid is water?
back: Nitrazine test and fern test; amniotic fluid is approximately 98-99% water.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the termination of pregnancy timing for oligohydramnios/polyhydramnios based on severity?
back: Mild-to-moderate oligo/polyhydramnios: 39 weeks. Severe oligo/polyhydramnios: 37 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the three named pelvic planes (inlet, cavity, outlet) and their anatomical landmarks?
back: Inlet: level of the pelvic brim. Cavity: level of the ischial spine (contains the plane of greatest and plane of least pelvic dimensions). Outlet: level of the ischial tuberosity.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the plane of greatest pelvic dimension - boundaries, diameter, and obstetric significance.
back: Anterior: posterior surface of the pubic symphysis; posterior: S2-S3 junction; lateral: obturator foramen; all diameters measure 12cm; it has no obstetric significance.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the plane of least pelvic dimension - boundaries and diameters.
back: Anterior: lower border of the pubic symphysis; posterior: S4-S5 junction; lateral: ischial spine; this is the narrowest plane of the pelvis; AP diameter 11.5-12cm; transverse (interischial/bispinous) diameter 10cm, the smallest diameter in the entire pelvis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define the midpelvis.
back: The area between the plane of greatest pelvic dimension and the plane of least pelvic dimension.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define the three AP diameters of the pelvic inlet, and their measurements.
back: True conjugate: distance from the sacral promontory to the upper border of the pubic symphysis, 11cm. Obstetric conjugate: distance from the sacral promontory to the mid-point of the pubic symphysis, 10.5cm (the narrowest, most functionally important AP diameter). Diagonal conjugate: distance from the sacral promontory to the lower border of the pubic symphysis, 12cm (the only one measurable clinically).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the transverse and oblique diameter of the pelvic inlet?
back: Transverse diameter: 13cm. Oblique diameter (right and left): distance from one side's sacroiliac joint to the opposite side's iliopectineal eminence, 12cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the anatomical and obstetric outlet boundaries and diameters?
back: Anatomical outlet: anterior - lower border of pubic symphysis; posterior - tip of coccyx; lateral - ischial tuberosity; AP diameter 13cm; transverse (bituberous) diameter 11cm. Obstetric outlet: the space between the plane of least pelvic dimensions and the anatomical outlet.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the critical obstetric conjugate value, and the clinical formula relating diagonal conjugate to obstetric conjugate?
back: Critical obstetric conjugate is <10cm (the value below which vaginal delivery is not possible). If diagonal conjugate = x cm, then obstetric conjugate = x - 1.5cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the overall most important pelvic diameter for labor, and the angle of pelvic inclination?
back: Most important diameter: the interischial (bispinous) diameter. Angle of inclination (between the pelvic inlet plane and the horizontal): 55 degrees.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does the subpubic angle differ between male and female pelvis?
back: The subpubic angle (between the descending rami of the pubic bones) is acute in the male pelvis and obtuse in the female pelvis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define contracted pelvis at the inlet, midpelvis, and outlet, and its management.
back: Contracted inlet: obstetric conjugate <10cm. Contracted midpelvis: interischial diameter <8cm. Contracted outlet: bituberous diameter <8cm (or any major pelvic diameter reduced by 1cm). Management is always cesarean section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish Naegele's pelvis from Robert's pelvis.
back: Naegele's pelvis: only one ala of the sacral bone is present (the other is absent). Robert's pelvis: both alae of the sacrum are absent.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the Caldwell-Moloy classification of pelvis types, including incidence, inlet shape, and favored fetal position.
back: Gynecoid (most common, ~50%): transverse oval inlet, transverse diameter > AP diameter, parallel/broad side walls, obtuse subpubic angle, most favorable for delivery, associated with occipitoanterior position. Android (~20%, male-type): heart-shaped inlet, transverse > AP diameter, convergent side walls, acute subpubic angle, prominent ischial spines, least favorable for delivery, associated with persistent occipitoposterior position. Anthropoid (~25%): anteroposterior oval inlet, AP > transverse diameter, parallel/narrow side walls, associated with direct occipitoposterior position. Platypelloid (least common, ~5%): flat bowl-shaped inlet, transverse diameter much greater than AP, divergent side walls, associated with face presentation, delivered face-to-pubis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define lie, and its three types with clinical implications.
back: Relationship between the long axis of the fetus and the long axis of the (corrected, non-dextrorotated) uterus. Longitudinal (most common): axes coincide. Oblique: axes form an angle. Transverse: axes are 90 degrees to each other; most common cause of cord prolapse; management is cesarean section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What causes unstable lie, and what is its most common cause?
back: The fetal lie repeatedly changes beyond 37 weeks; causes in order: idiopathic (most common) > placenta previa > polyhydramnios and abnormal pelvis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define presentation, presenting part, and denominator.
back: Presentation: the fetal part occupying the lower pole of the uterus. Presenting part: the specific part of the presentation lying directly over the internal os, felt first on P/V exam. Denominator: the bony landmark on the presenting part used to describe its relationship to the maternal pelvis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Match presenting parts to their denominators.
back: Vertex: occiput. Breech: sacrum. Brow: frontal eminence/bone. Face: mentum (chin).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is attitude, and its normal versus abnormal states?
back: The relationship between different parts of the fetus (degree of flexion/extension). Normal attitude: flexion. Abnormal attitudes: face and brow (though these are still a normal, cephalic, presentation - only the attitude is abnormal).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define station, and the significance of 0 and +2 stations.
back: The position of the fetal head relative to the ischial spines. 0 station indicates the head is engaged. +2 station or below is the level at which forceps or vacuum can be applied.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What structure is pierced during a pudendal nerve block, and at which anatomical landmark?
back: The sacrospinous ligament is pierced; the ischial spine is the anatomical landmark/site for the block.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define position, and list the most common positions in occipitoanterior, occipitoposterior, breech, and face presentations.
back: Position is the relationship of the denominator to the maternal pelvis. Most common position overall (antenatal): LOT (left occipitotransverse) > LOA; during labor: LOT; active labor: DOA (direct occipitoanterior). Most common occipitoanterior position: LOA. Most common occipitoposterior position: ROP. Most common position in breech: left sacroanterior. Most common position in face: left mentoanterior.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is fetal position identified on P/V examination?
back: First identify the occiput (found below the diamond/lambda-shaped posterior fontanelle); if the occiput is near the pubic symphysis, it is OA; near the sacral promontory, OP; midway between them, OT. If the occiput points toward the examiner's right, it corresponds to the mother's left, and vice versa.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is cephalopelvic disproportion (CPD), the best method of diagnosis, and radiological workup?
back: A mismatch between fetal head size and maternal pelvic dimensions preventing vaginal delivery; best diagnosed by trial of labor; radiological investigations in order of preference: MRI > CT > X-ray pelvis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish a fully flexed from a slightly deflexed fetal head on palpation.
back: Fully flexed head: posterior fontanelle is felt. Slightly deflexed head: anterior fontanelle (sinciput) is felt.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is moulding, and what are its three grades?
back: Alteration in fetal skull shape as it passes through the birth canal during labor. Grade 1: skull bones touch but do not overlap (can be seen normally). Grade 2: skull bones overlap but can be easily separated. Grade 3: fixed overlapping of skull bones (Grade 3 moulding with slow progress of labor indicates CPD).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank the AP diameters of the fetal skull from largest to smallest, and their associated presentations.
back: Mentovertical (14cm, largest - seen in brow presentation, requires cesarean) > submentovertical/occipitofrontal (11.5cm) > suboccipitofrontal (10cm) > suboccipitobregmatic/submentobregmatic (9.5cm, smallest, seen in a fully flexed vertex or fully extended face presentation).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank the transverse diameters of the fetal skull from smallest to largest (mnemonic).
back: Mnemonic 'Miss Tina So Pretty': bimastoid (7.5cm) < bitemporal (8cm) < super-subparietal (8.5cm) < biparietal (9.5cm, the most commonly engaging transverse diameter).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which fetal skull diameters measure exactly 9.5cm?
back: Biparietal diameter, suboccipitobregmatic diameter, and submentobregmatic diameter.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What defines the vertex, brow, and face regions of the fetal skull, and their engaging diameters by head position?
back: Vertex: between the anterior and posterior fontanelles; flexed head engages via suboccipitobregmatic diameter (9.5cm); deflexed/neutral head engages via occipitofrontal diameter (11.5cm); slightly deflexed engages via suboccipitofrontal diameter (10cm). Brow: between the anterior fontanelle and the root of the nose (with supraorbital ridges); partially extended head engages via mentovertical diameter (14cm), always requiring cesarean section. Face: between the root of the nose and the chin; fully extended head engages via submentobregmatic (9.5cm) or submentovertical (11.5cm) diameter.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare caput succedaneum and cephalhematoma by collection type, cause, and location.
back: Caput succedaneum: fluid collection, caused by prolonged stagnation of the fetal head during labor, located above the periosteum (can cross suture lines). Cephalhematoma: blood collection, caused by traumatic instrumental delivery, located below the periosteum (cannot cross suture lines).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare caput succedaneum and cephalhematoma by pitting, fracture association, timing, and resolution.
back: Caput succedaneum: pits on pressure, not associated with fracture, present at birth, resolves in 2-3 days. Cephalhematoma: does not pit on pressure, may be associated with skull fracture, appears hours after birth, resolves over 2-3 weeks (drainage is contraindicated).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish hand prolapse from compound presentation.
back: Hand prolapse: occurs in transverse lie, where the hand is the presenting part; managed by cesarean section. Compound presentation: occurs in a longitudinal lie, where both the head and a hand present together; managed by vaginal delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the technique and findings of the 1st (fundal) Leopold grip.
back: Performed facing the patient, palpating the fundus; assesses lie and presentation. An empty fundus suggests transverse lie; a broad, irregular, soft mass (buttocks) suggests cephalic presentation; a hard, globular mass (head) suggests breech presentation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the technique and findings of the 2nd (umbilical/lateral) Leopold grip.
back: Performed facing the patient, palpating the lateral sides of the uterus parallel to the umbilicus; assesses position. A smooth, regular, curved, board-like structure indicates the fetal back (corresponds to the occiput); small, multiple knob-like structures indicate the limbs.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the technique and findings of the 3rd (Pawlik's) Leopold grip.
back: Performed facing the patient, with a single hand grasping the lower pelvic area; assesses presentation and engagement. A firm, globular, round structure indicates cephalic presentation; if the head can be moved side to side (ballotable), it has not yet entered the pelvis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the technique and findings of the 4th (deep pelvic) Leopold grip.
back: The only grip performed facing the patient's feet, with fingers of both hands placed over the pelvic area parallel to the inguinal ligament; assesses attitude and confirms engagement of the head. If the examiner's fingers converge, the head is not engaged; if they diverge, the head is engaged.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the Bishop score assess, and what is its mnemonic for the 5 parameters?
back: Assesses cervical favorability/readiness for induction of labor (IOL). Mnemonic 'Delhi Police Employed Special Commandos': Dilatation, Position of cervix, Effacement, Station of fetal head, Consistency of cervix.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the Bishop score cutoffs for interpreting IOL success?
back: Maximum score 13; score <=5 is poor (cervical ripening needed before IOL); score >=6 means IOL can be attempted; score >=9 gives maximum chance of IOL success.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How does the modified Bishop score differ from the original, and what does the simplified Bishop score include?
back: Modified Bishop score replaces cervical effacement with cervical length measured by TVS (maximum score 9). Simplified Bishop score includes only three parameters (mnemonic 'DES'): Dilatation, Effacement, and Station.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the scoring criteria (0-3) for each Bishop score parameter.
back: Cervical dilatation: closed (0), 1-2cm (1), 3-4cm (2), >5cm (3). Cervix position: posterior (0), mid (1), anterior (2). Cervical effacement: 30% (0), 40-50% (1), 60-70% (2), >80% (3). Fetal head station: above -2 (0), -2 (1), -1/0 (2), below 0 (3). Cervical consistency: firm (0), medium (1), soft (2).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the mechanical methods of inducing labor?
back: Foley's catheter filled with 30-50mL normal saline (or extra-amniotic saline infusion) - considered the best method, especially for patients with a previous cesarean section; and stripping of the membranes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the medical (pharmacological) methods of inducing labor, with doses?
back: Misoprostol (PGE1): tablet 25mcg every 4 hours per-vaginally, maximum 6 doses. Dinoprostone (PGE2): available as Cerviprime gel 0.5mg every 6 hours (maximum 4 doses), or Cervidil, a slow-release 10mg dinoprostone insert placed in the posterior vaginal fornix.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the contraindications to induction of labor?
back: Severe cephalopelvic disproportion, contracted pelvis, transverse lie/brow presentation/face presentation in mentoposterior position, non-reassuring fetal heart rate, placenta previa, classical cesarean section, previous hysterotomy, previous myomectomy, and active genital herpes infection.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the criteria for adequate uterine contractions in labor, and where do contractions normally originate?
back: Frequency >=3 in 10 minutes, duration >=45 seconds (an alternate source states 40-45 seconds), intensity 200-250 Montevideo units (65-75mmHg); contractions normally originate from the cornua of the uterus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define tachysystole, its cause, and associated CTG finding.
back: More than 5 contractions in 10 minutes; if accompanied by fetal distress, it is termed hyperstimulation. Usually caused by augmentation of labor with misoprostol or oxytocin (iatrogenic); the associated CTG finding is prolonged deceleration (2-10 minutes).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of tachysystole/hyperstimulation with fetal distress?
back: Stop oxytocin, place the patient in the left lateral position, give oxygen by mask, and give terbutaline (a uterine relaxant/tocolytic).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What hormonal changes drive the onset of true labor?
back: Increased estrogen, increased oxytocin receptors, increased prostaglandins, and increased gap junctions between myometrial cells, combined with a functional withdrawal of progesterone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish true labor pain from false labor pain.
back: True labor pain: progressive, causes cervical dilatation, felt in the lower abdomen radiating to the back and thighs, associated with rupture of the bag of waters, not relieved by pain medication/enema/sedation, and is regular and rhythmic. False labor pain: non-progressive, does not cause cervical dilatation, non-radiating, not associated with membrane rupture, IS relieved by pain relief/enema/sedation, and is irregular.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the cardinal movements of labor, in order (mnemonic).
back: Mnemonic 'Every Decent Female I Employ Rises Extremely Late': Engagement, Descent, Flexion, Internal rotation, Extension (delivery of the head), Restitution, External rotation, Lateral flexion (delivery of the body). Crowning occurs after internal rotation but is NOT itself considered a cardinal movement.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is the fetal head delivered in vertex presentation versus breech/face presentation?
back: Vertex presentation: delivered by extension. Breech and face presentations: delivered by flexion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define engagement, its timing, and how it is assessed.
back: Occurs when the largest transverse diameter of the fetal head (the biparietal diameter, 9.5cm) crosses the pelvic brim. Timing: by 38 weeks in primigravida; at the onset of labor in multigravida. Assessment: on P/A exam, <=2/5th of the head is palpable above the pelvic brim; on P/V exam, the head is at 0 station or below.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the causes of an unengaged (free-floating) fetal head at term in a primigravida?
back: In order of frequency: deflexed/occipitoposterior position > cephalopelvic disproportion (CPD) > placenta previa.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define the four stages of labor by their start and end points.
back: Stage 1 (latent + active phase): begins with onset of regular uterine contractions, ends at full (10cm) cervical dilatation. Stage 2: begins at full dilatation, ends with delivery of the baby. Stage 3: begins after delivery of the baby, ends with delivery of the placenta. Stage 4: the observation period after placental delivery (1-2 hours), characterized by physiological chills.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What defines the start of the active phase of labor per different guidelines?
back: Modified WHO (older): 4cm. Labor Care Guide/next-generation WHO guideline: 5cm. ACOG: 6cm.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the recommended minimum rate of cervical dilatation in the active phase, per older versus newer WHO guidelines?
back: Earlier WHO guideline: minimum 1cm/hour. New WHO guideline: dilatation can be slower than 1cm/hour and still be acceptable if mother and baby are otherwise well.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the average durations of the latent phase, active phase (dilatation rate), stage 2, and stage 3 of labor, in primigravida versus multigravida?
back: Latent phase: <20 hours (primi), <14 hours (multi). Active phase dilatation rate: 1.2cm/hr (primi), 1.5cm/hr (multi; an alternate source cites 1cm/hr descent rate for primi and 2cm/hr for multi). Stage 2: average 50 minutes, maximum <3 hours (primi, +1 hour with epidural); average 30 minutes, maximum <2 hours (multi, +1 hour with epidural). Stage 3: active management 5-10 minutes, passive management 10-15 minutes, maximum 30 minutes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define prolonged latent phase, its causes, and management.
back: Latent phase >=20 hours in primigravida or >=14 hours in multigravida, with cervical dilatation <1cm/hour or fetal head descent <1cm (primi)/<2cm (multi). Causes: occipitoposterior position, unfavorable cervix, or false labor pains. Management: therapeutic rest; cesarean section is never done for prolonged latent phase alone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define protracted active phase, and its management algorithm.
back: Slow progression of cervical dilatation during the active phase (dilatation <1cm/hour, especially with moulding/caput, suggesting CPD). Management: perform P/A and P/V exam; if contractions are hypotonic, augment labor (if head is fixed: ARM then oxytocin after 30 minutes; if head is mobile: oxytocin then ARM after 30 minutes); if CPD is suspected, perform cesarean section; if due to occipitoposterior position, wait and watch.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define active phase arrest, and its management.
back: Diagnosed when membranes are ruptured and there has been no change in cervical dilatation for 4 hours with adequate uterine contractions, or for 6 hours with inadequate contractions despite oxytocin. Management: cesarean section (emergency cesarean if fetal distress is also present).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define prolonged/arrested second stage of labor, and its management based on fetal station.
back: Nulliparous: second stage >=3 hours without epidural or >=4 hours with epidural. Multiparous: second stage >=2 hours without epidural or >=3 hours with epidural. Management: if the fetal head is above +2 station, perform cesarean section; if at or below +2 station, perform instrumental delivery (forceps/vacuum). If Bandl's ring is present (obstructed labor) or moulding/caput is present with second-stage arrest (CPD), cesarean section is done regardless of station.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Under what conditions can forceps or vacuum be applied?
back: The cervix must be fully dilated, and the station of the fetal head must be at or below +2.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the causes and clinical features of obstructed labor?
back: Causes include transverse lie and conjoined twins.
