Clinical features: maternal dehydration, exhaustion, tachypnea, and tachycardia; on P/A exam - the upper uterine segment is thick, tender, and tonically contracted, while the lower uterine segment is thinned and stretched, with a palpable/visible Bandl's ring (a groove between the two segments); on P/V exam - hot and dry vagina, marked caput and moulding, and possible hematuria.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of obstructed labor, and its complications?
back: Immediate cesarean section; oxytocin, forceps, and vacuum should NOT be given. Complications: uterine rupture, and vesicovaginal fistula (VVF, developing 7-10 days later; obstructed labor is the most common cause of VVF in developing countries).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish Bandl's ring (retraction ring) from a constriction ring (Schroeder's ring).
back: Bandl's ring: a pathological retraction ring caused by obstructed labor, located between the upper and lower uterine segments, seen in the second stage of labor, felt AND seen on P/A exam, associated with maternal dehydration and fetal distress, relieved only by cesarean section. Constriction ring: caused by injudicious oxytocin use leading to localized uterine spasm, can occur anywhere in the uterus, seen in the first or second stage of labor, felt only on P/V exam, with a normal mother and fetus, relieved by sedation (if it recurs, a lower vertical cesarean section is the only indication for that specific incision type).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe augmentation of labor - definition, indication, and methods.
back: Accelerating the progress of labor; indicated for protracted active phase, after ruling out occipitoposterior position and CPD. Methods: artificial rupture of membranes (ARM, performed with Kocher forceps), and oxytocin infusion started at 6 mU/min, increased by 6 mU/min at intervals of 20-40 minutes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the contraindications to artificial rupture of membranes (ARM)?
back: Maternal HIV infection, active genital herpes infection, vasa previa/funic presentation, and intrauterine fetal death. In polyhydramnios, a controlled (rather than complete) rupture of membranes is performed instead.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of fetal distress in the first versus second stage of labor?
back: First stage (cervix <10cm dilated): cesarean section. Second stage (cervix fully dilated, 10cm): if fetal head is above +2 station, cesarean section; if at or below +2 station, instrumental delivery (forceps preferred over vacuum).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What assessments are recommended at admission for labor, and which are NOT routinely recommended?
back: Recommended: check vital signs, Leopold's maneuvers, auscultate fetal heart sounds, and P/V examination (contraindicated if active bleeding is present). Not recommended: routine pelvimetry, routine enema, routine CTG, and routine pelvic/perineal shaving.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What supportive care is recommended during the first stage of labor?
back: Ambulation is allowed in early labor; left lateral position is recommended in late labor; clear fluids may be taken orally (solids are not allowed); a birth companion is allowed; and adequate pain relief (epidural, opioids, massage) should be offered.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the pre-operative fasting protocol before cesarean section during labor?
back: Clear liquids should be stopped 2 hours before surgery; solid food should be stopped 6 hours before surgery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What maternal and fetal monitoring is recommended during the first stage of labor?
back: Maternal: pulse and BP hourly, temperature every 4 hours (hourly if membranes have ruptured), uterine contractions assessed every 30 minutes, and P/V examination every 4 hours. Fetal heart rate is also monitored at regular intervals.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What measures help prevent perineal tears during the second stage of labor?
back: Perineal massage, warm compresses to the perineum, controlled delivery of the fetal head, and perineal support, using the modified Ritgen maneuver (one hand first flexes then extends the fetal head while the other hand supports the perineum).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which interventions during the second stage of labor are NOT routinely recommended?
back: Routine episiotomy and fundal pressure.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common malpresentation, its most common cause, and most common cause of recurrence?
back: Breech is the most common malpresentation; its most common overall cause is prematurity; the most common cause of RECURRENT breech is uterine malformation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the risk of gross congenital anomalies in breech presentation, the investigation of choice, and the best management?
back: 10% risk of gross congenital anomalies; investigation of choice is USG; best management is cesarean section at 39 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Classify the types of breech presentation by hip/knee position, and their relative cord prolapse risk.
back: Frank/extended breech (most common): hip flexed, knees extended; vaginal delivery possible; lowest cord prolapse risk among breech types. Complete/flexed breech: hip and knees both flexed; vaginal delivery possible. Footling breech: hip and knees both extended (feet present first); cesarean section only, highest cord prolapse risk among breech types. Knee presentation: hips extended, knees flexed. Overall cord prolapse risk ranking: transverse lie > footling > knee > flexed > frank.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is 'Stargazer' breech, and its management?
back: Breech presentation with the fetal head extended (looking upward); managed by cesarean section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the absolute indications for cesarean section in breech presentation?
back: Footling presentation, knee presentation, Stargazer breech, previous history of cesarean section, breech with macrosomia, and preterm breech.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the criteria for a 'favorable' breech eligible for vaginal (assisted breech) delivery?
back: Gestational age >=36 weeks, spontaneous onset of labor, no history of prior cesarean section (VBAC is a relative contraindication), no other contraindication to vaginal delivery, facility for emergency cesarean available, and on USG: frank breech more favorable than complete breech, estimated fetal weight 2-4kg, head not extended, and no anomaly likely to cause abnormal labor.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management approach for breech presentation in the antenatal period, and what is vaginal delivery of a breech termed?
back: External cephalic version (ECV) is attempted at >=36 weeks; if vaginal delivery is undertaken for breech, it is termed assisted breech delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common cause of death in breech delivery, and how is the after-coming head delivered?
back: Most common cause of death: intracranial hemorrhage. The head (delivered last in breech) is born by the movement of flexion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the maneuvers used for delivery of the buttocks/legs and shoulders in breech delivery.
back: Groin traction: delivers the buttocks in a flexed breech. Pinard maneuver (pressure on the popliteal fossa): delivers extended legs in a frank breech. Lovset's maneuver (holding the baby's back and rotating): delivers the shoulders.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the maneuvers used to deliver the after-coming head in dorso-anterior versus dorso-posterior breech.
back: Dorso-anterior breech (most common): Burns-Marshall technique, Mauriceau-Smellie-Veit technique (malar flexion plus shoulder traction), or Piper's forceps (long forceps with a perineal curve). Dorso-posterior breech: Prague maneuver.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the presentation, delivery approach, and most common causes of transverse lie?
back: Presentation is the shoulder; vaginal delivery is not possible; it carries the maximum risk of cord prolapse. Most common cause overall: prematurity; most common cause at term: placenta previa. Management: if seen antenatally, ECV at >=36 weeks; if the patient presents in the latent phase of labor, ECV can still be attempted; if in the active phase, cesarean section is required. A dead baby with neglected transverse lie is also managed by cesarean section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the engaging diameter and management of brow presentation, and its diagnostic clinical sign?
back: Engaging diameter is the mentovertical diameter (14cm, the largest fetal skull diameter); management is always cesarean section; diagnosed when the anterior fontanelle and supraorbital ridges are both palpable on exam.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the denominator of face presentation, and how does management differ between mentoanterior and mentoposterior positions?
back: Denominator is the mentum (chin). Mentoanterior position: vaginal delivery possible, engaging diameter is submentobregmatic/submentovertical, head delivered by flexion, forceps can be used (vacuum cannot). Mentoposterior position: cesarean section required; neither forceps nor vacuum can be used.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common cause of face presentation, and which pelvis type is it most associated with?
back: Most common cause: anencephaly. Most commonly associated with the platypelloid pelvis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common occipitoposterior (OP) position, and its most common and second most common causes?
back: Most common OP position: right occipitoposterior (ROP). Most common cause of OP position: deflexed fetal head. Second most common cause: android pelvis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common outcome and best management of occipitoposterior position?
back: Most common outcome: the head anatomically rotates 3/8th of a circle to become direct occipitoanterior (DOA), allowing vaginal delivery. Best management: wait and watch.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the clinical (P/A exam) findings distinguishing occipitoposterior from occipitoanterior position?
back: Occipitoposterior: infra/subumbilical flattening, fetal limbs toward the umbilicus, fetal back toward the mother's flank, and fetal heart sounds best heard in the maternal flank area. Occipitoanterior: smooth baby bump contour, and fetal heart sounds heard near the umbilicus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define deep transverse arrest - associated pelvis type, level, and management.
back: Occurs when internal rotation fails and the fetal head remains transverse at the level of the ischial spines; most commonly associated with the android pelvis (due to prominent ischial spines); best management is cesarean section.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the ECV procedure - approach, setting, and anesthesia.
back: The fetus is rotated per-abdominally from breech or transverse lie into a cephalic presentation; performed in the outpatient department; no anesthesia is needed, though tocolytics are used to relax the uterus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the indication and ideal timing for ECV, and the protocol for Rh-negative women?
back: Indicated for breech or transverse lie in a singleton pregnancy; ideally performed at or after 36 weeks (usually done at 37 weeks, to minimize the chance of the fetus reverting); Rh-negative women are given anti-D (300mcg) after the procedure.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What monitoring is required during and after ECV, and is induction of labor routinely needed afterward?
back: Should be performed under continuous fetal monitoring; no routine induction of labor is needed after a successful ECV.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the prerequisites for performing ECV?
back: Gestational age >=36 weeks, adequate liquor volume with intact membranes, singleton pregnancy, normal fetal heart rate on CTG, and no contraindication to vaginal delivery; can also be performed in early labor (latent phase) if membranes are still intact.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the absolute contraindications to ECV?
back: Oligohydramnios, ruptured membranes, active phase of labor, twin/multifetal pregnancy, abnormal fetal heart rate on CTG, placenta previa or contracted pelvis, and a grossly anomalous uterus or fetus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the relative contraindications to ECV?
back: PIH, maternal heart disease, IUGR, and previous LSCS (previous cesarean section is a relative, not absolute, contraindication).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the key features of the Modified WHO Partogram?
back: Based on earlier WHO recommendations; the active phase (plotted) begins at 4cm and continues to 10cm; the latent phase and second stage of labor are NOT plotted; the minimum expected cervical dilatation rate in the active phase (used to draw the alert line) is 1cm/hour; the alert and action lines are drawn 4 hours apart.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the upper section of the Modified WHO Partogram record?
back: Fetal condition assessment: fetal heart rate (plotted as a circle every 30 minutes, normal range 110-160bpm), amniotic fluid status (I=intact, A=absent liquor, C=clear, B=blood-stained, M=meconium-stained), and presence of moulding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the middle section (cervicogram) of the Modified WHO Partogram record?
back: Time on the X-axis and cervical dilatation (marked X) plus descent of the fetal head (marked O) on the Y-axis; large squares represent 1 hour, small squares represent 30 minutes; two parallel lines (alert and action lines) are drawn 4 hours apart.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the lower section of the Modified WHO Partogram record, and what is/isn't charted overall?
back: Maternal condition assessment: uterine contractions assessed every 30 minutes by palpating the fundus, with the number of contractions per 10 minutes recorded (each square represents one contraction). Also charted: oxytocin, drugs, maternal pulse/BP/temperature, and urine output. NOT charted: respiratory rate, oral intake, and oxygen saturation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is contraction duration represented symbolically on the partogram?
back: Contractions lasting <20 seconds, 20-40 seconds, and >40 seconds are each represented with distinct symbols/shading.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the key features of the Labor Care Guide (LCG)/Next-Generation Partogram, based on the 2020 WHO guidelines?
back: The active phase begins at 5cm (rather than 4cm); cervical dilatation slower than 1cm/hour can still be acceptable if mother and baby are well; the latent phase is still not represented, but plotting begins at 5cm; there are NO alert or action lines; and unlike the Modified WHO Partogram, the second stage of labor IS represented.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Who is the LCG intended for, and at what levels of care is it used?
back: Intended for all women in labor (especially low-risk women); used at all levels of health care.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the 7 sections of the LCG (mnemonic)?
back: Mnemonic 'Smart Beautiful Women Love Me': 1) Patient details and labour details, 2) Supportive care, 3) Care of the baby, 4) Care of the woman, 5) Labour progress, 6) Medication, 7) Shared decision making.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What does the 'supportive care' section of the LCG record (mnemonic)?
back: Mnemonic elements: Companion, Pain relief, Oral fluid, Posture (mobile, left lateral, or supine - supine posture is flagged as an alert sign), each recorded as Yes/No/Declined by the woman.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Who devised the alert and action lines used on partograms, and what do they represent?
back: Devised by Philpott; the alert line represents the minimum expected cervical dilatation rate in the active phase (1cm/hour); the action line is drawn 4 hours to the right of the alert line, and crossing it prompts intervention.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is active management of the third stage of labor (AMTSL), and its benefits?
back: WHO-recommended active management of the third stage of labor; decreases the duration of the third stage (to 5-10 minutes), decreases blood loss, and decreases the chances of PPH - it is the best method to prevent PPH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the four steps of AMTSL?
back: 1) Injection of a uterotonic within 1 minute of delivery of the baby, or immediately after delivery of the anterior shoulder (the most important step); 2) delayed cord clamping (clamping the cord 1-3 minutes after delivery); 3) delivery of the placenta by controlled cord traction (modified Brandt-Andrews technique); 4) intermittent assessment of uterine tone (previously taught as routine uterine massage, which per WHO is not part of AMTSL but is used in PPH management instead).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What uterotonic agents are recommended for AMTSL, in order of preference, with doses?
back: Oxytocin 10 IU IM or IV infusion (first choice). If unavailable: methylergometrine 0.2mg IM (never IV, risk of hypertension), syntometrine (fixed-dose combination of 5U oxytocin plus 0.5mg methylergometrine), carbetocin (a synthetic, longer-acting oxytocin analogue, 100mcg slow IV - used only in AMTSL, not for treating established PPH), or misoprostol (PGE1) 400-600mcg orally (WHO recommends antenatal distribution of misoprostol to pregnant women to help prevent PPH).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the absolute indications for early cord clamping (within 1 minute)?
back: Birth asphyxia/need for neonatal resuscitation, known congenital heart disease, and a non-isoimmunized Rh-negative pregnancy (indirect Coombs test negative).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the indications for delayed cord clamping (1-3 minutes), and its benefit?
back: HIV-positive mothers, Rh-negative pregnancy with a POSITIVE indirect Coombs test (since maternal antibodies are already present, early clamping offers no protective benefit), preterm infants, COVID-19-positive patients, macrosomic babies, and post-term pregnancies; delayed clamping transfers an extra ~80mL of blood to the fetus, helping prevent neonatal anemia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is oxytocin's source, half-life, and key contraindications regarding administration route/diluent?
back: Synthesized by the paraventricular nucleus of the hypothalamus (stored in the posterior pituitary), a nonapeptide also responsible for milk ejection and afterpains; half-life 3-5 minutes. IV bolus administration is contraindicated (risk of hypotension/cardiac arrest); preparation in 5% dextrose is contraindicated (risk of water intoxication) - it should be prepared in Ringer's lactate or normal saline.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are methylergometrine's route, mechanism, and absolute contraindications (mnemonic)?
back: Given IM only (never IV, due to risk of transient severe hypertension); causes tetanic uterine contractions. Absolute contraindications (mnemonic 'TOPER'): after delivery of the first Twin (before the second twin is delivered), Organic heart disease, Peripheral vascular disease, Eclampsia/pre-eclampsia, and Rh-negative women.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are misoprostol's routes, half-life, and key feature regarding asthma?
back: Can be given orally, sublingually, or per rectally (available as a tablet); NOT used per-vaginally in this context; water and heat soluble, with a half-life of 20-40 minutes; unlike carboprost, misoprostol CAN be safely used in asthmatic patients. Side effect: hyperthermia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are carboprost's indication, features, and contraindications?
back: Best drug for treating established PPH (but not used for routine AMTSL); side effect: diarrhea. Absolute contraindications: asthmatics, pulmonary hypertension, and suspected amniotic fluid embolism; relative contraindications: pre-eclampsia and renal disease.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the clinical signs of placental separation?
back: A gush of blood per vaginally, a suprapubic bulge, apparent and permanent lengthening of the umbilical cord (second-best sign), and a slight rise in the height of the uterine fundus. The surest sign is feeling the placenta in the vagina.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the Schultze and Duncan methods of placental separation.
back: Schultze method (most common): central separation of the placenta, forming a retroplacental clot that reduces visible bleeding; the shiny fetal surface of the placenta emerges first. Duncan method (second most common): peripheral separation; the dull maternal surface of the placenta emerges first.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is trapped placenta?
back: The placenta has separated from the uterine wall but is not expelled, due to a closed internal os.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If the cord breaks during controlled cord traction, what is the next step?
back: Manual removal of the placenta (MRP).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define retained placenta/prolonged third stage, and its management based on uterine tone.
back: Placenta not delivered within 30 minutes of delivery of the baby. Management: first empty the bladder; if the uterus is relaxed, give oxytocin plus controlled cord traction; if the uterus is contracted with a closed os, perform manual removal of the placenta (MRP).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the algorithm for managing retained placenta based on signs of placental separation?
back: If all signs of placental separation are present, give oxytocin plus controlled cord traction; if this fails, or if signs are absent, perform manual removal of placenta; if a distinct separation plane cannot be established during MRP, suspect placenta accreta spectrum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Classify the types of placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) by depth of invasion, and which is most common.
back: Placenta accreta (most common): chorionic villi superficially attached to the myometrium. Placenta increta: villi invade into the myometrium. Placenta percreta: villi attach to/through the uterine serosa.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the two most important risk factors for PAS, and how does the patient present?
back: Previous history of uterine surgery (especially cesarean section) and placenta previa in the current pregnancy. Presents with intractable PPH and a placenta that fails to separate.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the investigation of choice for PAS, and its USG findings?
back: IOC: TVS. Findings: placental lakes/moth-eaten appearance, loss of the normal hypoechoic area behind the placenta (representing decidua basalis), and disruption of the continuous white line reflecting the bladder-uterine serosa interface.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the underlying pathology of PAS, and its management?
back: Absent decidua basalis and absent Nitabuch layer, allowing abnormal trophoblastic invasion. Management: hysterectomy (if diagnosed antenatally, plan cesarean section followed by hysterectomy).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define PPH by blood loss volume, per standard versus ACOG criteria.
back: Standard definition: >=500mL blood loss after vaginal delivery, or >=1000mL after cesarean section. ACOG definition: >=1000mL blood loss irrespective of delivery type, OR bleeding with signs/symptoms of hypovolemia.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What clinical/laboratory criteria also define PPH?
back: A fall in hematocrit of >=10% between admission and the postpartum period, or bleeding severe enough to require blood transfusion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish primary from secondary PPH by timing and most common cause.
back: Primary PPH: occurs within 24 hours of delivery; most common cause is atonic uterus. Secondary PPH: occurs from 24 hours up to 12 weeks after delivery; most common cause is retained placental tissue.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common cause of maternal mortality in India, and most common cause of shock after delivery?
back: Both are PPH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the '4 T's' causes of PPH?
back: Tone (decreased uterine tone/atony - most common), Trauma, retained Tissue, and Thrombin/thromboplastin (bleeding/coagulation disorders).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the initial resuscitative management of PPH?
back: Massage the uterus (P/A), insert two large-bore IV cannulas (14G/16G), give 1000mL of crystalloids, place a Foley catheter for input-output charting, send investigations (CBC, blood grouping and Rh typing), and arrange blood/blood products.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is atonic PPH distinguished from traumatic PPH on examination, and what confirms the atonic diagnosis?
back: P/A and P/V exam: if uterine tone is reduced or only maintained with massage, this suggests atonic PPH; if uterine tone is normal but a laceration/hematoma is found, this suggests traumatic PPH (managed by repair). Atonic PPH is confirmed once the entire placenta and membranes are verified to be expelled.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the first-line management of confirmed atonic PPH?
back: Uterotonic drugs, plus tranexamic acid, plus bimanual uterine massage/compression.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the uterotonic drug sequence for treating established PPH, with doses.
back: Oxytocin IV infusion (20-40 IU in 500mL NS) first; if this fails, methylergometrine 0.2mg IM (repeated every 2-4 hours); if this fails, carboprost 250mcg IM (repeated every 15-90 minutes, maximum 8 doses/2mg total); misoprostol can be added on (WHO: 800mcg oral/sublingual; ACOG: 600-1000mcg oral/sublingual/per rectal).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is tranexamic acid's dose and timing in PPH management?
back: 1g in 100mL normal saline, infused over 10-20 minutes, given within 3 hours of hemorrhage onset.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which drugs are NOT used for treating established PPH, and why?
back: Dinoprostone (PGE2): used for induction of labor, not PPH. Carbetocin: used for AMTSL/prevention, not for treating established PPH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If uterotonics and bimanual massage fail to control atonic PPH within 30 minutes, what is the next step?
back: Mechanical compression using a Bakri balloon catheter (maximum capacity 500mL; other options include an SB esophageal catheter or condom catheter); if bleeding stops, the balloon should be removed after 12 hours.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: If mechanical compression fails, what surgical options follow, in sequence?
back: Move to the operating theater for compression sutures (most commonly B-Lynch suture; others include Hayman, Cho square, and Gunshella sutures); if bleeding persists, proceed to stepwise devascularization (uterine artery ligation, then ovarian artery ligation, then anterior division of the internal iliac artery ligation); if bleeding still persists and the patient is unstable, proceed directly to subtotal hysterectomy (cervix spared) - pelvic pressure packs (umbrella or parachute pack) may also be applied as a temporizing measure.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the important exception to following the stepwise PPH algorithm in order?
back: If at any point the patient's vitals become unstable despite initial resuscitation and oxytocin, do NOT continue through each algorithm step sequentially - proceed directly to uterine artery ligation (or hysterectomy as needed).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the 'water under the bridge' relationship relevant to hysterectomy, and its clinical significance?
back: The uterine artery crosses under the ureter about 2cm lateral to the internal os; this is the most common site of ureteric injury during hysterectomy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define uterine inversion, and its most common cause.
back: Prolapse of the uterine fundus into (or through) the uterine cavity/vagina; an obstetric emergency; most commonly caused by mismanaged third stage of labor (e.g., excessive cord traction before placental separation).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What type of shock occurs initially in uterine inversion, and what is the ultimate cause of death?
back: Initial shock is neurogenic (due to traction on pelvic structures/peritoneum); this is later followed by hemorrhagic shock, which is the actual cause of death if untreated.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the P/A and P/V examination findings in uterine inversion?
back: P/A: a cup-like depression below the umbilicus (fundus not palpable in its normal position). P/V: a globular mass (the inverted fundus) felt in the vagina, with bleeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is uterine inversion distinguished from uterine prolapse on exam?
back: In uterine inversion, the internal os is NOT visible (it has inverted with the fundus). In uterine prolapse, the internal os IS visible.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management sequence for uterine inversion?
back: Call for help (obstetric emergency), resuscitate the patient, stop oxytocin; if the placenta is still attached, do NOT try to separate it - attempt manual replacement of the uterus (Johnson's technique) directly; if the placenta has already separated, attempt manual replacement, and if this fails, give a uterine relaxant/tocolytic and reattempt; if this still fails, proceed to abdominal surgery (Huntington's or Haultain's method). Once repositioned: stop the tocolytic, deliver/remove the placenta, start oxytocin (to prevent re-inversion), and start antibiotics.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What type of diagnosis is amniotic fluid embolism, and its alternate name?
back: A clinical diagnosis with no confirmatory test (though pulmonary artery catheterization may be useful); also called anaphylactoid syndrome of pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When does amniotic fluid embolism typically occur, and how quickly can it be fatal?
back: At the time of labor or within 30 minutes of labor; can cause maternal death within 30-40 minutes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the two clinical phases of amniotic fluid embolism.
back: Phase 1: cardiorespiratory arrest (difficulty breathing, increased pulse rate, decreased BP). Phase 2: DIC with excessive bleeding.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the diagnostic clues for amniotic fluid embolism?
back: Unexplained shock with relatively little visible bleeding initially, clinical onset within 30 minutes of labor, abrupt cardiorespiratory arrest, overt DIC, and absence of fever; P/A and P/V exams are typically normal.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the most common cause of postpartum collapse overall?
back: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How do PPH, uterine inversion, and amniotic fluid embolism differ as causes of postpartum collapse?
back: PPH: excessive visible bleeding leading to shock, with decreased uterine tone on P/A and excessive bleeding on P/V. Uterine inversion: immediate shock after delivery (initially neurogenic, then hemorrhagic), with a cup-like depression on P/A and a globular mass on P/V. Amniotic fluid embolism: breathlessness/respiratory difficulty/hypoxia plus shock, typically with normal P/A and P/V findings.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What condition should be suspected if a postpartum patient has visual disturbance, headache, hypoglycemia, and shock, and what causes it?
back: Pituitary apoplexy (hemorrhage/infarction of the pituitary gland); can be caused by a prolactinoma enlarging during pregnancy, or by severe PPH after delivery leading to Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary necrosis); first-line management is steroids.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the clinical features of vulval hematoma, and the artery typically involved?
back: Difficulty passing urine and severe pelvic pain after delivery; caused by injury to the pudendal artery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of vulval hematoma based on severity?
back: Check vitals; conservative management (analgesics and sitz bath) if stable; surgical drainage of the hematoma if vitals deteriorate, the hematoma increases in size, or pain becomes severe and unbearable.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the indications for episiotomy?
back: After-coming head of breech, instrumental delivery, macrosomia, and shoulder dystocia (WHO does NOT recommend routine episiotomy for all vaginal deliveries).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which perineal muscles are NOT cut during episiotomy?
back: Ischiococcygeus/coccygeus, ischiocavernosus, anal sphincter, and obturator internus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the sequence of layers repaired after episiotomy?
back: Vaginal mucosa, then muscles, then skin.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare median and mediolateral episiotomy by bleeding, healing, and anal sphincter involvement.
back: Median episiotomy: fewer muscle fibers cut, less bleeding, quicker healing, less wound gaping, less dyspareunia, BUT higher risk of extending into the anal sphincter if the tear extends further (making it less preferred). Mediolateral episiotomy (angle 45-60 degrees, most preferred): more muscle fibers cut, more bleeding, slower healing, more wound gaping, more dyspareunia, BUT does NOT involve the anal sphincter even if extended.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Classify perineal tears by grade and structures involved.
back: Grade 1: vaginal mucosa/perineal skin (corresponds to a median episiotomy). Grade 2: Grade 1 plus perineal muscles (corresponds to a mediolateral episiotomy). Grade 3: Grade 2 plus anal sphincter, subdivided into 3a (<50% of external anal sphincter thickness torn), 3b (>=50% torn), and 3c (internal anal sphincter torn). Grade 4: Grade 3 plus anal/rectal mucosa.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How are Grade 1-2 versus Grade 3-4 perineal tears repaired, and what follow-up is advised after repair of a complete tear?
back: Grade 1-2: repaired like an episiotomy, in the labor room under local anesthesia. Grade 3-4 (complete perineal tear): repaired in the operating theater under epidural or general anesthesia; the patient should avoid pregnancy for 1 year (at least 6 months) after repair; subsequent delivery can be vaginal, unless there is a history of >=2 anal sphincter injuries, in which case cesarean section is recommended.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define VBAC and TOLAC.
back: VBAC: vaginal birth after cesarean. TOLAC: trial of labor after cesarean.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the absolute contraindications to VBAC?
back: History of >=2 previous LSCS (per Indian national guidelines), classical cesarean section, myomectomy, hysterotomy, or uterine rupture.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the relative contraindications to VBAC?
back: Home birth or lack of 24-hour emergency facility availability, any contraindication to vaginal delivery (e.g., placenta previa, CPD) in the current pregnancy, and cephalopelvic disproportion in the current pregnancy.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Which conditions are NOT contraindications to VBAC?
back: Macrosomia, malpresentation, post-term pregnancy, twin pregnancy, and polyhydramnios in the current pregnancy are not, by themselves, contraindications to VBAC.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare uterine rupture risk by type of previous cesarean incision.
back: Classical (upper uterine segment, vertical) incision: 4-9% rupture risk; VBAC is contraindicated in the next pregnancy (must repeat cesarean section). Transverse LSCS (lower uterine segment): <2% rupture risk; VBAC is allowed in the next pregnancy. Low vertical/Kronig's incision (vertical incision in the lower uterine segment): 2-7% rupture risk; ACOG allows VBAC, but Indian national guidelines do not.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What monitoring is recommended during a trial of VBAC, and how many prior LSCS can TOLAC be attempted after?
back: Monitor maternal pulse rate, continuous CTG, and uterine scar tenderness; TOLAC can be attempted after any number of previous LSCS deliveries (assuming no other contraindication) and after a previous Kronig (low vertical) incision.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the relative versus absolute contraindication status of ECV and IPV in VBAC?
back: External cephalic version (ECV) is a relative contraindication in VBAC. Internal podalic version (IPV) is an absolute contraindication in VBAC.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Is twin pregnancy a contraindication to VBAC, and what are the exceptions?
back: Not a contraindication in general, EXCEPT for monochorionic monoamniotic twins, conjoined twins, or when the first twin is cephalic and the second twin is in transverse lie.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish impending scar rupture/dehiscence from complete scar rupture during VBAC by examination findings.
back: Impending rupture/dehiscence (serosal layer intact): maternal tachycardia, vaginal bleeding, hematuria; P/A shows suprapubic tenderness and continued uterine contractions; FHS initially tachycardic, later bradycardic; free fluid absent in the peritoneum. Complete scar rupture (all uterine layers ruptured): hypotension and tachycardia (shock), profuse bleeding, profuse hematuria; P/V shows loss of fetal station, no uterine contractions felt, fetal parts felt superficially, a palpable gap in the uterus, and absent FHS; free fluid present in the peritoneum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of both impending and complete uterine scar rupture?
back: Immediate cesarean section, with maternal resuscitation and exploratory laparotomy to remove the placenta and baby and repair the uterus.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish classical cesarean section from LSCS by incision location.
back: Classical cesarean: incision in the upper uterine segment. LSCS (lower segment cesarean section): incision in the lower uterine segment.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the indications for classical cesarean section?
back: Cancer cervix (absolute indication), severely contracted pelvis, lower uterine segment adhered to the bladder, placenta previa with bleeding and blood vessels covering the lower segment, an unformed lower uterine segment (as in preterm cesarean section), and perimortem cesarean section (maternal cardiac arrest, requiring delivery within 4 minutes).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the delivery approach for a woman with a previous classical cesarean section?
back: Vaginal delivery should never be attempted; a repeat LSCS is performed in the next pregnancy to avoid uterine rupture.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the key procedural facts about cesarean section - anesthesia level, positioning, and timing recommendations?
back: Level of analgesia required: T4 (T10 for epidural analgesia/painless labor); patient position: supine with a 15-degree left lateral tilt (or a wedge under the right hip) to avoid aortocaval compression; ideal time gap before the next pregnancy: 18 months (minimum 6 months); elective cesarean section (for non-medical reasons) is performed at 39 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the prerequisites for instrumental (forceps or vacuum) delivery (mnemonic)?
back: Mnemonic 'FORCEP': Favorable position and station (>=+2), fully dilated cervical Os, Ruptured membranes, Contracted (adequately toned) uterus, Episiotomy given and Empty bladder, and adequate Pelvis - plus patient consent must be obtained.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Where is the vacuum cup applied, and where is this landmark located?
back: Applied at the flexion point, located 3cm anterior to the posterior fontanelle (preferred landmark) or 6cm posterior to the anterior fontanelle.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the vacuum's initial and maximum application pressures?
back: Initial pressure: 0.2 kg/cm2. Maximum pressure: 0.8 kg/cm2 (equivalent to <=600mmHg).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Distinguish low forceps from outlet forceps by fetal head station, and name the classic outlet forceps.
back: Low forceps: station >=+2 (head has not yet reached the pelvic floor). Outlet forceps: station +3 to +5 (head is on the perineum, scalp visible at the introitus, skull on the pelvic floor); the classic outlet forceps is Wrigley's forceps.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare forceps and vacuum by ability to rotate the fetal head, and their respective uses.
back: Vacuum CAN rotate the fetal head; forceps CANNOT. Vacuum is used only in vertex presentation. Forceps can be used in face presentation (mentoanterior position only) and for the after-coming head of breech (using Piper's forceps), in addition to vertex presentation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In which situations is vacuum delivery NOT used?
back: Prematurity (risk of intracranial hemorrhage), mentoanterior face presentation, and the after-coming head of breech.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What advantages does vacuum have over forceps?
back: Vacuum can rotate the fetal head, and can be applied at a slightly lower cervical dilatation (>=6cm, versus full dilatation required for forceps).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What advantages does forceps have over vacuum?
back: Preferred in preterm delivery, mentoanterior face presentation, and for delivery of the after-coming head in breech.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In which clinical situations is forceps preferred over vacuum?
back: Maternal heart disease and fetal distress (since forceps typically achieves faster delivery).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the fetal versus maternal complication burden of forceps versus vacuum.
back: Forceps causes more maternal complications; vacuum causes more fetal complications.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: List the fetal complications more specific to forceps versus to vacuum delivery.
back: Forceps: facial nerve palsy, brachial plexus injury, corneal injury, and intraventricular hemorrhage. Vacuum: 6th (abducens) nerve palsy, shoulder dystocia, cephalohematoma, subgaleal hematoma, and retinal injury.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the maximum traction force applied during forceps delivery, in primigravida versus multigravida, and along which diameter is the forceps applied?
back: Maximum traction: 20kg in primigravida, 13kg in multigravida; forceps is applied along the occipitomental diameter.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the absolute contraindications to any instrumental delivery?
back: Cephalopelvic disproportion/contracted pelvis, obstructed labor, transverse lie, brow presentation, and mentoposterior face presentation.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the indications for instrumental delivery?
back: Fetal distress in the second stage of labor, prolonged second stage, and maternal exhaustion; prophylactic use (to shorten the second stage) is also indicated in maternal heart disease and PIH.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the components and normal criteria for a reactive (normal) non-stress test (NST)?
back: FHR 110-160bpm, FHR variability 5-25bpm, and accelerations - an increase in FHR by >=15bpm lasting >=15 seconds associated with fetal movement (at <32 weeks, a smaller increase of >=10bpm for >=10 seconds is adequate, reflecting increased capillary permeability); a reactive NST requires >=2 such accelerations within a 20-minute period.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is done if an NST shows fewer than 2 accelerations in the initial 20-minute period?
back: Continue monitoring for an additional 20 minutes (total 40 minutes); if still fewer than 2 accelerations (non-reactive NST), proceed to the biophysical profile (BPP), which is diagnostic.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare the false-negative and false-positive rates of NST.
back: False-negative rate (hypoxic fetus with a normal/reactive NST) is low. False-positive rate (normal fetus with a non-reactive NST) is relatively high.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the indications for antepartum fetal monitoring with NST?
back: Maternal complaint of decreased fetal movement, all post-term pregnancies, and all high-risk pregnancies (beginning at 32 weeks).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the five components of the biophysical profile (BPP/Manning score), with their mnemonic and 2-point scoring criteria?
back: Mnemonic 'TB Meningitis Always Notorious': fetal Tone (2 points if >=1 episode of limb/spine extension with return to flexion), fetal Breathing movements (2 points if >=1 episode of rhythmic breathing for >=30 seconds), gross body Movement (2 points if >=3 discrete body/limb movements), Amniotic fluid volume (2 points if the single deepest vertical pocket is >=2cm), and NST (2 points if reactive) - assessed via USG over 30 minutes.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the modified biophysical profile, and its two components?
back: A screening test combining just the NST and the amniotic fluid index (AFI).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Interpret BPP scores and their management implications.
back: 10 or 8 (with normal AFV): normal fetus with normal pH; repeat BPP weekly. 6 with normal AFV: equivocal; deliver if >=37 weeks, repeat in 24 hours if <37 weeks. 8 or 6 with DECREASED AFV: suspect chronic asphyxia; deliver at >=36 weeks. 4: deliver at >=32 weeks. 2 or 0: immediate delivery (fetal acidemia essentially certain).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: For a pregnant woman complaining of decreased fetal movements, what are the preferred screening versus diagnostic tests?
back: Screening test: modified BPS (preferred over plain NST). Diagnostic test: full biophysical profile (BPP).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the Cardiff 'count to 10' method for assessing fetal movements, and when are fetal movements maximal?
back: At rest, 10 fetal movements should be felt within 2 hours; during activity, 10 movements within 12 hours. Fetal movements are maximal around 34 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What do the upper and lower tracings of a CTG represent?
back: Upper tracing: fetal heart rate (FHR). Lower tracing: uterine contractions.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What defines fetal bradycardia on CTG, and its prognostic significance if persistent?
back: FHR <110bpm; persistent fetal bradycardia carries a poor prognosis.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define a deceleration on CTG, and prolonged deceleration.
back: A decrease in FHR by 15bpm lasting 15 seconds (up to a maximum of 2 minutes) is a standard deceleration. Prolonged deceleration lasts 2-10 minutes, commonly caused by uterine hyperstimulation (from oxytocin or misoprostol); a deceleration lasting >=10 minutes is instead considered a change in the baseline FHR.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare early, late, and variable decelerations by shape, onset, timing relative to contractions, and underlying cause.
back: Early deceleration: gradual ('U'-shaped) onset, dip coincides exactly with the uterine contraction (both begin and end together), physiological, caused by fetal head compression. Late deceleration: gradual onset, but the dip begins and ends AFTER the uterine contraction; the most ominous deceleration type; caused by uteroplacental insufficiency. Variable deceleration: abrupt ('V'-shaped) onset, no fixed relationship to uterine contractions; caused by umbilical cord compression.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How is a 'severe' variable deceleration defined?
back: A deceleration reaching a nadir more than 60bpm below the baseline FHR and lasting longer than 60 seconds.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the characteristics of a normal (Category 1) CTG?
back: FHR 110-160bpm, variability 5-25bpm, accelerations present or absent, early decelerations present or absent, but late and variable decelerations must be ABSENT.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What defines a Category 3 CTG, and its causes?
back: Either a sinusoidal heart rate pattern (a constant, sine-wave-like FHR with absent variability, seen in fetal anemia, Rh incompatibility, TTTS, and vasa previa), OR absent variability combined with persistent bradycardia, persistent late deceleration, or persistent variable deceleration (persistent variable deceleration is the only indication for amnioinfusion).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the management of Category 3 CTG, and the steps of in-utero resuscitation?
back: Management: in-utero resuscitation plus immediate cesarean section. In-utero resuscitation steps: left lateral positioning, stop oxytocin, start oxygen by mask and IV fluids, and give tocolytics if FHR does not normalize.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Rank the CTG findings by worst prognosis.
back: Sinusoidal heart wave pattern (worst) > late deceleration > variable deceleration.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What are the five technical criteria for correctly measuring nuchal translucency?
back: Measured in the mid-sagittal plane, between 11-13 weeks 6 days (or when CRL is <84mm), with the fetal head in a neutral position, measured from the inner border to the inner border of the nuchal skin edge, and with the amnion visible as a separate structure from the fetal skin.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Besides aneuploidy, what other conditions cause increased nuchal translucency?
back: Congenital heart disease, congenital diaphragmatic hernia, and (in twin pregnancies) it serves as an early marker for twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the characteristic biochemical marker pattern in Down syndrome (trisomy 21) on first and second trimester screening?
back: hCG is INCREASED, while essentially all other markers (PAPP-A, AFP, unconjugated estriol) are DECREASED - a useful pattern to remember, since Down syndrome is the one aneuploidy where hCG rises rather than falls.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is cystic hygroma, how does it differ from simple increased nuchal translucency, and what is its significance as an aneuploidy marker?
back: A cystic malformation showing more extensive fluid collection, more widespread distribution, and often visible septations (compared to simple nuchal translucency); cystic hygroma is actually a BETTER marker of aneuploidy than nuchal translucency alone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the 'combined test' for first-trimester aneuploidy screening, and why is it preferred?
back: The combination of the dual test (PAPP-A and hCG) with nuchal translucency measurement; it has better sensitivity than either component alone.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: In second-trimester USG soft-marker screening for aneuploidy, how many markers must be present to call the screen positive?
back: Any 2 of the soft markers being present is considered screening-positive, warranting further workup (karyotyping) to rule out Down syndrome.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define the puerperium.
back: The period from delivery until 6 weeks after delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: How long do blood volume, cardiac output, GFR, and hCG take to return to normal postpartum?
back: Blood volume: 1 week. Cardiac output: 10 days. GFR: 2 weeks. hCG: 2-4 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What causes 'after-pains,' and in whom are they most seen?
back: Pain in the abdomen during breastfeeding, caused by oxytocin-induced uterine contractions; most seen in multiparous women; decreases by day 3 postpartum.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the recommended postnatal visit schedule, per ACOG versus Government of India guidelines?
back: ACOG: initial visit at 3 weeks, final visit at 12 weeks. GOI: 1st visit on day 1, 2nd visit on day 3, 3rd visit on day 7, 4th visit at 6 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe the timeline of uterine involution after delivery.
back: Immediately after delivery: just below the umbilicus (equivalent to 20 weeks size); no change for the first 24 hours; day 2 onward: 1 fingerbreadth below the umbilicus, decreasing by 1 fingerbreadth daily; at 1 week: midway between the umbilicus and pubic symphysis; at 2 weeks (10-12 days): the uterus becomes a pelvic organ (no longer palpable abdominally); at 4 weeks: involution is complete.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the weight of the uterus immediately postpartum, and how does it decrease over the following weeks?
back: Immediately postpartum: 1000g; after 1 week: 500g; after 2 weeks: 300g; after 4 weeks: 80-100g.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: When does ovulation and menstruation return in non-breastfeeding/partially breastfeeding women?
back: Ovulation returns as early as 28 days, generally by 7 weeks (range 5-11 weeks); menstruation returns by 6-8 weeks.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is the 'rule of 3' for starting contraception postpartum, and the contraceptive of choice for breastfeeding women?
back: Partially breastfeeding/non-breastfeeding women should start contraception by 3 weeks; completely breastfeeding women can wait until 3 months. Contraceptive of choice in breastfeeding women is the progestin-only pill (POP), preferred over IUCD; estrogen-containing methods are avoided since estrogen decreases milk secretion.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: What is lochia, its components, average duration, and the three sequential stages?
back: The vaginal discharge after delivery, composed of decidua, blood, leukocytes, and exudate; average duration 24-36 days; sequence: lochia rubra (red, initially), lochia serosa (pink/brown), then lochia alba (yellowish-white, final stage).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define puerperal pyrexia, and describe postpartum endometritis - most common site, risk factors, and presentation.
back: Puerperal pyrexia is a temperature of 38.0C (100.4F) or higher postpartum. Postpartum endometritis: most common site is the placental implantation site; risk factors include cesarean section, history of chorioamnionitis, and prolonged rupture of membranes; presents with fever, subinvolution of the uterus, uterine tenderness, and possibly foul-smelling lochia; managed with broad-spectrum antibiotics.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Describe septic pelvic thrombophlebitis - when to suspect it, most commonly involved vein, and management.
back: Suspected when a patient with endometritis has persistent fever despite broad-spectrum antibiotics; a clinical diagnosis (blood cultures are typically negative; CT/MRI may show a thrombosed vein; a cord-like structure may be felt on P/V exam); most commonly involves the right ovarian vein; managed by adding heparin to the broad-spectrum antibiotics.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Compare postpartum blues, postpartum depression, and postpartum psychosis by onset, duration, and management.
back: Postpartum blues: due to sudden withdrawal of estrogen and progesterone; mild symptoms (insomnia, fatigue, anxiety); develops 2-4 days postpartum, lasts <2 weeks (usually <10 days); no treatment needed. Postpartum depression: seen from 2 weeks to 12 months postpartum; features instability, phobia, and anxiety; managed with antidepressants. Postpartum psychosis: seen in <0.1% of cases; develops 2-3 days postpartum; features attention deficit, confusion, and altered sensorium; managed with antidepressants and antipsychotics.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
front: Define subinvolution, its causes, presentation, and management.
back: Failure of the uterus to return to its normal size/involute appropriately; caused by retained placental fragments or infection; presents with excessive bleeding and prolonged lochia, with the uterus larger than expected on exam; IOC is pelvic USG; managed with uterotonics followed by gentle curettage (which carries a risk of causing Asherman syndrome).
